description_plain: Your child understands how volume relates to multiplication and addition and can solve problems about the volume of rectangular boxes.

subject: Math
description: Measure volumes by counting unit cubes, using cubic cm, cubic in., cubic ft., and non-standard units.
description_plain: Your child can measure volume by counting cubes and using units like cubic centimeters, inches, or feet.

subject: Math
description: A solid figure which can be packed without gaps or overlaps using n unit cubes is said to have a volume of n cubic units.
description_plain: Your child understands that a solid filled exactly with a certain number of unit cubes has a volume equal to that number of cubes.

subject: Math
description: A cube with side length 1 unit, called a "unit cube," is said to have "one cubic unit" of volume, and can be used to measure volume.
description_plain: Your child knows that a cube with sides of one unit has a volume of one cubic unit and can use it to measure volume.

subject: Math
description: Recognize volume as an attribute of solid figures and understand concepts of volume measurement.
description_plain: Your child understands that volume is a property of solid shapes and learns how to measure it.

subject: Math
description: Geometric measurement: Understand concepts of volume and relate volume to multiplication and to addition.
description_plain: Your child learns what volume means and how it connects to multiplication and addition.

subject: Math
description: Make a line plot (dot plot) to display a data set of measurements in fractions of a unit. Use operations on fractions for this grade to solve problems involving information presented in line plot (dot plot).
description_plain: Your child can make a line plot to show measurements with fractions and use fraction math to solve problems from the plot.

subject: Math
description: Represent and interpret data.
description_plain: Represent and interpret data.

subject: Math
description: Convert among different-sized standard measurement units within a given measurement system (e.g., convert 5 cm to 0.05 m), and use these conversions in solving multi-step, real-world problems.
description_plain: Your child can change measurements from one unit to another within the same system (like centimeters to meters) and use these changes to solve multi-step real-world problems.

subject: Math
description: Convert like measurement units within a given measurement system.
description_plain: Your child can convert between units that measure the same thing within one measurement system.

subject: Math
description: Add, subtract, multiply, and divide decimals to hundredths, using concrete models or drawings and strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction and between multiplication and division; relate the strategy to a written method and explain the reasoning used.
description_plain: Your child can add, subtract, multiply, and divide decimals to two decimal places using drawings or place value strategies, and can check if their answers make sense by estimating.

subject: Math
description: Find whole-number quotients of whole numbers with up to four-digit dividends and two-digit divisors, using strategies based on place value, the properties of operations, and/or the relationship between multiplication and division. Illustrate and explain the calculation by using equations, rectangular arrays, and/or area models.
description_plain: Your child can divide large whole numbers (up to four digits) by two-digit numbers using place value and other strategies, and can explain their work with equations or pictures.

subject: Math
description: Fluently multiply multi-digit whole numbers. (Include two-digit x four-digit numbers and, three-digit x three-digit numbers) using the standard algorithm.
description_plain: Your child can quickly multiply large numbers, including two-digit by four-digit and three-digit by three-digit numbers, using the standard method.

subject: Math
description: Perform operations with multi-digit whole numbers and with decimals to hundredths.
description_plain: Your child can work with large whole numbers and decimals to the hundredths place using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding to round decimals to any place.
description_plain: Your child can round decimals to any place value.

subject: Math
description: Compare two decimals to thousandths based on meanings of the digits in each place, using >, =, and < symbols to record the results of comparisons.
description_plain: Your child can compare two decimals to the thousandths place using >, =, and < symbols.

subject: Math
description: Read and write decimals to thousandths using base-ten numerals, number names, and expanded form, e.g., 347.392 = 3 × 100 + 4 × 10 + 7 × 1 + 3 × (1∕10) + 9 × (1∕100) + 2 × (1∕1000).
description_plain: Your child can read and write decimals to the thousandths place using numbers, words, and expanded form to show the value of each digit.

subject: Math
description: Read, write, and compare decimals to thousandths.
description_plain: Your child can read, write, and compare decimals up to the thousandths place.

subject: Math
description: Explain patterns in the number of zeros of the product when multiplying a number by powers of 10, and explain patterns in the placement of the decimal point when a decimal is multiplied or divided by a power of 10. Use whole-number exponents to denote powers of 10.
description_plain: Your child can explain patterns when multiplying or dividing by powers of 10, like how zeros or decimal points move, and use exponents to show powers of 10.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that in a multi-digit number, including decimals, a digit in any place represents 10 times as much as it represents in the place to its right and 1∕10 of what it represents in the place to its left.
description_plain: Your child knows that in numbers, each digit is worth ten times more than the digit to its right and one-tenth of the digit to its left.

subject: Math
description: Understand the place value system.
description_plain: Your child understands how place value works in numbers.

subject: Math
description: Generate two numerical patterns using two given rules. Identify apparent relationships between corresponding terms. Form ordered pairs consisting of corresponding terms from the two patterns, and graph the ordered pairs on a coordinate plane.
description_plain: Your child can create two number patterns from two rules, find relationships between the numbers in the patterns, make pairs from matching numbers, and graph these pairs on a coordinate plane.

subject: Math
description: Analyze patterns and relationships.
description_plain: Your child can find and understand patterns and relationships in numbers.

subject: Math
description: Write simple expressions that record calculations with numbers, and interpret numerical expressions without evaluating them.
description_plain: Your child can write simple math expressions to show calculations and understand what these expressions mean without solving them.

subject: Math
description: Use parentheses, brackets, or braces in numerical expressions, and evaluate expressions with these symbols, e.g.,(6 x 30) + (6 x ½).
description_plain: Your child can use parentheses, brackets, or braces in math expressions and solve problems that use these symbols.

subject: Math
description: Write and interpret numerical expressions.
description_plain: Your child can write and understand math expressions.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Relating the choice of measures of center and variability to the shape of the data distribution and the context in which the data were gathered.
description_plain: Your child can choose the right way to describe data based on how the data looks and where it came from.

subject: Math
description: Giving quantitative measures of center (median, and/or mean) and variability (range and/or interquartile range), as well as describing any overall pattern and any striking deviations from the overall pattern with reference to the context in which the data were gathered.
description_plain: Your child can find and explain the middle and spread of data, describe patterns, and point out unusual data based on the situation.

subject: Math
description: Describing the nature of the attribute under investigation, including how it was measured and its units of measurement.
description_plain: Your child can describe what is being measured in data, how it was measured, and the units used.

subject: Math
description: Reporting the number of observations.
description_plain: Your child can tell how many observations or data points were collected.

subject: Math
description: Summarize numerical data sets in relation to their context, such as by:
description_plain: Your child can summarize sets of numbers by explaining what they mean in real life.

subject: Math
description: Read and interpret circle graphs.
description_plain: Your child can read and understand circle graphs (pie charts).

subject: Math
description: Display numerical data in plots on a number line, including dot plots, histograms, and box plots.
description_plain: Your child can show number data on number lines using dot plots, histograms, and box plots.

subject: Math
description: Summarize and describe distributions.
description_plain: Your child can summarize and describe how data is spread out and what patterns it shows.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that a measure of center for a numerical data set summarizes all of its values with a single number, while a measure of variation describes how its values vary with a single number.
description_plain: Your child understands that one number can show the middle of data, and another number can show how much the data varies.

subject: Math
description: Understand that a set of data collected to answer a statistical question has a distribution, which can be described by its center (median, mean, and/or mode), spread (range, interquartile range), and overall shape.
description_plain: Your child understands that data collected to answer questions has a pattern that can be described by its center, spread, and overall shape.

subject: Math
description: Recognize a statistical question as one that anticipates variability in the data related to the question and accounts for it in the answers.
description_plain: Your child can recognize questions that expect different answers because the data changes each time.

subject: Math
description: Develop understanding of statistical variability.
description_plain: Your child understands that data can vary and learns how to describe that variability.

subject: Math
description: Use variables to represent two quantities in a real-world problem that change in relationship to one another; write an equation to express one quantity, thought of as the dependent variable, in terms of the other quantity, thought of as the independent variable. Analyze the relationship between the dependent and independent variables using graphs and tables, and relate these to the equation.
description_plain: Your child can use letters to stand for two amounts in a real-life problem that change depending on each other.

subject: Math
description: Represent and analyze quantitative relationships between dependent and independent variables.
description_plain: Your child can show and study how one quantity depends on another using math.

subject: Math
description: Write an inequality of the form x > c or x < c to represent a constraint or condition in a real-world or mathematical problem. Recognize that inequalities of the form x > c or x < c have infinitely many solutions; represent solutions of such inequalities on number line diagrams.
description_plain: Your child can write inequalities to describe limits in real life or math problems, know these have many answers, and show those answers on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems by writing and solving equations of the form x + p = q and px = q for cases in which p, q, and x are all nonnegative rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life and math problems by writing and solving simple equations like x plus a number equals another number.

subject: Math
description: Use variables to represent numbers and write expressions when solving a real-world or mathematical problem; understand that a variable can represent an unknown number, or, depending on the purpose at hand, any number in a specified set.
description_plain: Your child can use letters to stand for numbers and write expressions to solve real-life or math problems, knowing that letters can mean unknown or any number in a group.

subject: Math
description: Understand solving an equation or inequality as a process of answering a question: Which values from a specified set, if any, make the equation or inequality true? Use substitution to determine whether a given number in a specified set makes an equation or inequality true.
description_plain: Your child understands solving equations or inequalities means finding which numbers make them true and can check by plugging in numbers.

subject: Math
description: Reason about and solve one-variable equations and inequalities.
description_plain: Your child can think through and solve equations and inequalities with one variable.

subject: Math
description: Identify when two expressions are equivalent (i.e., when the two expressions name the same number regardless of which value is substituted into them).
description_plain: Your child can tell when two expressions are equal because they give the same result no matter what number is used.

subject: Math
description: Apply the properties of operations to generate equivalent expressions.
description_plain: Your child can use math rules to create different expressions that are equal.

subject: Math
description: Evaluate expressions at specific values of their variables. Include expressions that arise from formulas used in real-world problems. Perform arithmetic operations, including those involving whole-number exponents, in the conventional order when there are no parentheses to specify a particular order (Order of Operations).
description_plain: Your child can find the value of expressions by plugging in numbers for letters, including using formulas from real-life problems, and follows the correct order of operations.

subject: Math
description: Identify parts of an expression using mathematical terms (sum, term, product, factor, quotient, and coefficient); view one or more parts of an expression as a single entity.
description_plain: Your child can name parts of an expression like sum, product, factor, and coefficient, and see parts as a whole when needed.

subject: Math
description: Write expressions that record operations with numbers and with letters standing for numbers.
description_plain: Your child can write expressions that show math operations using numbers and letters that stand for numbers.

subject: Math
description: Write, read, and evaluate expressions in which letters stand for numbers.
description_plain: Your child can write, read, and find the value of expressions where letters stand for numbers.

subject: Math
description: Write and evaluate numerical expressions involving whole-number exponents.
description_plain: Your child can write and solve number expressions that include whole-number exponents.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of arithmetic to algebraic expressions.
description_plain: Your child can use what they know about arithmetic to work with algebra expressions.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems by graphing points in all four quadrants of the coordinate plane. Include use of coordinates and absolute value to find distances between points with the same first coordinate or the same second coordinate.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems by plotting points in all four parts of the coordinate plane and use coordinates and absolute value to find distances between points.

subject: Math
description: Distinguish comparisons of absolute value from statements about order.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between comparing absolute values and comparing the order of numbers.

subject: Math
description: Understand the absolute value of a rational number as its distance from 0 on the number line; interpret absolute value as magnitude for a positive or negative quantity in a real-world situation.
description_plain: Your child understands absolute value as the distance of a number from zero and can explain it in real situations.

subject: Math
description: Write, interpret, and explain statements of order for rational numbers in real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child can write and explain statements about the order of rational numbers in real-life situations.

subject: Math
description: Interpret statements of inequality as statements about the relative positions of two numbers on a number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child can understand inequality signs by showing which number is bigger or smaller on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Understand ordering and absolute value of rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child understands how to order rational numbers and what absolute value means.

subject: Math
description: Find and position integers and other rational numbers on a horizontal or vertical number line diagram; find and position pairs of integers and other rational numbers on a coordinate plane.
description_plain: Your child can find and place integers and other rational numbers on number lines and coordinate planes.

subject: Math
description: Understand signs of numbers in ordered pairs as indicating locations in quadrants of the coordinate plane; recognize that when two ordered pairs differ only by signs, the locations of the points are related by reflections across one or both axes.
description_plain: Your child understands that the signs in ordered pairs tell where points are on the coordinate plane and that changing signs reflects points across axes.

subject: Math
description: Recognize opposite signs of numbers as indicating locations on opposite sides of 0 on the number line; recognize that the opposite of the opposite of a number is the number itself, e.g., –(–3) = 3, and that zero is its own opposite.
description_plain: Your child knows that opposite signs mean numbers are on opposite sides of zero and that the opposite of the opposite number is the original number.

subject: Math
description: Understand a rational number as a point on the number line. Extend number line diagrams and coordinate axes familiar from previous grades to represent points on the line and in the plane with negative number coordinates.
description_plain: Your child understands that rational numbers can be shown as points on a number line, including negative numbers.

subject: Math
description: Understand that positive and negative numbers are used together to describe quantities having opposite directions or values (e.g., temperature above/below zero, elevation above/below sea level, credits/debits, and positive/negative electric charge). Use positive and negative numbers (whole numbers, fractions, and decimals) to represent quantities in real-world contexts, explaining the meaning of zero in each situation.
description_plain: Your child understands that positive and negative numbers can show things like direction or value, and can explain what zero means in different situations.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of numbers to the system of rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can use what they know about numbers to understand and work with positive and negative numbers, including fractions and decimals.

subject: Math
description: Use prime factorization to find the greatest common factor of two whole numbers less than or equal to 100 and the least common multiple of two whole numbers less than or equal to 12. Use the distributive property to express a sum of two whole numbers 1–100 with a common factor as a multiple of a sum of two relatively prime numbers.
description_plain: Your child can use prime factors to find the greatest common factor of two numbers up to 100 and the least common multiple of two numbers up to 12, and use the distributive property to rewrite sums with common factors.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add, subtract, multiply, and divide multi-digit decimals using the standard algorithm for each operation.
description_plain: Your child can quickly and accurately add, subtract, multiply, and divide decimals with many digits using standard methods.

subject: Math
description: Fluently divide multi-digit numbers using the standard algorithm.
description_plain: Your child can quickly and accurately divide large numbers using the standard method.

subject: Math
description: Compute fluently with multi-digit numbers and find common factors and multiples.
description_plain: Your child can work easily with large numbers and find common factors and multiples.

subject: Math
description: Interpret and compute quotients of fractions, and solve word problems involving division of fractions by fractions, e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can understand and calculate division of fractions and solve word problems about dividing fractions by fractions using pictures or equations.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication and division to divide fractions by fractions.
description_plain: Your child can use what they know about multiplication and division to solve problems with fractions dividing by fractions.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems that relate the mass of an object to its volume.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems that connect how heavy an object is to how much space it takes up.

subject: Math
description: Use ratio reasoning to convert measurement units within and between measurement systems; manipulate and transform units appropriately when multiplying or dividing quantities.
description_plain: Your child can use ratios to change measurements between different units correctly.

subject: Math
description: Find a percent of a quantity as a rate per 100 (e.g., 30% of a quantity means 30∕100 times the quantity); solve problems involving finding the whole, given a part and the percent.
description_plain: Your child can find a percent of a number and solve problems where they find the whole number when given a part and the percent.

subject: Math
description: Solve unit rate problems, including those involving unit pricing, and constant speed.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems about rates, like finding the best price per item or how fast something is moving.

subject: Math
description: Make tables of equivalent ratios relating quantities with whole-number measurements. Find missing values in the tables, and plot the pairs of values on the coordinate plane. Use tables to compare ratios.
description_plain: Your child can make tables showing equal ratios with whole numbers, find missing numbers, plot these on a graph, and use the tables to compare ratios.

subject: Math
description: Use ratio and rate reasoning to solve real-world and mathematical problems, e.g., by reasoning about tables of equivalent ratios, tape diagrams, double number line diagrams, or equations.
description_plain: Your child can use ratios and rates to solve real-life and math problems by working with tables, diagrams, or equations that show equivalent ratios.

subject: Math
description: Understand the concept of a unit rate a/b associated with a ratio a:b with b ≠ 0, and use rate language in the context of a ratio relationship, including the use of units.
description_plain: Your child understands what a unit rate is and can talk about rates in ratio problems.

subject: Math
description: Understand the concept of a ratio including the distinctions between part:part and part:whole and the value of a ratio; part/part and part/whole. Use ratio language to describe a ratio relationship between two quantities.
description_plain: Your child understands what a ratio is, can tell the difference between comparing parts to parts and parts to whole, and can use words to explain these relationships.

subject: Math
description: Understand ratio and rate concepts and use ratio and rate reasoning to solve problems.
description_plain: Your child understands ratios and rates and can use them to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Represent three-dimensional figures using nets made up of rectangles and triangles, and use the nets to find the surface areas of these figures. Apply these techniques in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can draw flat patterns (nets) made of rectangles and triangles to build 3D shapes and use these to find surface area in real problems.

subject: Math
description: Draw polygons in the coordinate plane given coordinates for the vertices; use coordinates to find the length of a side joining points with the same first coordinate or the same second coordinate. Apply these techniques in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can draw shapes on a grid using points and find side lengths when sides are straight up/down or left/right, solving real problems.

subject: Math
description: Find the volume of a right rectangular prism with fractional edge lengths by packing it with unit cubes of the appropriate unit fraction edge lengths, and show that the volume is the same as would be found by multiplying the edge lengths of the prism. Apply the formulas V = lwh and V = Bh to find volumes of right rectangular prisms with fractional edge lengths in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can find the volume of boxes with fractional side lengths by filling them with small cubes and by multiplying the edges, using formulas in real-life problems.

subject: Math
description: Find the area of right triangles, other triangles, special quadrilaterals, and polygons by composing into rectangles or decomposing into triangles and other shapes; apply these techniques in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of triangles, special four-sided shapes, and other polygons by breaking them into rectangles or triangles, and use this in real problems.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving area, surface area, and volume.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life and math problems involving area, surface area, and volume.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Design and use a simulation to generate frequencies for compound events.
description_plain: Your child can create and use simulations to find how often combined events happen.

subject: Math
description: Represent sample spaces for compound events using methods such as organized lists, tables, and tree diagrams. For an event described in everyday language (e.g., "rolling double sixes"), identify the outcomes in the sample space which compose the event.
description_plain: Your child can list all possible outcomes for combined events using organized lists, tables, or tree diagrams, and pick out the outcomes that match a described event.

subject: Math
description: Understand that, just as with simple events, the probability of a compound event is the fraction of outcomes in the sample space for which the compound event occurs.
description_plain: Your child understands that the chance of combined events happening is the part of all possible outcomes where those events happen.

subject: Math
description: Find probabilities of compound events using organized lists, tables, tree diagrams, and simulation.
description_plain: Your child can find the chances of combined events happening by using lists, tables, tree diagrams, or simulations.

subject: Math
description: Develop a probability model (which may not be uniform) by observing frequencies in data generated from a chance process.
description_plain: Your child creates a probability model based on how often things happen in real-life chance events.

subject: Math
description: Develop a uniform probability model by assigning equal probability to all outcomes, and use the model to determine probabilities of events.
description_plain: Your child makes a simple probability model where all outcomes are equally likely and uses it to find chances of events.

subject: Math
description: Develop a probability model and use it to find probabilities of events. Compare probabilities from a model to observed frequencies; if the agreement is not good, explain possible sources of the discrepancy.
description_plain: Your child builds probability models to find chances of events and compares these to actual results, explaining differences.

subject: Math
description: Approximate the probability of a chance event by collecting data on the chance process that produces it and observing its long-run relative frequency, and predict the approximate relative frequency given the probability.
description_plain: Your child estimates the chance of an event by collecting data and watching how often it happens over time.

subject: Math
description: Understand that the probability of a chance event is a number between 0 and 1 that expresses the likelihood of the event occurring. Larger numbers indicate greater likelihood. A probability near 0 indicates an unlikely event, a probability around ½ indicates an event that is neither unlikely nor likely, and a probability near 1 indicates a likely event.
description_plain: Your child understands that probability is a number from 0 to 1 showing how likely something is to happen, with bigger numbers meaning more likely.

subject: Math
description: Investigate chance processes and develop, use, and evaluate probability models.
description_plain: Your child explores chance events and learns how to create, use, and check probability models.

subject: Math
description: Use measures of center and measures of variability for numerical data from random samples to draw informal comparative inferences about two populations.
description_plain: Your child uses averages and variation from random samples to compare two groups and make guesses about them.

subject: Math
description: Informally assess the degree of visual overlap of two numerical data distributions with similar variabilities, measuring the difference between the centers by expressing it as a multiple of a measure of variability.
description_plain: Your child looks at two sets of data to see how much they overlap and compares their centers using measures of spread.

subject: Math
description: Draw informal comparative inferences about two populations.
description_plain: Your child makes informal comparisons between two groups using data.

subject: Math
description: Use data from a random sample to draw inferences about a population with an unknown characteristic of interest. Generate multiple samples (or simulated samples) of the same size to gauge the variation in estimates or predictions.
description_plain: Your child uses data from random samples to make guesses about a larger group and tries multiple samples to see how results can change.

subject: Math
description: Understand that statistics can be used to gain information about a population by examining a sample of the population; Generalizations about a population from a sample are valid only if the sample is representative of that population. Understand that random sampling tends to produce representative samples and support valid inferences.
description_plain: Your child understands that looking at a small, random part of a group can give good information about the whole group if the sample is fair.

subject: Math
description: Use random sampling to draw inferences about a population.
description_plain: Your child uses random samples to learn about larger groups.

subject: Math
description: Extend analysis of patterns to include analyzing, extending, and determining an expression for simple arithmetic and geometric sequences (e.g., compounding, increasing area), using tables, graphs, words, and expressions.
description_plain: Your child can study patterns that grow by adding or multiplying, and describe them using tables, graphs, words, and math expressions.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems leading to inequalities of the form px + q > r or px + q < r, where p, q, and r are specific rational numbers. Graph the solution set of the inequality and interpret it in the context of the problem.
description_plain: Your child solves word problems that lead to inequalities, graphs the solutions, and explains what the answers mean.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems leading to equations of the form px + q = r and p(x ÷ q) = r, where p, q, and r are specific rational numbers. Solve equations of these forms fluently. Compare an algebraic solution to an arithmetic solution, identifying the sequence of the operations used in each approach.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that lead to simple equations and can compare solving them with algebra or arithmetic methods.

subject: Math
description: Use variables to represent quantities in a real-world or mathematical problem, and construct simple equations and inequalities to solve problems by reasoning about the quantities.
description_plain: Your child can use letters to stand for numbers in real problems and write simple equations or inequalities to solve them.

subject: Math
description: Solve multi-step real-life and mathematical problems posed with positive and negative rational numbers in any form (whole numbers, fractions, and decimals), using tools strategically. Apply properties of operations to calculate with numbers in any form; convert between forms as appropriate; and assess the reasonableness of answers using mental computation and estimation strategies.
description_plain: Your child can solve multi-step problems with positive and negative numbers in different forms, use math tools wisely, and check if answers make sense.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-life and mathematical problems using numerical and algebraic expressions and equations.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life and math problems using number and algebra expressions and equations.

subject: Math
description: Understand that rewriting an expression in different forms in a problem context can shed light on the problem and how the quantities in it are related.
description_plain: Your child understands that changing how an expression looks can help explain a problem and how the numbers relate.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations to add, subtract, factor, and expand linear expressions with rational coefficients.
description_plain: Your child can add, subtract, factor, and expand expressions with rational numbers using math rules.

subject: Math
description: Use properties of operations to generate equivalent expressions.
description_plain: Your child uses math properties to create expressions that mean the same thing.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving the four operations with integers and other rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life and math problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division with integers and rational numbers.

subject: Math
description: Convert a rational number to a decimal using long division; know that the decimal form of a rational number terminates in 0s or eventually repeats.
description_plain: Your child can change a fraction or rational number into a decimal by dividing and knows decimals either end or repeat.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to multiply and divide rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child uses math rules to multiply and divide rational numbers.

subject: Math
description: Understand that integers can be divided, provided that the divisor is not zero, and every quotient of integers (with non-zero divisor) is a rational number. If p and q are integers, then –(p∕q) = (–p)∕q = p∕(–q). Interpret quotients of rational numbers by describing real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child understands dividing integers works when the divisor isn't zero and explains division of rational numbers using real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Understand that multiplication is extended from fractions to rational numbers by requiring that operations continue to satisfy the properties of operations, particularly the distributive property, leading to products such as (–1)(–1) = 1 and the rules for multiplying signed numbers. Interpret products of rational numbers by describing real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child understands multiplying rational numbers follows math rules, including multiplying negatives, and explains these products with real-world examples.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication and division and of fractions to multiply and divide integers and other rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can multiply and divide positive and negative numbers, including fractions, and use these skills to solve real-world problems.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to add and subtract rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can use rules about adding and subtracting numbers to help solve problems with fractions and decimals.

subject: Math
description: Understand subtraction of rational numbers as adding the additive inverse, p – q = p + (–q). Show that the distance between two rational numbers on the number line is the absolute value of their difference, and apply this principle in real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child understands that subtracting a number is the same as adding its opposite and can find the distance between two numbers on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Understand p + q as the number located a distance |q| from p, in the positive or negative direction depending on whether q is positive or negative. Show that a number and its opposite have a sum of 0 (are additive inverses). Interpret sums of rational numbers by describing real- world contexts.
description_plain: Your child understands adding positive or negative numbers as moving along a number line and knows that a number plus its opposite equals zero.

subject: Math
description: Describe situations in which opposite quantities combine to make zero.
description_plain: Your child can describe situations where opposite numbers add up to zero.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of addition and subtraction to add and subtract integers and other rational numbers; represent addition and subtraction on a horizontal or vertical number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract integers and rational numbers and show these on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of operations with fractions to add, subtract, multiply, and divide rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can add, subtract, multiply, and divide rational numbers using what they know about fractions.

subject: Math
description: Use proportional relationships to solve multi-step ratio, rate, and percent problems.
description_plain: Your child can use proportional relationships to solve multi-step problems with ratios, rates, and percents.

subject: Math
description: Explain what a point (x, y) on the graph of a proportional relationship means in terms of the situation, with special attention to the points (0, 0) and (1, r) where r is the unit rate.
description_plain: Your child can explain what points on a graph mean in a proportional relationship, especially the starting point and the unit rate.

subject: Math
description: Represent proportional relationships by equations.
description_plain: Your child can write equations that show proportional relationships between quantities.

subject: Math
description: Identify the constant of proportionality (unit rate) in tables, graphs, equations, diagrams, and verbal descriptions of proportional relationships.
description_plain: Your child can find the unit rate (constant ratio) in tables, graphs, equations, pictures, and words.

subject: Math
description: Decide whether two quantities are in a proportional relationship, e.g., by testing for equivalent ratios in a table, or graphing on a coordinate plane and observing whether the graph is a straight line through the origin.
description_plain: Your child can decide if two quantities are proportional by checking if their ratios are equal or if their graph is a straight line through the origin.

subject: Math
description: Recognize and represent proportional relationships between quantities.
description_plain: Your child can recognize and show proportional relationships between amounts.

subject: Math
description: Compute unit rates associated with ratios of fractions, including ratios of lengths, areas, and other quantities measured in like or different units.
description_plain: Your child can find unit rates from ratios, including those with fractions and different units like length or area.

subject: Math
description: Analyze proportional relationships and use them to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can understand and use ratios to solve problems in everyday life and math.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving area, volume, and surface area of two- and three-dimensional objects composed of triangles, quadrilaterals, polygons, cubes, and right prisms.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life and math problems about the area, volume, and surface area of shapes like triangles, rectangles, cubes, and prisms.

subject: Math
description: Use facts about supplementary, complementary, vertical, and adjacent angles in a multi-step problem to write simple equations and use them to solve for an unknown angle in a figure.
description_plain: Your child can use angle facts to write and solve simple equations to find missing angles.

subject: Math
description: Give an informal derivation of the relationship between the circumference and area of a circle.
description_plain: Your child can explain in simple terms how the distance around a circle relates to the space inside it.

subject: Math
description: Know the formulas for the area and circumference of a circle and use them to solve problems.
description_plain: Your child knows the formulas for a circle’s area and circumference and can use them to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Understand and describe the relationship among the radius, diameter, and area of a circle.
description_plain: Your child understands and can explain how the radius, diameter, and area of a circle are connected.

subject: Math
description: Understand and describe the relationships among the radius, diameter, and circumference of a circle.
description_plain: Your child understands and can explain how the radius, diameter, and circumference of a circle are related.

subject: Math
description: Know that a circle is a two-dimensional shape created by connecting all of the points equidistant from a fixed point called the center of the circle.
description_plain: Your child knows a circle is a flat shape made by connecting points all the same distance from the center.

subject: Math
description: Circles and measurement:
description_plain: Circles and measurement:

subject: Math
description: Solve real-life and mathematical problems involving angle measure, area, surface area, and volume.
description_plain: Your child can solve real and math problems about angles, area, surface area, and volume.

subject: Math
description: Describe the shape of the two-dimensional face of the figure that results from slicing three-dimensional figures, as in plane sections of right rectangular prisms and right rectangular pyramids.
description_plain: Your child can describe the shape you get when you cut 3D shapes like rectangular prisms or pyramids.

subject: Math
description: Draw (freehand, with ruler and protractor, and with technology) two-dimensional geometric shapes with given conditions. Focus on constructing triangles from three measures of angles or sides, noticing when the conditions determine a unique triangle, more than one triangle, or no triangle.
description_plain: Your child can draw 2D shapes by hand, with tools, or technology using given measurements and can tell when those measurements make one, more than one, or no triangle.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems involving scale drawings of geometric figures, such as computing actual lengths and areas from a scale drawing and reproducing a scale drawing at a different scale.
description_plain: Your child solves problems using scale drawings by finding actual lengths and areas or making drawings at different scales.

subject: Math
description: Draw, construct, and describe geometrical figures and describe the relationships between them.
description_plain: Your child can draw and build shapes and explain how they relate to each other.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Describe qualitatively the functional relationship between two quantities by analyzing a graph (e.g., where the function is increasing or decreasing, linear or nonlinear). Sketch a graph that exhibits the qualitative features of a function that has been described verbally.
description_plain: Your child can look at a graph and describe how two things change together, like when a line goes up or down, and draw a graph from a description.

subject: Math
description: Construct a function to model a linear relationship between two quantities. Determine the rate of change and initial value of the function from a description of a relationship or from two (x, y) values, including reading these from a table or from a graph. Interpret the rate of change and initial value of a linear function in terms of the situation it models, and in terms of its graph or a table of values.
description_plain: Your child can create a function to show a straight-line relationship between two things, find the rate of change and starting value, and explain what they mean.

subject: Math
description: Use functions to model relationships between quantities.
description_plain: Your child uses functions to show how different amounts are connected.

subject: Math
description: Interpret the equation y = mx + b as defining a linear function whose graph is a straight line; give examples of functions that are not linear.
description_plain: Your child understands that the equation y = mx + b makes a straight line on a graph and can give examples of rules that don’t make straight lines.

subject: Math
description: Compare properties of two functions each represented in a different way (algebraically, graphically, numerically in tables, or by verbal descriptions).
description_plain: Your child can compare two functions shown in different ways, like with an equation, graph, table, or words.

subject: Math
description: Understand that a function is a rule that assigns to each input exactly one output. The graph of a function is the set of ordered pairs consisting of an input and the corresponding output.
description_plain: Your child understands that a function is a rule that gives exactly one answer for each input, and can find points on its graph that match inputs and outputs.

subject: Math
description: Define, evaluate, and compare functions.
description_plain: Your child can define what functions are, find their values, and compare different functions.

subject: Math
description: Understand that patterns of association can also be seen in bivariate categorical data by displaying frequencies and relative frequencies in a two-way table. Construct and interpret a two-way table summarizing data on two categorical variables collected from the same subjects. Use relative frequencies calculated for rows or columns to describe possible association between the two variables.
description_plain: Your child can make and read two-way tables to see how two categories of data might be related.

subject: Math
description: Use the equation of a linear model to solve problems in the context of bivariate measurement data, interpreting the slope and intercept.
description_plain: Your child can use a line equation to solve problems with two related measurements and explain what the slope and intercept mean.

subject: Math
description: Know that straight lines are widely used to model relationships between two quantitative variables. For scatter plots that suggest a linear association, informally fit a straight line and informally assess the model fit by judging the closeness of the data points to the line.
description_plain: Your child knows that straight lines often show relationships between two numbers and can draw a line that fits data points on a graph.

subject: Math
description: Construct and interpret scatter plots for bivariate measurement data to investigate patterns of association between two quantities. Describe patterns such as clustering, outliers, positive or negative association, linear association, and nonlinear association.
description_plain: Your child can make and read scatter plots to find patterns between two sets of data, like clusters or trends.

subject: Math
description: Investigate patterns of association in bivariate data.
description_plain: Your child can study how two sets of data relate to each other.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems leading to two linear equations in two variables.
description_plain: Your child can solve real and math problems that involve two linear equations with two variables.

subject: Math
description: Solve systems of two linear equations in two variables algebraically (using substitution and elimination strategies), and estimate solutions by graphing the equations. Solve simple cases by inspection.
description_plain: Your child can solve two equations with two variables using methods like substitution and elimination, estimate answers by graphing, and solve simple cases by looking.

subject: Math
description: Understand that solutions to a system of two linear equations in two variables correspond to points of intersection of their graphs, because points of intersection satisfy both equations simultaneously.
description_plain: Your child understands that the solution to two straight-line equations is where their graphs cross, because that point fits both equations.

subject: Math
description: Analyze and solve pairs of simultaneous linear equations.
description_plain: Your child can analyze and solve pairs of straight-line equations at the same time.

subject: Math
description: Solve linear equations with rational number coefficients, including equations whose solutions require expanding expressions using the distributive property and collecting like terms.
description_plain: Your child can solve linear equations with fractions and decimals, including those that need expanding and combining like terms.

subject: Math
description: Give examples of linear equations in one variable with one solution, infinitely many solutions, or no solutions. Show which of these possibilities is the case by successively transforming the given equation into simpler forms, until an equivalent equation of the form x = a, a = a, or a = b results (where a and b are different numbers).
description_plain: Your child can give examples of straight-line equations with one solution, many solutions, or no solutions, and show which by simplifying the equation step-by-step.

subject: Math
description: Solve linear equations in one variable.
description_plain: Your child can solve one-step and multi-step linear equations with one variable.

subject: Math
description: Analyze and solve linear equations and pairs of simultaneous linear equations.
description_plain: Your child understands how to solve single and pairs of linear equations.

subject: Math
description: Use similar triangles to explain why the slope m is the same between any two distinct points on a non- vertical line in the coordinate plane. Derive the equation y = mx for a line through the origin and the equation y = mx + b for a line intercepting the vertical axis at b.
description_plain: Your child can explain why the slope (steepness) is the same between any two points on a line and use the equations y = mx and y = mx + b to describe lines.

subject: Math
description: Graph proportional relationships, interpreting the unit rate as the slope of the graph. Compare two different proportional relationships represented in different ways.
description_plain: Your child can graph proportional relationships and understand the unit rate as the slope, and compare different proportional relationships shown in various ways.

subject: Math
description: Understand the connections between proportional relationships, lines, and linear equations.
description_plain: Your child understands how proportional relationships, lines, and straight-line equations are connected.

subject: Math
description: Perform operations with numbers expressed in scientific notation, including problems where both decimal and scientific notation are used. Use scientific notation and choose units of appropriate size for measurements of very large or very small quantities (e.g., use millimeters per year for seafloor spreading). Interpret scientific notation that has been generated by technology.
description_plain: Your child can do math with numbers in scientific notation, including mixing decimals and scientific notation, choose the right units for very big or small measurements, and understand scientific notation from technology.

subject: Math
description: Use numbers expressed in the form of a single digit multiplied by an integer power of 10 to estimate very large or very small quantities, and express how many times as much one is than the other.
description_plain: Your child can use numbers written as a single digit times a power of 10 to estimate very big or small amounts and compare how many times one number is bigger than another.

subject: Math
description: Use square root and cube root symbols to represent solutions to equations of the form x² = p and x³ = p, where p is a positive rational number. Evaluate square roots of small perfect squares and cube roots of small perfect cubes. Know that is irrational.
description_plain: Your child can use square root and cube root symbols to solve equations like x² = p and x³ = p, and can find the roots of small perfect squares and cubes.

subject: Math
description: Know and apply the properties of integer exponents to generate equivalent numerical expressions.
description_plain: Your child understands how to use rules for exponents to rewrite and simplify expressions with whole number powers.

subject: Math
description: Work with radicals and integer exponents.
description_plain: Your child works with roots (like square roots) and whole number exponents.

subject: Math
description: Use rational approximations of irrational numbers to compare the size of irrational numbers, locate them approximately on a number line diagram, and estimate the value of expressions (e.g., π²).
description_plain: Your child can use close decimal guesses to compare and place irrational numbers on a number line and estimate values like π squared.

subject: Math
description: Know that numbers that are not rational are called irrational. Understand informally that every number has a decimal expansion. For rational numbers show that the decimal expansion repeats eventually, and convert a decimal expansion which repeats eventually into a rational number.
description_plain: Your child knows that some numbers can't be written as simple fractions and understands that decimals either repeat or go on forever.

subject: Math
description: Know that there are numbers that are not rational, and approximate them by rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child knows there are numbers that aren't fractions and can estimate them using fractions.

subject: Math
description: Know the formulas for the volumes of cones, cylinders, and spheres, and use them to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child knows the formulas to find the volume of cones, cylinders, and spheres and can use them to solve real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving volume of cylinders, cones, and spheres.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life and math problems involving the volume of cylinders, cones, and spheres.

subject: Math
description: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem to find the distance between two points in a coordinate system.
description_plain: Your child can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find the distance between two points on a graph.

subject: Math
description: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem to determine unknown side lengths in right triangles in real-world and mathematical problems in two and three dimensions.
description_plain: Your child can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find missing side lengths in right triangles in both 2D and 3D problems.

subject: Math
description: Analyze and justify the Pythagorean Theorem and its converse using pictures, diagrams, narratives, or models.
description_plain: Your child can explain and prove the Pythagorean Theorem and its reverse using pictures, diagrams, stories, or models.

subject: Math
description: Understand the relationship among the sides of a right triangle.
description_plain: Your child understands how the sides of a right triangle relate to each other.

subject: Math
description: Understand and apply the Pythagorean Theorem.
description_plain: Your child can understand and use the Pythagorean Theorem to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Use informal arguments to establish facts about the angle sum and exterior angle of triangles, about the angles created when parallel lines are cut by a transversal, and the angle-angle criterion for similarity of triangles.
description_plain: Your child can explain facts about angles in triangles and parallel lines and how to tell if triangles are similar.

subject: Math
description: Understand that a two-dimensional figure is similar to another if the second can be obtained from the first by a sequence of rotations, reflections, translations, and dilations. Given two similar two- dimensional figures, describe a sequence that exhibits the similarity between them.
description_plain: Your child can tell when two flat shapes are similar by showing how one can be turned, flipped, slid, or resized to match the other.

subject: Math
description: Describe the effects of dilations, translations, rotations, and reflections on two-dimensional figures using coordinates.
description_plain: Your child can describe how moving, flipping, or resizing shapes changes their position using coordinates.

subject: Math
description: Understand that a two-dimensional figure is congruent to another if the second can be obtained from the first by a sequence of rotations, reflections, and translations. Given two congruent figures, describe a sequence that exhibits the congruence between them.
description_plain: Your child can tell when two flat shapes are exactly the same size and shape by showing how one can be turned, flipped, or slid to match the other.

subject: Math
description: Parallel lines are transformed to parallel lines.
description_plain: Your child understands that when shapes are moved or changed, parallel lines stay parallel.

subject: Math
description: Angles are transformed to angles of the same measure.
description_plain: Your child understands that when shapes are moved or changed, angles keep the same size.

subject: Math
description: Lines are transformed to lines, and line segments to line segments of the same length.
description_plain: Your child understands that when shapes are moved or changed, lines stay lines and line segments keep their length.

subject: Math
description: Verify experimentally the properties of rotations, reflections, and translations:
description_plain: Your child can test and confirm how rotations, reflections, and slides change shapes.

subject: Math
description: Understand congruence and similarity using physical models, transparencies, or geometry software.
description_plain: Your child uses models or tools to understand when shapes are the same size and shape or just similar.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: (+) Analyze decisions and strategies using probability concepts (e.g., product testing, medical testing, or pulling a hockey goalie at the end of a game and replacing the goalie with an extra skater).★
description_plain: Your child can analyze real-life decisions using probability, like testing products or making game strategies.

subject: Math
description: (+) Analyze decisions and strategies using probability concepts (e.g., product testing, medical testing, or pulling a hockey goalie at the end of a game and replacing the goalie with an extra skater).★
description_plain: Your child can analyze real-life decisions using probability, like testing products or making game strategies.

subject: Math
description: (+) Use probabilities to make fair decisions (e.g., drawing by lots or using a random number generator).★
description_plain: Your child uses probability to help make fair choices, such as drawing lots or using random numbers.

description: (+) Use probabilities to make fair decisions (e.g., drawing by lots or using a random number generator).★

description_plain: Your child uses probability to help make fair choices, such as drawing lots or using random numbers.

subject: Math
description: (+) Evaluate and compare strategies on the basis of expected values.★
description_plain: Your child can figure out and compare different choices by calculating their expected results.

subject: Math
description: (+) Evaluate and compare strategies on the basis of expected values.★
description_plain: Your child can figure out and compare different choices by calculating their expected results.

subject: Math
description: (+) Evaluate and compare strategies on the basis of expected values.★
description_plain: Your child can figure out and compare different choices by calculating their expected results.

description: (+) Evaluate and compare strategies on the basis of expected values.★
description_plain: Your child can figure out and compare different choices by calculating their expected results.

subject: Math
description: (+) Find the expected payoff for a game of chance.★
description_plain: Your child can calculate the average expected result when playing a game of chance.

description: Understand that a function from one set (called the domain) to another set (called the range) assigns to each element of the domain exactly one element of the range. If f is a function and x is an element of its domain, then f(x) denotes the output of f corresponding to the input x. The graph of f is the graph of the equation y = f(x).
description_plain: Your child understands that a function matches each input with exactly one output and can read graphs that show these relationships.

description: Know and apply the Remainder Theorem: For a polynomial p(x) and a number a, the remainder on division by x – a is p(a), so p(a) = 0 if and only if (x – a) is a factor of p(x).
description_plain: Your child knows that plugging a number into a polynomial gives the remainder when dividing by (x – that number), and if the result is zero, (x – that number) is a factor.
