description_plain: Your child can explain ways to help people in their neighborhood or community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify different community helpers (e.g., mayor, police, firefighters, teachers, doctors, cashiers), and analyze the ways they help people
description_plain: Your child can name community helpers like police and firefighters and explain how they help people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain community rules and norms, who makes them, and ways rules and norms contribute to the health of the community
description_plain: Your child can explain community rules, who makes them, and how these rules help keep the community healthy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why people have responsibilities to each other, and analyze actions people take to show civility to each other
description_plain: Your child can explain why people have responsibilities to each other and how they show kindness and respect.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the ways members of a community interact, help each other, and contribute to the community as a whole.
description_plain: Your child can understand how people in a community work together, help each other, and contribute to the community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the similarities and differences between students' neighborhoods and those of their classmates (e.g., landscape, population, availability of resources)
description_plain: Your child can compare their neighborhood with classmates' neighborhoods by looking at things like landscape, population, and resources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain what resources are available in students' neighborhoods and larger communities (e.g., schools, trees, stores, rivers, health services, recreation), and analyze how those resources are made available, and to whom
description_plain: Your child can name resources in their neighborhood and community and explain how these resources are provided and who can use them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the geographical landscape of students' neighborhoods (e.g., urban, rural, suburban, has trees, hilly)
description_plain: Your child can describe the physical features of their neighborhood, like whether it is urban, rural, or suburban and if it has trees or hills.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify boundaries of a neighborhood versus the larger community
description_plain: Your child can identify the boundaries that separate their neighborhood from the larger community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify buildings and places in students' neighborhoods (e.g., school, library, places of worship, community centers), and explain their locations in relation to each other
description_plain: Your child can name important buildings and places in their neighborhood and explain where they are located in relation to each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the relationship between geography, location, and resource availability in how neighborhoods and communities are defined.
description_plain: Your child can understand how geography, location, and available resources help define neighborhoods and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the differences and similarities between how holidays and traditions are celebrated at school and at home
description_plain: Your child can explain how holidays and traditions are celebrated differently or the same at school and at home.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify people, holidays, and traditions that are celebrated and the historical events they represent (e.g., Labor Day, Indigenous Peoples' Day / Columbus Day, Veterans Day, Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. Day, Presidents' Day, Juneteenth, Memorial Day)
description_plain: Your child can name people, holidays, and traditions celebrated and explain the important events they remember, like Labor Day and Veterans Day.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify school traditions (e.g., special school-specific traditions like field day, spirit week)
description_plain: Your child can recognize special school traditions like field day or spirit week.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain traditions and holidays that are celebrated and recognized at school.
description_plain: Your child understands the holidays and traditions celebrated and recognized at school.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the words in the Pledge of Allegiance and why it is recited at school
description_plain: Your child can explain what the words in the Pledge of Allegiance mean and why it is said at school.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the United States flag at school as a way to show national pride
description_plain: Your child can recognize the United States flag at school and understand it shows pride in the country.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify school symbols (e.g., mascot, school colors) and ways to show school pride
description_plain: Your child can identify school symbols like the mascot and school colors and ways to show pride in their school.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways to help in the classroom and at school
description_plain: Your child can explain ways to help out in the classroom and around the school.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain responsibilities that students have to each other
description_plain: Your child understands the responsibilities students have to be kind and helpful to each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the roles and responsibilities of being classroom citizens.
description_plain: Your child understands what it means to be a good member of the classroom by following rules and helping others.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the responsibilities of people at school and at home and how they work together to help students to learn effectively (e.g., teachers, principal, class helpers, custodian, family caregivers)
description_plain: Your child can talk about how teachers, principals, helpers, and family members all work together to help students learn.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the rules, norms, and rights at school help students to learn and teachers to teach and how they create a way for students to work together
description_plain: Your child can explain how school rules help students learn, teachers teach, and everyone work together.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze differences in rules in different places like at home, at school, or in public
description_plain: Your child can notice how rules are different at home, at school, and in public places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify rules and norms of school within the classroom and in other spaces at school (e.g., cafeteria, hallways)
description_plain: Your child can name the rules to follow in the classroom and other places at school like the cafeteria and hallways.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze rules, roles, and responsibilities at school.
description_plain: Your child understands the rules, roles, and responsibilities people have at school.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze what the school provides for the local community socially and how the local community helps the school
description_plain: Your child can explain how the school helps the local community and how the community helps the school.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the relative location of the school in relation to other nearby resources such as stores, services, etc.
description_plain: Your child can describe where the school is compared to nearby places like stores and services.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the relative location of the school in relation to where the students live
description_plain: Your child can explain where the school is located compared to where students live.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the school's address and its location on a map
description_plain: Your child can say the school's address and find it on a map.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the school's place within the local community geographically, socially, and economically.
description_plain: Your child understands where the school is in the community and how it fits in socially and economically.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways jobs provide resources to the community
description_plain: Your child can explain how different jobs provide things that the community needs.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain what income is and the role of money in obtaining goods and services
description_plain: Your child understands what income is and how money is used to buy goods and services.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify ways jobs are determined by where one lives (e.g., closeness to the family) or ways jobs may determine where one lives (e.g., moving to be closer to a job)
description_plain: Your child can explain how where people live can affect their jobs and how jobs can affect where people live.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain what jobs are, different types of jobs, and how they are used to obtain wants and needs
description_plain: Your child understands what jobs are, different kinds of jobs, and how jobs help people get what they want and need.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain scarcity and ways wants and needs play out with limited resources
description_plain: Your child can explain that there are limited resources and how that affects what people want and need.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways families share and distribute resources
description_plain: Your child understands how families share and use resources together.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify family wants and needs, and explain how wants and needs are important in understanding the responsibilities individuals have to each other and the family as a whole
description_plain: Your child can identify family wants and needs and understand how these help family members take care of each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the differences between needs and wants and how these concepts impact family units.
description_plain: Your child understands the difference between needs and wants and how this affects families.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify differences and similarities between the traditions of their families, their classmates' families, and those represented in literature
description_plain: Your child can notice similarities and differences between their family's traditions, classmates' families, and those in stories.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify ways students can explore the different traditions of people in their community
description_plain: Your child can find ways to learn about different traditions in their community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways in which where people live can affect traditions
description_plain: Your child understands that where people live can influence their traditions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify family traditions as they relate to culture (e.g., food, clothing, activities, holidays, religion)
description_plain: Your child can identify family traditions related to culture like food, clothing, holidays, and activities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics of culture (e.g., food, clothing, activities, holidays, religion)
description_plain: Your child can recognize parts of culture such as food, clothing, activities, holidays, and religion.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain a variety of family and cultural traditions through looking at the students' own, those of classmates, and those represented in literature.
description_plain: Your child can explain different family and cultural traditions by learning about their own, classmates', and those in books.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how families make up neighborhoods and neighborhoods create, are a part of, and influence the larger community
description_plain: Your child understands that families make neighborhoods and neighborhoods are part of and affect the larger community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the physical environment where students live
description_plain: Your child can describe the physical environment where they live.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the location of the student's home address and addresses to important places and relative locations near their home
description_plain: Your child can identify their home address and important nearby places and explain where they are.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify resources near families and resources that are farther away (e.g., schools, stores, services for the unhoused, disability services)
description_plain: Your child can name resources close to their family and others that are farther away, like schools and stores.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the purpose and features of a map and a globe and the locations of where students and their classmates live (e.g., city/town, state, and country)
description_plain: Your child can explain what maps and globes are for and find where they and their classmates live on them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the physical location of students' and classmates' families within the community and the relationships between families and the community.
description_plain: Your child understands where their family and classmates live in the community and how families and the community are connected.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways to help at home
description_plain: Explain ways to help at home

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain responsibilities that members of a family have to the family unit (including to pets)
description_plain: Your child understands the responsibilities family members have to each other, including caring for pets.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what rules and norms families have, and explain who makes them and why they exist
description_plain: Your child can name family rules and habits, and explain who makes them and why they are important.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify roles people have in a family unit, and explain how the roles relate to each other
description_plain: Your child can name different jobs or roles people have in a family and explain how these roles work together.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify examples and configurations of a family unit, and explain the characteristics of what makes a family (e.g., adopted families, foster families, heterosexual couple families, families with same sex caregivers, interracial families, families with a single caregiver, extended families)
description_plain: Your child understands different types of families and what makes a family, like adoption, foster care, or single parents.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain families, family roles, and family rules through looking at the student's own, those of classmates, and those represented in literature.
description_plain: Your child can talk about families, their roles, and rules by looking at their own family, classmates' families, and stories.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how different economic systems can create more equitable outcomes for individuals and communities, particularly for those traditionally marginalized from the economic system.
description_plain: Your child can explain how different ways of running economies can help make life fairer for everyone, especially for people who have been treated unfairly before.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how inequities within the economic system have been addressed or sustained by the actions of those traditionally privileged and marginalized.
description_plain: Your child can look at how unfairness in the economy has been kept or changed by people with more or less privilege.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how those traditionally privileged and marginalized across intersecting identities can influence and interact with economic systems.
description_plain: Your child understands how people with different backgrounds and identities can affect and be affected by the economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the ways that different political systems utilize economic systems to organize and distribute goods and services to individuals and communities.
description_plain: Your child can name how different governments use economic systems to share goods and services with people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue whether the costs and benefits of an aspect of the means of production equitably serve all individuals and communities.
description_plain: Your child can explain if the costs and benefits of how things are made are fair to all people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and communities acting through intersectional identities and lived experiences can affect the means of production.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people's identities and experiences affect how things are made and produced.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain who has the power to make decisions related to the means of production and the effects those decisions have on individuals and communities
description_plain: Your child understands who makes decisions about producing goods and services and how those decisions affect people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the individuals and communities involved in the production of any good or service, the materials needed for producing them, where and how the materials are obtained, and the various interrelationships among all of these elements.
description_plain: Your child can name the people and communities involved in making goods or services, the materials used, where they come from, and how all these parts connect.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how a resource can be used differently to create a more equitable outcome for individuals and communities including how individuals and communities can influence systems of power to achieve that change.
description_plain: Your child can explain how using a resource differently can help make things fairer for people and how people can work to change systems of power.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how decisions affecting access to goods and services are influenced by systems of power and cultural norms including how these effects of decisions create more equitable or inequitable outcomes.
description_plain: Your child can explain how decisions about who gets goods and services are influenced by power and culture, and how these decisions can be fair or unfair.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how scarcity affects the cost and availability of desired goods and services, and who has the power to influence the factors related to cost and availability and why.
description_plain: Your child understands how limited resources affect the price and availability of goods and services, and who controls these factors and why.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the choices communities make about how to use resources based on the scarcity of that resource, including those that are familiar and unfamiliar.
description_plain: Your child can explain how communities decide to use resources when they are limited, including resources they know well and those they don't.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the systematic analysis of the spatial patterns provides an integral understanding of a place or region and supports equitable decisions about climate and land use.
description_plain: Your child can explain how studying maps and spatial patterns helps us understand places and make fair choices about climate and land use.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple types of maps, charts, and graphs and how they are used to interpret topographical information, draw inferences about the development of societies, and determine how places shape events and how places may be changed by events.
description_plain: Your child can use different maps, charts, and graphs to understand land shapes, how societies grow, and how places and events affect each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the characteristics and elements of maps, globes, geographic tools, and other technologies are used and selected to identify and describe local, regional and/or global locations.
description_plain: Your child understands how to use maps, globes, and tools to find and describe places nearby or far away.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify maps, globes, and other geographic tools and technologies that are used to describe where places are located both absolutely and relatively across time, space, and distance.
description_plain: Your child can identify maps, globes, and tools that show where places are located in different ways and times.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how decisions about resources and the environment made by individuals and/or communities impact current and future peoples differently and how those decisions might be made more equitable.
description_plain: Your child can explain how choices about resources and the environment affect people now and in the future, and how to make those choices fairer.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and societies at local, regional and/or global levels influence political, economic, and social decision-making.
description_plain: Your child understands how people and societies at different levels influence political, economic, and social decisions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how humans and their societies and institutions affect, modify and/or preserve the environment, as well as how the modifications of the physical environment affect physical, behavioral, and diverse cultural systems.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people and their communities change or protect the environment, and how these changes affect cultures and behaviors.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics of human systems, physical systems, and the environment, and ways they interact at local, regional and/or global levels.
description_plain: Your child can name features of human and physical systems and the environment, and how they interact locally and globally.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the relationship between populations and physical systems influence decision-making about the equitable access to resources and land at the local, regional, and/or global levels.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the relationship between people and the environment affects fair access to resources and land in different places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how human systems and the distribution of populations interact with and impact physical systems, and how conflict and access to resources influence physical systems.
description_plain: Your child understands how people and where they live affect the environment, and how conflicts and resource access change physical systems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how and why a population's characteristics, including their spatial distribution, growth, and movement, have divided, organized, and unified areas of Earth's surface and impacted both human and physical systems.
description_plain: Your child can explain how population features like size, growth, and movement shape areas on Earth and affect both people and the environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics of populations based on their size, place, region, and cultural demographics, as well as identifying patterns of migration.
description_plain: Your child can identify population traits like size, location, culture, and migration patterns.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how all individuals can act as local, national, and/or global agents of social change by using lessons learned from history.
description_plain: Your child understands how anyone can work for social change locally, nationally, or globally by learning from history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze historical change through the intersectional identities and lived experiences of people who have accomplished social change throughout history in relation to systems of power, identity, and resistance.
description_plain: Your child can study how people with different identities and experiences have made social change in history, especially related to power and resistance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how historical and social change have been and continue to be accomplished in relation to systems of power, identity, and resistance.
description_plain: Your child understands how social and historical changes happen through power, identity, and resistance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify peoples, events, technologies, and ideas involved in historical and social change in various geographical and temporal locations.
description_plain: Your child can name important people, events, ideas, and inventions involved in social and historical change in different places and times.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue, using multiple narratives rooted in identity, power, and resistance, how history itself is an interpretation of events.
description_plain: Your child can explain how history is told in different ways based on identity, power, and resistance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple types of sources, including art, music, oral histories, pamphlets, film, texts, etc., through a critical reflection of the creators' and students' intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can look at different sources like art, music, and stories and think about how the creator's and their own identities affect how they understand history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the purpose, audience, and perspective of multiple types of sources (art, music, oral histories, pamphlets, film, texts, etc.) relating to a historical event or series of events, individual, or group of people, including indications of bias toward or against the subject portrayed.
description_plain: Your child understands the purpose, audience, and viewpoint of different sources about history, including recognizing bias.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify key people, central ideas, and the mechanisms by which stories are told and retold regarding an event or series of events, centering the voices of historical actors and groups engaged in resistance and change.
description_plain: Your child can identify key people and ideas in stories about events, focusing on voices of those who resisted and made change.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how social change, intersectional identities, and lived experiences are crucial to the study and practice of history.
description_plain: Your child understands why social change, identities, and experiences are important to studying and practicing history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple sources to compare and contrast historical events through the lenses of identity, power, and resistance.
description_plain: Your child can compare historical events by looking at identity, power, and resistance using different sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain multiple causes and effects of historical events, centering and representing the voices and experiences of individuals and communities who were agents of change and resistance.
description_plain: Your child can explain many causes and effects of historical events, focusing on people and groups who made change and resisted.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify historical events that are culturally relevant to global, national, and local histories and connect to students' intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can name historical events that connect to cultures and histories around the world and relate to their own identities and experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue for a possible solution to make rights equitable and the roles of those involved in pursuing that solution.
description_plain: Your child can explain a fair solution to make rights equal for everyone and describe who helps make that happen.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and communities have been included or excluded from the political process based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences and the impact these actions have had on their rights, responsibilities, and the functioning of a democratic society.
description_plain: Your child understands how some people or groups have been included or left out of politics because of who they are and how this affects their rights and duties.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain different ways communities and individuals inform themselves, exercise their rights and responsibilities, and engage formally and/or informally in political processes.
description_plain: Your child can explain different ways people learn about and take part in politics, both officially and in everyday life.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what rights and responsibilities individuals and communities have in a society and who can take advantage of them.
description_plain: Your child can name the rights and duties people and communities have and who can use them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how rules and laws can be used to create an equitable society.
description_plain: Your child can explain how rules and laws can help make society fair for everyone.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how rules and laws positively and/or negatively impact different individuals and communities based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child understands how rules and laws can help or hurt different people based on who they are and their experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why rules and laws exist, and how they are implemented by and for individuals and communities based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can explain why we have rules and laws and how they are made and used for different people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what rules and laws are, and who has the power to make them, in different settings and cultures that are familiar and unfamiliar to students.
description_plain: Your child can identify what rules and laws are and who makes them in different places and cultures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how power can be distributed and used to create a more equitable society for communities and individuals based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can explain how power can be shared and used to make society fairer for all people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the purpose of government and the use of power, including balancing order and freedom, to advance and control different communities and individuals based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child understands why governments exist and how they use power to keep order and freedom for different people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how political power is and has been obtained and used to govern communities and individuals with attention to their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people get and use political power to lead communities, considering who they are.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what political power is and who has political power in a society.
description_plain: Your child can explain what political power is and who holds it in society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the similarities and differences of the communities being looked at and those of the students (e.g., ways of life, traditions, celebrations, the way communities are organized spatially, community leaders, language, currency)
description_plain: Your child can compare the ways of life, traditions, leaders, language, and money of different communities to their own.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what resources, goods, and services are available to the communities being looked at, and explain ways they obtain those resources and the influences of environment on resource availability
description_plain: Your child can name the resources, goods, and services in different communities and explain how the environment affects what they have.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify physical locations of the communities being looked at and their locations on a map, explain what the physical environment is like for the communities being looked at, and analyze the ways environment influences their ways of life
description_plain: Your child can find where communities are on a map, describe their environment, and explain how it shapes their way of life.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the similarities and differences in the characteristics of communities throughout the world.
description_plain: Your child can compare what makes communities around the world alike and different.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the similarities and differences of the communities being looked at and those of the students (e.g., ways of life, traditions, celebrations, the way communities are organized spatially, types of government)
description_plain: Your child can compare the ways of life, traditions, celebrations, community setup, and types of government of different U.S. communities to their own.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the resources, goods, and services available, and explain how people obtain those resources and the influences of environment on resource availability
description_plain: Your child can name the resources, goods, and services in U.S. communities and explain how people get them and how the environment affects this.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the physical locations of the communities being studied and their locations on a map or globe, explain what the physical environment is like for the communities being looked at, and analyze the ways environment influences their ways of life
description_plain: Your child can find U.S. communities on a map, describe their environment, and explain how it influences their way of life.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the similarities and differences in the characteristics of communities throughout the United States.
description_plain: Your child can compare what makes communities across the United States alike and different.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the similarities and differences of the neighboring communities and those of the students
description_plain: Your child can compare nearby communities to their own and explain what is similar and different.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the availability of resources in the neighboring communities
description_plain: Your child can name the resources available in nearby communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the topography of the neighboring communities (e.g., trees or no trees, hills, beach), and analyze differences between urban, suburban, and rural communities and the relationship to population
description_plain: Your child can describe the land features of nearby communities and explain how city, suburb, and country areas differ in population.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify neighboring communities around students' homes and school (e.g., another city/town, an Indigenous community, a nearby ethnic enclave) and their locations on a map
description_plain: Your child can find nearby communities on a map and name places like towns, Indigenous communities, or ethnic neighborhoods.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the similarities and differences in the characteristics of nearby local communities.
description_plain: Your child can compare what makes nearby communities alike and different.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how to create change through service and community actions (e.g., town hall meetings, peaceful protests, marches), and ways students can be a part of those actions
description_plain: Your child can explain how people make change by helping in the community, like joining meetings or peaceful protests, and how they can join in.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how to create change through voting
description_plain: Your child can explain how voting helps people create change.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify people who have brought change to society, and explain the actions they took to achieve change
description_plain: Your child can name people who made changes in society and explain what they did.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the meaning of social change
description_plain: Your child understands what social change means.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how people create positive social change and the ways students can contribute.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people make positive changes in society and how they can help.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways people in a society work together to resolve conflicts in the community
description_plain: Your child can explain how people work together to solve problems in their community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways problems at home or school can be resolved (e.g., asking for help from trusted adults, communicating)
description_plain: Your child can explain how to solve problems at home or school by asking for help or talking.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify leaders who have helped people resolve their problems (e.g., mayor, judge, Abraham Lincoln, Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr., Susan B. Anthony, Mahatma Gandhi, Malala Yousafzai), and explain their roles in resolution
description_plain: Your child can name leaders who helped solve problems and explain what they did.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how to resolve problems in different settings.
description_plain: Your child can explain how to solve problems in different places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze ways rules may change depending on the setting
description_plain: Your child can explain how rules can change depending on where you are.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the rules and norms of home, school, and the local community, and analyze the ways power is distributed between people at school, at home, and in public
description_plain: Your child can explain the rules at home, school, and community, and how power is shared in these places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways rules, norms, and rights in all settings create a way for people to live and work together
description_plain: Your child can explain how rules and rights help people live and work together.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the norms, rules, and responsibilities in a community and how different rules and responsibilities apply in different settings.
description_plain: Your child can explain the rules and responsibilities in a community and how they differ in various places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how leaders such as community and grassroot leaders are leaders not through elections, but because they have worked to bring change to their communities
description_plain: Your child can explain how some leaders are chosen because they help their community, not by being elected.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how leaders represent the members of a community
description_plain: Your child can explain how leaders speak for the people in their community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how leaders create and enforce rules and laws for the common good of the community
description_plain: Your child can explain how leaders make and enforce rules to help everyone in the community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the election process for leaders and the responsibilities (e.g., decision making, serving the community) that come with leadership
description_plain: Your child can describe how leaders are chosen by voting and what leaders do to help the community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain who community leaders are, both elected and non-elected, and the characteristics of a community leader.
description_plain: Your child understands who community leaders are, whether they are elected or not, and what makes a good leader.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways that people who participate in making the laws and rules can help to create equality and fairness for all people
description_plain: Your child can explain how people who make rules try to be fair and treat everyone equally.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the roles of people who make rules and laws (e.g., mayor, school committee, town/city council)
description_plain: Your child can explain what different leaders like mayors and school committees do to make rules.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics of human rights (e.g., equality, universality, inclusivity), and explain how students can help to promote human rights (e.g., volunteering at a food bank, running a food drive, trash clean up at a park, other service projects)
description_plain: Your child can identify important human rights like fairness and explain how they can help others through activities like volunteering.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the differences between human rights (e.g., access to food, shelter, clean water) and civil rights (e.g., voting rights, having representation in governments)
description_plain: Your child understands the difference between human rights like food and water and civil rights like voting and having a say in government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics of, and explain the responsibilities of citizenship
description_plain: Your child can identify what it means to be a good citizen and what responsibilities come with it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the rights and responsibilities that come with being a citizen of a community.
description_plain: Your child understands the rights and duties people have as members of their community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how people can have abundance of money and/or resources that may allow them to save for the future and/or share with others
description_plain: Your child can explain how some people have extra money or resources to save or share with others.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways people may change how they budget for needs and wants if they face a scarcity of money and/or resources
description_plain: Your child can explain how people change their spending when they have less money or resources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways people make, buy, and sell goods and services
description_plain: Your child can explain how people make, buy, and sell things and services.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the role of money and how people handle scarcity and abundance.
description_plain: Your child understands how money is used and how people deal with having too little or too much.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how people's wants and needs can be different and how different incomes support different types of wants and needs
description_plain: Your child can explain how people want different things and how money affects what they can have.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain job opportunities within students' communities and why some jobs are not locally available, and analyze how different jobs make different contributions to the community
description_plain: Your child can explain what jobs are in their community, why some jobs aren’t nearby, and how different jobs help the community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify different types of jobs and careers, and explain the tools and resources needed to fulfill these jobs
description_plain: Your child can name different jobs and explain what tools or materials people need for those jobs.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the purpose of jobs
description_plain: Explain the purpose of jobs

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze different jobs and careers and how they contribute to the community and economy.
description_plain: Your child understands different jobs and how they help the community and economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how goods and services are distributed in times of emergency (e.g., clean water, community shelters)
description_plain: Your child can explain how goods and services like water and shelters are shared during emergencies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain scarcity, and analyze ways community organizations and businesses respond to community needs
description_plain: Your child understands scarcity and how groups and businesses help meet community needs.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify individuals, organizations and businesses that provide goods and services within students' communities (e.g., schools, stores, police, senior centers, shelters, town/city government)
description_plain: Your child can name people and places in the community that provide goods and services, like stores and police.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what goods and services are available in students' communities
description_plain: Your child can identify goods and services available in their community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the use of goods and services in the local community.
description_plain: Your child understands how goods and services are used in their local community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze human-environmental factors that have changed the local community (e.g., damming a river to make a reservoir, cutting down trees for buildings and to make space for more housing, severe weather events)
description_plain: Your child can explain how things like building dams or cutting trees have changed their community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain factors that may cause people to migrate out of a community (e.g., change in jobs, cost of living, family)
description_plain: Your child can explain reasons why people might move away from a community, like job changes or family.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain factors that may attract people to migrate into a community (e.g., access to jobs, affordable housing, access to parks and greenspaces, quality of schools, availability of goods and services, family)
description_plain: Your child can explain reasons why people might move into a community, like jobs, schools, or parks.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify narratives of immigrant populations within the local community, what they brought to the community (e.g., skills, cultural items from their home country, traditions from their home country), and how immigration can change what a community is like
description_plain: Your child can tell stories about immigrant groups in their community and how they brought new skills and traditions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the ways communities change.
description_plain: Your child understands how communities change over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways people show pride or belonging to their community
description_plain: Your child can explain ways people show they belong or feel proud of their community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze similarities and differences between cultures in their communities
description_plain: Your child can compare different cultures in their community and see what is alike and different.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify local Indigenous communities (e.g., Nahaganset (Narragansett), Nehantick and Eastern Nehantick (Niantic), Nipmuc, Manissean, Massachuset, and Wampanoag (Pokanoket), and explain characteristics of their cultures (e.g., food traditions, regalia, art and musical traditions, heritage celebrations)
description_plain: Your child can identify local Indigenous groups and explain some of their traditions like food, art, and celebrations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify cultures represented in students' communities and where in the world those cultures originated from, and explain characteristics of their cultures (e.g., food traditions, clothing traditions, art and musical traditions, religious beliefs, heritage celebrations)
description_plain: Your child can name cultures in their community, where they come from, and describe their traditions like food and holidays.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the meanings of "culture" and "diversity," and explain the characteristics of culture
description_plain: Your child understands what culture and diversity mean and what makes up a culture.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the culture and diversity in local communities.
description_plain: Your child understands the different cultures and diversity in their local community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how natural resources can contribute to jobs (e.g., forestry, construction, fishing, and mining)
description_plain: Your child can explain how natural resources like forests and water help create jobs like fishing and construction.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how humans can change those resources (e.g., by adding things such as buildings and fisheries, damming a river to make a reservoir for drinking water, community expansion over open land)
description_plain: Your child can explain how people change natural resources by building things like houses or dams.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify natural resources (e.g., forests, water, land) in the local community, and explain how the community uses those resources
description_plain: Your child can identify natural resources in their community and explain how people use them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the use of available natural resources in the local community.
description_plain: Your child understands how natural resources are used in their community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify that there may be people who are unhoused in their communities and there may be shelters and services that are available to them
description_plain: Your child knows some people may not have homes and that shelters and services are available to help them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the different types of homes in students' communities (e.g., houses, apartments, shelters)
description_plain: Your child can explain the different types of homes in their community like houses, apartments, and shelters.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify places that provide resources, goods, and services near their homes and in the community, and explain how those resources are made available, and to whom
description_plain: Your child can name places near home that provide goods and services and explain who uses them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the landscape around students' homes and in their communities (such as urban, rural, suburban, has trees, hilly)
description_plain: Your child can recognize and describe the types of land and surroundings near your home and community, like if it's a city, countryside, or has hills and trees.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the physical features of the local community and its available goods and services.
description_plain: Your child understands the natural features of your local area and knows about the goods and services available there.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how to give directions using a physical map and an electronic mapping tool
description_plain: Your child can explain how to give directions using both paper maps and electronic map tools.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify places of the world on physical and electronic maps
description_plain: Your child can find and point out places on both physical and electronic maps.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify components of maps (e.g., compass rose, where things are in relation to each other, taking a bird's eye view of a place)
description_plain: Your child can identify parts of a map, like the compass rose and how things are located compared to each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify students' home addresses and addresses for other family members and friends
description_plain: Your child can say their home address and the addresses of family and friends.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how to read a map for various purposes such as identifying addresses and sharing directions.
description_plain: Your child understands how to read maps to find addresses and share directions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the availability of resources, goods, and services that are available in a community (e.g., stores, library, schools)
description_plain: Your child can explain what resources, goods, and services are available in a community, like stores, libraries, and schools.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the use of buildings and places that are part of a community
description_plain: Your child understands how different buildings and places are used in a community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the people who make up a community
description_plain: Your child can name the different people who live and work in a community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the meaning of community
description_plain: Your child understands what the word 'community' means.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain what makes a community a community.
description_plain: Your child can explain what makes a group of people and places a community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how different economic systems can create more equitable outcomes for individuals and communities, particularly for those traditionally marginalized from the economic system.
description_plain: Your child can explain how different ways of running economies can help make life fairer for everyone, especially for people who have been treated unfairly before.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how inequities within the economic system have been addressed or sustained by the actions of those traditionally privileged and marginalized.
description_plain: Your child can look at how unfairness in the economy has been kept or changed by people with more or less privilege.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how those traditionally privileged and marginalized across intersecting identities can influence and interact with economic systems.
description_plain: Your child understands how people with different backgrounds and identities can affect and be affected by the economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the ways that different political systems utilize economic systems to organize and distribute goods and services to individuals and communities.
description_plain: Your child can name how different governments use economic systems to share goods and services with people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue whether the costs and benefits of an aspect of the means of production equitably serve all individuals and communities.
description_plain: Your child can explain if the costs and benefits of how things are made are fair to all people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and communities acting through intersectional identities and lived experiences can affect the means of production.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people's identities and experiences affect how things are made and produced.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain who has the power to make decisions related to the means of production and the effects those decisions have on individuals and communities
description_plain: Your child understands who makes decisions about producing goods and services and how those decisions affect people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the individuals and communities involved in the production of any good or service, the materials needed for producing them, where and how the materials are obtained, and the various interrelationships among all of these elements.
description_plain: Your child can name the people and communities involved in making goods or services, the materials used, where they come from, and how all these parts connect.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how a resource can be used differently to create a more equitable outcome for individuals and communities including how individuals and communities can influence systems of power to achieve that change.
description_plain: Your child can explain how using a resource differently can help make things fairer for people and how people can work to change systems of power.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how decisions affecting access to goods and services are influenced by systems of power and cultural norms including how these effects of decisions create more equitable or inequitable outcomes.
description_plain: Your child can explain how decisions about who gets goods and services are influenced by power and culture, and how these decisions can be fair or unfair.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how scarcity affects the cost and availability of desired goods and services, and who has the power to influence the factors related to cost and availability and why.
description_plain: Your child understands how limited resources affect the price and availability of goods and services, and who controls these factors and why.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the choices communities make about how to use resources based on the scarcity of that resource, including those that are familiar and unfamiliar.
description_plain: Your child can explain how communities decide to use resources when they are limited, including resources they know well and those they don't.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the systematic analysis of the spatial patterns provides an integral understanding of a place or region and supports equitable decisions about climate and land use.
description_plain: Your child can explain how studying maps and spatial patterns helps us understand places and make fair choices about climate and land use.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple types of maps, charts, and graphs and how they are used to interpret topographical information, draw inferences about the development of societies, and determine how places shape events and how places may be changed by events.
description_plain: Your child can use different maps, charts, and graphs to understand land shapes, how societies grow, and how places and events affect each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the characteristics and elements of maps, globes, geographic tools, and other technologies are used and selected to identify and describe local, regional and/or global locations.
description_plain: Your child understands how to use maps, globes, and tools to find and describe places nearby or far away.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify maps, globes, and other geographic tools and technologies that are used to describe where places are located both absolutely and relatively across time, space, and distance.
description_plain: Your child can identify maps, globes, and tools that show where places are located in different ways and times.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how decisions about resources and the environment made by individuals and/or communities impact current and future peoples differently and how those decisions might be made more equitable.
description_plain: Your child can explain how choices about resources and the environment affect people now and in the future, and how to make those choices fairer.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and societies at local, regional and/or global levels influence political, economic, and social decision-making.
description_plain: Your child understands how people and societies at different levels influence political, economic, and social decisions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how humans and their societies and institutions affect, modify and/or preserve the environment, as well as how the modifications of the physical environment affect physical, behavioral, and diverse cultural systems.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people and their communities change or protect the environment, and how these changes affect cultures and behaviors.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics of human systems, physical systems, and the environment, and ways they interact at local, regional and/or global levels.
description_plain: Your child can name features of human and physical systems and the environment, and how they interact locally and globally.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the relationship between populations and physical systems influence decision-making about the equitable access to resources and land at the local, regional, and/or global levels.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the relationship between people and the environment affects fair access to resources and land in different places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how human systems and the distribution of populations interact with and impact physical systems, and how conflict and access to resources influence physical systems.
description_plain: Your child understands how people and where they live affect the environment, and how conflicts and resource access change physical systems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how and why a population's characteristics, including their spatial distribution, growth, and movement, have divided, organized, and unified areas of Earth's surface and impacted both human and physical systems.
description_plain: Your child can explain how population features like size, growth, and movement shape areas on Earth and affect both people and the environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics of populations based on their size, place, region, and cultural demographics, as well as identifying patterns of migration.
description_plain: Your child can identify population traits like size, location, culture, and migration patterns.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how all individuals can act as local, national, and/or global agents of social change by using lessons learned from history.
description_plain: Your child understands how anyone can work for social change locally, nationally, or globally by learning from history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze historical change through the intersectional identities and lived experiences of people who have accomplished social change throughout history in relation to systems of power, identity, and resistance.
description_plain: Your child can study how people with different identities and experiences have made social change in history, especially related to power and resistance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how historical and social change have been and continue to be accomplished in relation to systems of power, identity, and resistance.
description_plain: Your child understands how social and historical changes happen through power, identity, and resistance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify peoples, events, technologies, and ideas involved in historical and social change in various geographical and temporal locations.
description_plain: Your child can name important people, events, ideas, and inventions involved in social and historical change in different places and times.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue, using multiple narratives rooted in identity, power, and resistance, how history itself is an interpretation of events.
description_plain: Your child can explain how history is told in different ways based on identity, power, and resistance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple types of sources, including art, music, oral histories, pamphlets, film, texts, etc., through a critical reflection of the creators' and students' intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can look at different sources like art, music, and stories and think about how the creator's and their own identities affect how they understand history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the purpose, audience, and perspective of multiple types of sources (art, music, oral histories, pamphlets, film, texts, etc.) relating to a historical event or series of events, individual, or group of people, including indications of bias toward or against the subject portrayed.
description_plain: Your child understands the purpose, audience, and viewpoint of different sources about history, including recognizing bias.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify key people, central ideas, and the mechanisms by which stories are told and retold regarding an event or series of events, centering the voices of historical actors and groups engaged in resistance and change.
description_plain: Your child can identify key people and ideas in stories about events, focusing on voices of those who resisted and made change.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how social change, intersectional identities, and lived experiences are crucial to the study and practice of history.
description_plain: Your child understands why social change, identities, and experiences are important to studying and practicing history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple sources to compare and contrast historical events through the lenses of identity, power, and resistance.
description_plain: Your child can compare historical events by looking at identity, power, and resistance using different sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain multiple causes and effects of historical events, centering and representing the voices and experiences of individuals and communities who were agents of change and resistance.
description_plain: Your child can explain many causes and effects of historical events, focusing on people and groups who made change and resisted.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify historical events that are culturally relevant to global, national, and local histories and connect to students' intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can name historical events that connect to cultures and histories around the world and relate to their own identities and experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue for a possible solution to make rights equitable and the roles of those involved in pursuing that solution.
description_plain: Your child can explain a fair solution to make rights equal for everyone and describe who helps make that happen.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and communities have been included or excluded from the political process based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences and the impact these actions have had on their rights, responsibilities, and the functioning of a democratic society.
description_plain: Your child understands how some people or groups have been included or left out of politics because of who they are and how this affects their rights and duties.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain different ways communities and individuals inform themselves, exercise their rights and responsibilities, and engage formally and/or informally in political processes.
description_plain: Your child can explain different ways people learn about and take part in politics, both officially and in everyday life.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what rights and responsibilities individuals and communities have in a society and who can take advantage of them.
description_plain: Your child can name the rights and duties people and communities have and who can use them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how rules and laws can be used to create an equitable society.
description_plain: Your child can explain how rules and laws can help make society fair for everyone.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how rules and laws positively and/or negatively impact different individuals and communities based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child understands how rules and laws can help or hurt different people based on who they are and their experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why rules and laws exist, and how they are implemented by and for individuals and communities based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can explain why we have rules and laws and how they are made and used for different people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what rules and laws are, and who has the power to make them, in different settings and cultures that are familiar and unfamiliar to students.
description_plain: Your child can identify what rules and laws are and who makes them in different places and cultures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how power can be distributed and used to create a more equitable society for communities and individuals based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can explain how power can be shared and used to make society fairer for all people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the purpose of government and the use of power, including balancing order and freedom, to advance and control different communities and individuals based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child understands why governments exist and how they use power to keep order and freedom for different people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how political power is and has been obtained and used to govern communities and individuals with attention to their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people get and use political power to lead communities, considering who they are.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what political power is and who has political power in a society.
description_plain: Your child can explain what political power is and who holds it in society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways that students can contribute to positive change on small and large scales (e.g., in their schools, community, country, or world)
description_plain: Your child can describe ways they can help make positive changes at school, in their community, or even the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify contemporary individuals and groups who are working on positive social change (e.g., Greta Thunberg, scientists who work on vaccines, people working for human rights, and front-line workers such as firefighters, police, teachers, doctors, and nurses) and explain how they impact change and the challenges they face
description_plain: Your child can name people and groups today who work to make the world better and explain how they help and the problems they face.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify historical figures who brought positive social change (e.g., Mahatma Gandhi, Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr., Mother Teresa, Nelson Mandela, Susan B Anthony, Jane Goodall), and explain how they impacted change throughout the world and the challenges they faced
description_plain: Your child can name important people from history who made positive changes and explain how they helped and the challenges they had.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how people have brought social change to the world and ways students can contribute to positive change.
description_plain: Your child understands how people have made the world better and how they can help too.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what peace is, and explain ways people try to achieve it
description_plain: Your child knows what peace means and can explain ways people try to keep or make peace.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways governments can resolve conflicts (e.g., agreeing to a cease fire, negotiating a treaty, signing an agreement)
description_plain: Your child can explain how governments solve conflicts by making agreements like treaties or ceasefires.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the causes of wars (e.g., history of unresolved conflict, quest for power over people, quest for power over resources), and explain how they impact people
description_plain: Your child can name reasons why wars happen and explain how wars affect people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify mass conflicts (e.g., civil wars, political instability, and persecution)
description_plain: Your child can identify big conflicts like civil wars and political problems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why governments experience conflict with one another and ways they work together to resolve conflict.
description_plain: Your child understands why governments sometimes fight and how they work together to fix problems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways that individuals can contribute to the resolution of world issues (e.g., by working for or volunteering their time at the above-named organizations)
description_plain: Your child can explain how people can help solve world problems by volunteering or working with organizations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how governmental and non-governmental organizations help individuals and communities internationally deal with issues such scarcity, health, environment, etc. (e.g., World Health Organization, World Trade Organization, Peace Corps, Doctors Without Borders)
description_plain: Your child understands how groups like the World Health Organization help people and communities around the world with problems like health and the environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways leaders in government set international rules about issues such as health, the environment, war, and peace (e.g., United Nations, G-7 Summit)
description_plain: Your child can explain how government leaders make international rules about health, the environment, war, and peace.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the benefits of diplomacy, and explain ways that governments work together to resolve problems, prevent wars, and keep peace
description_plain: Your child knows why diplomacy is important and how governments work together to solve problems and keep peace.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the rules and responsibilities governments and individuals have to each other.
description_plain: Your child understands the rules and duties that governments and people have to each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how individuals and groups have become global leaders through their actions and advocacy for positive change
description_plain: Your child can explain how people and groups become world leaders by standing up for positive change.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the titles and responsibilities of governmental global leaders such as prime minister, emperor/empress, president, and analyze their roles and responsibilities
description_plain: Your child knows the titles of world leaders like president or prime minister and what they do.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify different types of governments (e.g., monarchies, oligarchies, democracies), and analyze their structures
description_plain: Your child can name different types of governments and understand how they are organized.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze types of governments around the world and what makes a person a global leader.
description_plain: Your child understands different governments around the world and what makes someone a global leader.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze ways people work together to resolve global issues such as climate change, wars, and disease
description_plain: Your child can explain how people work together to solve big world problems like climate change, wars, and diseases.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the decisions that people and governments make that can affect the rest of the world (e.g., medical, environmental)
description_plain: Your child understands how decisions made by people and governments can affect the whole world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the responsibilities governments around the world have to each other
description_plain: Your child knows the responsibilities governments have to each other around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the responsibilities people around the world have to each other in an interconnected world
description_plain: Your child understands the responsibilities people have to each other in our connected world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the responsibilities and actions of a global citizen.
description_plain: Your child can explain what it means to be a global citizen and the actions and duties that come with it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the different ways people utilize global communications (e.g., keeping in touch with family, playing video games with distant friends, for commerce)
description_plain: Your child can explain different ways people use global communication, like talking to family, playing games, or buying and selling things.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the influences of the internet on the speed of communication between people
description_plain: Your child understands how the internet makes communication faster between people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the historical evolution of communications such as the printing press (newspapers, books), radio, telephones, the internet
description_plain: Your child can name old and new ways people have communicated, like newspapers, radio, telephones, and the internet.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how the world is interconnected through global communications.
description_plain: Your child understands how people around the world are connected through communication.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the influences of the global economy on peoples' jobs and who benefits
