description_plain: Your child can explain how the global economy affects jobs and who benefits from it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify ways individuals and governments are involved in an interdependent global economy
description_plain: Your child can name ways that people and governments work together in the world to buy and sell things.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why areas of the world that have a surplus of certain resources trade with areas that have less and vice versa
description_plain: Your child understands why places with extra resources trade with places that have less, so everyone gets what they need.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the difference between imports and exports, and identify ways goods and services are distributed throughout the world
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between things a country buys from others (imports) and things it sells to others (exports), and how goods and services travel around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how goods and services are traded around the world and contribute to a global economy.
description_plain: Your child understands how goods and services are bought and sold worldwide and how this helps the global economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the characteristics of local traditions, celebrations, and holidays (e.g., food, clothing, activities, religion), identify what parts of the world they are from, and analyze the similarities and differences between them
description_plain: Your child can describe local traditions, celebrations, and holidays, say where they come from, and notice how they are alike or different.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways that local traditions, celebrations, and holidays have global and cultural influences
description_plain: Your child understands how local traditions and holidays are influenced by cultures from around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what cultural heritage is, and explain ways global cultures are represented by students' families and communities
description_plain: Your child can explain what cultural heritage means and how their family and community show cultures from around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze ways local communities are influenced by global cultural heritages.
description_plain: Your child understands how local communities are shaped by cultures from different parts of the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze ways mass migrations of people can affect locations (e.g., depletion of resources, a change in the environment) and other people (e.g., colonialism's effects on Indigenous peoples, additional strain on resources)
description_plain: Your child can explain how large groups of people moving to new places can change the land and affect other people living there.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze differences between voluntary migrants, refugees, and people who are enslaved
description_plain: Your child understands the differences between people who move by choice, people who run from danger, and people who are forced to move as slaves.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the reasons people have moved and migrated to other places whether they need to, want to, or are forced to (e.g., family, seasonal work, war, a change in the environment like drought, enslavement)
description_plain: Your child can explain why people move to new places, whether they want to, have to, or are forced to, like for family, work, war, or weather changes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the reasons why people move throughout the world and the effects of migration.
description_plain: Your child understands why people move around the world and what happens because of these moves.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways that people can help the environment (e.g., recycling, creating renewable energy such as solar energy, composting, planting trees, urban gardening, ecotourism)
description_plain: Your child can name ways people help the environment, like recycling, using solar energy, composting, planting trees, and gardening in cities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze ways large-scale environmental changes can lead to an increase in natural disasters (e.g., stronger hurricanes, longer periods of drought, increase in wildfires)
description_plain: Your child understands how big changes in the environment can cause more natural disasters, like stronger storms or longer droughts.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze ways that changes in the environment affect animal habitats and populations
description_plain: Your child can explain how changes in the environment affect where animals live and how many there are.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways that individuals and communities can combat pollution through means such as reducing, reusing, and recycling the use of plastics, rubbers, glasses, and paper
description_plain: Your child understands how people and communities can reduce pollution by reusing, recycling, and using less plastic, glass, and paper.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the different types of pollution caused by humans, and analyze the ways pollution can affect the environment (e.g., smoke from wildfires, remnants of plastics in landfills and ocean waters, carbon dioxide emissions from industrialization)
description_plain: Your child can name different kinds of pollution caused by people and explain how pollution harms the environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain human activities that change the environment (e.g., deforestation, damming rivers)
description_plain: Your child understands how human actions like cutting down forests or building dams change the environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze global environmental challenges that happen as a result of human-environmental interactions.
description_plain: Your child can explain big environmental problems caused by how people and nature interact.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the shared experiences, values, and rituals that many people around the world experience (e.g., popular culture such as movies, music, fast food restaurants, shared historical events, sports, fandoms)
description_plain: Your child understands shared experiences and popular things many people enjoy worldwide, like movies, music, sports, and events.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify Indigenous peoples from different areas of the world, explain the characteristics of their cultures, and analyze their similarities and differences
description_plain: Your child can name Indigenous peoples from different places, describe their cultures, and notice how they are similar or different.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify cultures from different areas of the world, and analyze their similarities and differences (it is suggested that teachers use student backgrounds or student choice to choose groups to learn about)
description_plain: Your child can name cultures from different parts of the world and explain how they are alike and different.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the characteristics of culture (e.g., food, clothing, music, art, education, government)
description_plain: Your child understands what culture is by learning about food, clothing, music, art, schools, and governments.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the culture and diversity of communities around the world.
description_plain: Your child understands the different cultures and diversity found in communities around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze ways recent changes in the Earth's climate have affected various communities (e.g., erosion and flooding of coastal Alaskan villages, droughts in the Western United States)
description_plain: Your child can explain how recent climate changes have affected communities, like flooding in Alaska or droughts in the western U.S.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze environmental situations that may cause people to migrate (e.g., migration away from a location to escape a natural disaster, migration to a location that has access to more resources)
description_plain: Your child understands how environmental problems can make people move to new places, like escaping disasters or finding more resources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze ways that natural disasters can change the economic situation of people (e.g., destruction of property, access to food sources, access to clean water)
description_plain: Your child can explain how natural disasters can change people's lives by damaging homes and limiting food and water.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain types of natural disasters (e.g., hurricanes, floods, blizzards, drought, wildfires), and analyze how they can affect human settlement and population
description_plain: Your child understands different natural disasters and how they affect where people live and how many live there.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain that different environments provide different resources for human habitation, and analyze how that can encourage human settlement
description_plain: Your child knows that different environments have different resources, which can encourage people to live there.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze ways throughout time people have adapted to living in different environments (e.g., mountains, deserts, rainforests, woodlands, cold climates, warm climates)
description_plain: Your child can explain how people have learned to live in many different environments like mountains, deserts, and rainforests.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze different global environments and how the environment affects human settlement patterns and migration.
description_plain: Your child understands how different environments around the world affect where people live and move.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify resources that are man-made (e.g., reservoirs, hydroelectricity), and analyze how they help sustain human settlement
description_plain: Your child can name man-made resources like reservoirs and hydroelectric power and explain how they help people live in certain places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how access to natural resources affects human population and settlement
description_plain: Your child understands how having access to natural resources affects where people live and how many live there.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how people get and utilize natural resources (e.g., metals for technology, wood for housing, water for drinking, land for farming, sun for energy)
description_plain: Your child can explain how people get and use natural resources like metals, wood, water, land, and sunlight.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways that natural resources are important to plant and animal life, as well as humans
description_plain: Your child understands why natural resources are important for plants, animals, and people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the different types of natural resources that are available around the world, and analyze who has access to them
description_plain: Your child can name different natural resources around the world and explain who can use them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the use of natural resources around the globe and how resources affect human settlement.
description_plain: Your child understands how natural resources are used worldwide and how they affect where people live.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics and locations of different types of bodies of water around the globe (e.g., Mediterranean Sea, Great Lakes, Nile River), and explain how humans use them
description_plain: Your child can name different bodies of water around the world, say where they are, and explain how people use them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the physical characteristics and locations of different land features around the world (e.g., Amazon rainforest, Sahara Desert, Mount Everest, Great Barrier Reef), and explain how people interact with those locations (e.g., resources available for human use, how people adapt to living in different places)
description_plain: Your child can name different land features around the world, say where they are, and explain how people use or live in those places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the equator, Prime Meridian, and four hemispheres on a map and globe, and explain what those features are
description_plain: Your child can find the equator, Prime Meridian, and four hemispheres on a map or globe and explain what they are.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the locations of the seven continents and the five oceans
description_plain: Your child can find the seven continents and five oceans on a map or globe.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain global physical and geographical features.
description_plain: Your child understands important landforms and natural features around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the different uses of maps and globes and how they show different features such as populations, culture, socio-political lines, and topographies
description_plain: Your child can explain how maps and globes show different things like where people live, cultures, borders, and land shapes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the relative locations of students' homes and schools, and explain the difference between absolute location and relative locations
description_plain: Your child can find where their home and school are on a map and explain the difference between exact locations and nearby places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify latitude and longitude in geographical terms as ways of showing absolute location
description_plain: Your child knows that latitude and longitude are lines used to show exact places on Earth.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify geographical map and globe terms (e.g., compass rose, map key, latitude, and longitude, time zones)
description_plain: Your child can name parts of maps and globes like the compass rose, map key, latitude, longitude, and time zones.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain maps and globes, geographical concepts pertaining to them, how to read them, and their uses.
description_plain: Your child understands how to read maps and globes and knows what they are used for.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how different economic systems can create more equitable outcomes for individuals and communities, particularly for those traditionally marginalized from the economic system.
description_plain: Your child can explain how different ways of running economies can help make life fairer for everyone, especially for people who have been treated unfairly before.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how inequities within the economic system have been addressed or sustained by the actions of those traditionally privileged and marginalized.
description_plain: Your child can look at how unfairness in the economy has been kept or changed by people with more or less privilege.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how those traditionally privileged and marginalized across intersecting identities can influence and interact with economic systems.
description_plain: Your child understands how people with different backgrounds and identities can affect and be affected by the economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the ways that different political systems utilize economic systems to organize and distribute goods and services to individuals and communities.
description_plain: Your child can name how different governments use economic systems to share goods and services with people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue whether the costs and benefits of an aspect of the means of production equitably serve all individuals and communities.
description_plain: Your child can explain if the costs and benefits of how things are made are fair to all people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and communities acting through intersectional identities and lived experiences can affect the means of production.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people's identities and experiences affect how things are made and produced.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain who has the power to make decisions related to the means of production and the effects those decisions have on individuals and communities
description_plain: Your child understands who makes decisions about producing goods and services and how those decisions affect people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the individuals and communities involved in the production of any good or service, the materials needed for producing them, where and how the materials are obtained, and the various interrelationships among all of these elements.
description_plain: Your child can name the people and communities involved in making goods or services, the materials used, where they come from, and how all these parts connect.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how a resource can be used differently to create a more equitable outcome for individuals and communities including how individuals and communities can influence systems of power to achieve that change.
description_plain: Your child can explain how using a resource differently can help make things fairer for people and how people can work to change systems of power.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how decisions affecting access to goods and services are influenced by systems of power and cultural norms including how these effects of decisions create more equitable or inequitable outcomes.
description_plain: Your child can explain how decisions about who gets goods and services are influenced by power and culture, and how these decisions can be fair or unfair.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how scarcity affects the cost and availability of desired goods and services, and who has the power to influence the factors related to cost and availability and why.
description_plain: Your child understands how limited resources affect the price and availability of goods and services, and who controls these factors and why.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the choices communities make about how to use resources based on the scarcity of that resource, including those that are familiar and unfamiliar.
description_plain: Your child can explain how communities decide to use resources when they are limited, including resources they know well and those they don't.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the systematic analysis of the spatial patterns provides an integral understanding of a place or region and supports equitable decisions about climate and land use.
description_plain: Your child can explain how studying maps and spatial patterns helps us understand places and make fair choices about climate and land use.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple types of maps, charts, and graphs and how they are used to interpret topographical information, draw inferences about the development of societies, and determine how places shape events and how places may be changed by events.
description_plain: Your child can use different maps, charts, and graphs to understand land shapes, how societies grow, and how places and events affect each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the characteristics and elements of maps, globes, geographic tools, and other technologies are used and selected to identify and describe local, regional and/or global locations.
description_plain: Your child understands how to use maps, globes, and tools to find and describe places nearby or far away.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify maps, globes, and other geographic tools and technologies that are used to describe where places are located both absolutely and relatively across time, space, and distance.
description_plain: Your child can identify maps, globes, and tools that show where places are located in different ways and times.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how decisions about resources and the environment made by individuals and/or communities impact current and future peoples differently and how those decisions might be made more equitable.
description_plain: Your child can explain how choices about resources and the environment affect people now and in the future, and how to make those choices fairer.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and societies at local, regional and/or global levels influence political, economic, and social decision-making.
description_plain: Your child understands how people and societies at different levels influence political, economic, and social decisions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how humans and their societies and institutions affect, modify and/or preserve the environment, as well as how the modifications of the physical environment affect physical, behavioral, and diverse cultural systems.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people and their communities change or protect the environment, and how these changes affect cultures and behaviors.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics of human systems, physical systems, and the environment, and ways they interact at local, regional and/or global levels.
description_plain: Your child can name features of human and physical systems and the environment, and how they interact locally and globally.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the relationship between populations and physical systems influence decision-making about the equitable access to resources and land at the local, regional, and/or global levels.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the relationship between people and the environment affects fair access to resources and land in different places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how human systems and the distribution of populations interact with and impact physical systems, and how conflict and access to resources influence physical systems.
description_plain: Your child understands how people and where they live affect the environment, and how conflicts and resource access change physical systems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how and why a population's characteristics, including their spatial distribution, growth, and movement, have divided, organized, and unified areas of Earth's surface and impacted both human and physical systems.
description_plain: Your child can explain how population features like size, growth, and movement shape areas on Earth and affect both people and the environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics of populations based on their size, place, region, and cultural demographics, as well as identifying patterns of migration.
description_plain: Your child can identify population traits like size, location, culture, and migration patterns.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how all individuals can act as local, national, and/or global agents of social change by using lessons learned from history.
description_plain: Your child understands how anyone can work for social change locally, nationally, or globally by learning from history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze historical change through the intersectional identities and lived experiences of people who have accomplished social change throughout history in relation to systems of power, identity, and resistance.
description_plain: Your child can study how people with different identities and experiences have made social change in history, especially related to power and resistance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how historical and social change have been and continue to be accomplished in relation to systems of power, identity, and resistance.
description_plain: Your child understands how social and historical changes happen through power, identity, and resistance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify peoples, events, technologies, and ideas involved in historical and social change in various geographical and temporal locations.
description_plain: Your child can name important people, events, ideas, and inventions involved in social and historical change in different places and times.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue, using multiple narratives rooted in identity, power, and resistance, how history itself is an interpretation of events.
description_plain: Your child can explain how history is told in different ways based on identity, power, and resistance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple types of sources, including art, music, oral histories, pamphlets, film, texts, etc., through a critical reflection of the creators' and students' intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can look at different sources like art, music, and stories and think about how the creator's and their own identities affect how they understand history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the purpose, audience, and perspective of multiple types of sources (art, music, oral histories, pamphlets, film, texts, etc.) relating to a historical event or series of events, individual, or group of people, including indications of bias toward or against the subject portrayed.
description_plain: Your child understands the purpose, audience, and viewpoint of different sources about history, including recognizing bias.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify key people, central ideas, and the mechanisms by which stories are told and retold regarding an event or series of events, centering the voices of historical actors and groups engaged in resistance and change.
description_plain: Your child can identify key people and ideas in stories about events, focusing on voices of those who resisted and made change.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how social change, intersectional identities, and lived experiences are crucial to the study and practice of history.
description_plain: Your child understands why social change, identities, and experiences are important to studying and practicing history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple sources to compare and contrast historical events through the lenses of identity, power, and resistance.
description_plain: Your child can compare historical events by looking at identity, power, and resistance using different sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain multiple causes and effects of historical events, centering and representing the voices and experiences of individuals and communities who were agents of change and resistance.
description_plain: Your child can explain many causes and effects of historical events, focusing on people and groups who made change and resisted.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify historical events that are culturally relevant to global, national, and local histories and connect to students' intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can name historical events that connect to cultures and histories around the world and relate to their own identities and experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue for a possible solution to make rights equitable and the roles of those involved in pursuing that solution.
description_plain: Your child can explain a fair solution to make rights equal for everyone and describe who helps make that happen.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and communities have been included or excluded from the political process based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences and the impact these actions have had on their rights, responsibilities, and the functioning of a democratic society.
description_plain: Your child understands how some people or groups have been included or left out of politics because of who they are and how this affects their rights and duties.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain different ways communities and individuals inform themselves, exercise their rights and responsibilities, and engage formally and/or informally in political processes.
description_plain: Your child can explain different ways people learn about and take part in politics, both officially and in everyday life.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what rights and responsibilities individuals and communities have in a society and who can take advantage of them.
description_plain: Your child can name the rights and duties people and communities have and who can use them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how rules and laws can be used to create an equitable society.
description_plain: Your child can explain how rules and laws can help make society fair for everyone.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how rules and laws positively and/or negatively impact different individuals and communities based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child understands how rules and laws can help or hurt different people based on who they are and their experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why rules and laws exist, and how they are implemented by and for individuals and communities based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can explain why we have rules and laws and how they are made and used for different people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what rules and laws are, and who has the power to make them, in different settings and cultures that are familiar and unfamiliar to students.
description_plain: Your child can identify what rules and laws are and who makes them in different places and cultures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how power can be distributed and used to create a more equitable society for communities and individuals based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can explain how power can be shared and used to make society fairer for all people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the purpose of government and the use of power, including balancing order and freedom, to advance and control different communities and individuals based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child understands why governments exist and how they use power to keep order and freedom for different people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how political power is and has been obtained and used to govern communities and individuals with attention to their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people get and use political power to lead communities, considering who they are.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what political power is and who has political power in a society.
description_plain: Your child can explain what political power is and who holds it in society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the major industries in the West (e.g., mining, oil, gas, forestry, health care, technology, tourism), explain how they support jobs and the economy, and argue the ways geography and the environment impacted the growth of these industries
description_plain: Your child can name important industries in the Western U.S., explain how they create jobs, and describe how the land and environment helped them grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify notable landmarks (e.g., California missions, Hoover Dam, Route 66, numerous national and state parks) and explain their importance (e.g., supports tourism, creates community pride)
description_plain: Your child can identify famous places in the West and explain why they are important for visitors and local pride.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major cities of the West and their locations, analyze why the cities are located where they are, and argue the ways that geography and access to resources impacted the growth of these cities
description_plain: Your child can name big cities in the West, say where they are, and explain how geography and resources helped them grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the geography and environment of the Western region contributed to its social, cultural, and economic development.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the land and environment shaped the culture, economy, and communities in the Western U.S.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify population and demographic statistics (e.g., gender, ethnic backgrounds, religions) of the West and analyze what the data describes about the region
description_plain: Your child can find information about the people living in the West, like their backgrounds and religions, and explain what this tells us about the region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify immigrant groups that came to the West over time (e.g., Chinese, Japanese, Mexicans, white and Black Americans moving west, and people from other Central American countries and other regions of the world), explain their reasons for leaving their home country and coming to the United States, and analyze the ways they continue traditions today (e.g, through food, music, art, language, community gatherings and celebrations)
description_plain: Your child can name groups of immigrants who moved to the West, explain why they came, and describe how they keep their traditions alive today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the reasons colonial European immigrants (e.g., Spanish, Russian) came to the West and analyze the patterns of their settlements
description_plain: Your child knows why early European settlers came to the West and can explain where they settled.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the different Indigenous groups in the West (e.g., Athabaskan, Cahuilla, Chamorros, Chumash, Hawaiian, Haida, Paiute, Salish, Tlingit, Washoe, Yurok), their location on a map, and explain the ways they continue their cultural traditions today (e.g., through food, regalia, music, art, language, community gatherings and celebrations) including differences in traditions of groups living in the Pacific Northwest, the Great Basin, Alaska, Hawaii, southern California, and the territories
description_plain: Your child can name Native American groups in the West, show where they live on a map, and explain how they keep their cultures alive through food, music, and celebrations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the ways diverse peoples have come to live in the Western region of the United States over time.
description_plain: Your child understands how different groups of people have come to live in the Western U.S. over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the symbolism associated with the states and territories (e.g., flag, state motto, state flower, state bird) and explain the reasons those symbols are used
description_plain: Your child can identify state symbols like flags and flowers and explain why those symbols are important.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify when each state and territory in the West became a state or joined the United States and explain why
description_plain: Your child knows when Western states and territories became part of the U.S. and why.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the U.S. territories and capitals considered part of the Western region of the United States (American Samoa, Guam, Midway Islands, Northern Mariana Islands, and Wake Island) and where they are located
description_plain: Your child can name U.S. territories in the West, their capitals, and where they are located.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the states and their capitals in the Western region of the United States (Alaska, California, Colorado, Hawaii, Idaho, Montana, Nevada, Oregon, Utah, Washington, and Wyoming) and where they are located
description_plain: Your child can name Western states and their capitals and show where they are on a map.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the states and territories that make up the Western region including their admittance or incorporation to the United States and their identities.
description_plain: Your child understands which states and territories make up the Western region and their history in the U.S.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major natural resources in the West (e.g., minerals, crude oil, fish - freshwater and marine, timber) and explain their importance to the region
description_plain: Your child can name important natural resources in the West and explain why they matter to the area.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain different climate zones and weather patterns in areas of the West
description_plain: Your child understands different weather and climate zones found in the Western U.S.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major geographical features of the West (e.g., Pacific Ocean, Sierra Nevada Mountains, part of the Rocky Mountains, Mojave and Great Basin deserts, Mount Saint Helens, Mauna Loa) on a map and explain their importance to the region
description_plain: Your child can find major land features in the West on a map and explain why they are important.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the geography and environment of the Western region of the United States.
description_plain: Your child understands the geography and environment of the Western U.S.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major industries in the Southwest (e.g., agriculture - cattle, sheep, alfalfa, hay, mining, manufacturing, oil, natural gas, tourism), explain how they support jobs and the economy, and argue the ways geography and the environment impacted the growth of these industries
description_plain: Your child can name key industries in the Southwest, explain how they create jobs, and describe how the land helped these industries grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify notable landmarks (e.g., Los Alamos, Alamo Mission, numerous national and state parks) and explain their importance (e.g., supports tourism, creates community pride)
description_plain: Your child can identify important landmarks in the Southwest and explain why they matter to visitors and local people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major cities of the Southwest and their locations, analyze why the cities are located where they are, and argue the ways that geography and access to resources impacted the growth of cities
description_plain: Your child can name big cities in the Southwest, say where they are, and explain how geography and resources helped them grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the geography and environment of the Southwestern region contributed to its social, cultural, and economic development.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the land and environment shaped the culture, economy, and communities in the Southwestern U.S.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify population and demographic statistics (e.g., gender, ethnic backgrounds, religions) of the Southwest and analyze what the data describes about the region
description_plain: Your child can find information about the people living in the Southwest, like their backgrounds and religions, and explain what this tells us about the region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify immigrant groups that have come to the Southwest over time (e.g., white and Black Americans moving west, Mexicans, and people from other Central American countries and other regions of the world), explain their reasons for leaving their home country and coming to the United States, and the ways they continue traditions today (e.g, through food, music, art, language, community gatherings and celebrations)
description_plain: Your child can name groups of immigrants who moved to the Southwest, explain why they came, and describe how they keep their traditions alive today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the reasons Spanish colonial immigrants moved into the Southwest and analyze the patterns of their settlements
description_plain: Your child knows why Spanish settlers moved to the Southwest and can explain where they settled.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the different Indigenous groups in the Southwest (e.g., Ancestral Puebloans, Apache, Cherokee, Hopi, Kiowa, Diné, Zuni), their location on a map, and explain the ways they continue their cultural traditions today (e.g., through food, regalia, music, art, language, community gatherings and celebrations)
description_plain: Your child can name Native American groups in the Southwest, show where they live on a map, and explain how they keep their cultures alive through food, music, and celebrations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the ways diverse peoples have come to live in the Southwestern region of the United States over time.
description_plain: Your child understands how different groups of people have come to live in the Southwestern U.S. over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the symbolism associated with the states (e.g., flag, state motto, state flower, state bird) and the explain reasons those symbols are used
description_plain: Your child can identify state symbols like flags and flowers and explain why those symbols are important.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify when each state in the Southwest became a state and explain why
description_plain: Your child knows when Southwestern states became part of the U.S. and why.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the states and capitals in the Southwestern region of the United States (Arizona, New Mexico, Oklahoma, and Texas) and where they are located on a map
description_plain: Your child can name Southwestern states and their capitals and show where they are on a map.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the states that make up the Southwestern region including their admittance to the United States and their identities.
description_plain: Your child understands which states make up the Southwestern region and their history in the U.S.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major natural resources in the Southwest (e.g., coal, copper, iron, silver, silicon, lumber, fish - freshwater and marine) and explain their importance to the region
description_plain: Your child can name important natural resources in the Southwest and explain why they matter to the area.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain different climate zones and weather patterns in areas of the Southwest
description_plain: Your child understands different weather and climate zones found in the Southwestern U.S.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major geographical features of the Southwest (e.g., Rio Grande, Colorado River, parts of the Mojave, Sonoran, and Chihuahuan deserts, part of the Rocky Mountains) on a map and explain their importance to the region
description_plain: Your child can find important places like the Rio Grande and the Rocky Mountains on a map of the Southwest and explain why they matter to the area.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the geography and environment of the Southwestern region of the United States.
description_plain: Your child understands what the land and environment are like in the Southwestern United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major industries in the Midwest (e.g., agriculture - beef, wheat, corn, soybeans, banking, car manufacturing), explain how they support jobs and the economy, and argue the ways geography and the environment impacted the growth of these industries
description_plain: Your child can name big businesses in the Midwest like farming and car making, explain how they create jobs, and describe how the land helped these businesses grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify notable landmarks (e.g., Gateway Arch, Great Plains, national and state parks) and explain their importance
description_plain: Your child can point out important places like the Gateway Arch and national parks in the Midwest and explain why they are special.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major cities of the Midwest and their locations, analyze why the cities are located where they are, and argue the ways that geography and access to resources impacted the growth of these cities
description_plain: Your child can name major cities in the Midwest, find them on a map, and explain how the land and resources helped these cities grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the geography and environment of the Midwestern region contributed to its social, cultural, and economic development.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the land and environment helped shape the culture, economy, and communities in the Midwest.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify population and demographic statistics (e.g., gender, ethnic backgrounds, religions) of the Midwest and analyze what the data describes about the region
description_plain: Your child can find facts about the people living in the Midwest, like their backgrounds and religions, and explain what these facts tell us about the region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify immigrant groups that came to the Midwest over time (e.g., Germans, Irish, Poles, Jews, Hungarians, Czechs, Swedes, Norwegians, Black Americans moving north, and people from other regions of the world), explain their reasons for leaving their home countries and coming to the United States, and explain the ways they continue traditions today (e.g., through food, music, art, language, community gatherings and celebrations)
description_plain: Your child can name groups of immigrants who moved to the Midwest, explain why they left their countries, and describe how they keep their traditions alive today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the reasons different early colonial European immigrants (e.g., French, Spanish, English) came to the Midwest and analyze the patterns of their settlements
description_plain: Your child can explain why early European settlers like the French and English came to the Midwest and where they settled.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the different Indigenous groups in the Midwest (e.g., Dakota, Huron, Omaha, Kickapoo, Kiowa, Lakota, Osage, Ojibwa, Pawnee, Quapaw, Sioux), their location on a map, and explain the ways they continue their cultural traditions today (e.g., through food, regalia, music, art, language, community gatherings and celebrations), paying particular attention to the differences in traditions of groups living near the Great Lakes and those living on the Great Plains
description_plain: Your child can name Native American groups in the Midwest, show where they lived on a map, and explain how they keep their traditions alive today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the ways diverse peoples have come to live in the Midwestern region of the United States over time.
description_plain: Your child understands how different groups of people have come to live in the Midwest over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the symbolism associated with the states (e.g., flag, state motto, state flower, state bird) and explain the reasons those symbols are used
description_plain: Your child can identify state symbols like flags and flowers in the Midwest and explain why those symbols are important.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify when each state in the Midwest became a state and explain why
description_plain: Your child can say when each Midwestern state became part of the United States and explain why.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the states and capitals in the Midwestern region of the United States (Illinois, Indiana, Iowa, Kansas, Michigan, Minnesota, Missouri, Nebraska, North Dakota, Ohio, South Dakota, Wisconsin) and where they are located on a map
description_plain: Your child can name the Midwestern states and their capitals and find them on a map.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the states that make up the Midwestern region including their admittance to the United States and their identities.
description_plain: Your child understands which states make up the Midwest, when they joined the U.S., and what makes each state unique.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major natural resources in the Midwest (e.g., coal, petroleum, natural gas, crude oil, minerals, agriculture - hogs, corn, soybeans, wheat) and explain their importance to the region
description_plain: Your child can name important natural resources in the Midwest like coal and crops and explain why they matter to the region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain different climate zones and weather patterns of the Midwest
description_plain: Your child understands the different weather and climate areas in the Midwest.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain major geographical features of the Midwest (e.g., Great Plains, Great Lakes, Missouri River, Ohio River, upper Mississippi River) on a map and explain their importance to the region
description_plain: Your child can find big land features like the Great Lakes and rivers on a map of the Midwest and explain why they are important.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the geography and environment of the Midwestern region of the United States.
description_plain: Your child understands the land and environment of the Midwestern United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major industries in the Southeast (e.g., agriculture, steel manufacturing, coal mining, lumber industry, service industry, tourism), explain how they support jobs and the economy, and argue the ways geography and the environment impacted the growth of these industries
description_plain: Your child can name important businesses in the Southeast like farming and tourism, explain how they create jobs, and describe how the land helped these businesses grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify notable landmarks (e.g., Kennedy Space Center, Edmund Pettus (Selma) Bridge, Music City, national and state parks) and explain their importance (e.g., supports tourism, creates community pride)
description_plain: Your child can point out important places like the Kennedy Space Center and national parks in the Southeast and explain why they are special.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major cities of the Southeast and their locations, analyze why the cities are located where they are, and argue the ways that geography and access to resources impacted the growth of these cities
description_plain: Your child can name major cities in the Southeast, find them on a map, and explain how the land and resources helped these cities grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the geography and environment of the Southeastern region contributed to its social, cultural, and economic development.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the land and environment helped shape the culture, economy, and communities in the Southeast.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify population and demographic statistics (e.g., gender, ethnic backgrounds, religions) of the Southeast and analyze what the data describes about the region
description_plain: Your child can find facts about the people living in the Southeast, like their backgrounds and religions, and explain what these facts tell us about the region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify immigrant groups that have come to the Southeast over time (e.g., English, French, Scottish, Scotch-Irish, Germans, Mexicans, Spanish, and people from many Central American countries and other regions of the world), explain their reasons for leaving their home country and coming to the United States, and explain the ways they continue traditions today (e.g, through food, music, art, language, community gatherings and celebrations)
description_plain: Your child can name groups of immigrants who moved to the Southeast, explain why they left their countries, and describe how they keep their traditions alive today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how people from Africa were forced to move to the United States through the transatlantic slave trade, and analyze the effects of that trade in the Southeast
description_plain: Your child understands how people from Africa were forced to come to the Southeast and how this affected the region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the reasons different early colonial European immigrants (e.g., English, French, Spanish) came to the Southeast, and analyze the patterns of their settlements
description_plain: Your child can explain why early European settlers like the English and French came to the Southeast and where they settled.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify different Indigenous groups in the Southeast (e.g., Chickasaw, Choctaw, Cherokee, Creek, Natchez, Seminole, Taino), their location on a map, and explain the ways they continue their cultural traditions today (e.g., through food, regalia, music, art, language, community gatherings and celebrations)
description_plain: Your child can name Native American groups in the Southeast, show where they lived on a map, and explain how they keep their traditions alive today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the ways diverse peoples have come to live in the Southeastern region of the United States over time.
description_plain: Your child understands how different groups of people have come to live in the Southeast over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the symbolism associated with the states and territories (e.g., flag, state motto, state flower, state bird), and explain the reasons those symbols are used
description_plain: Your child can identify state symbols like flags and flowers in the Southeast and explain why those symbols are important.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify when each state and territory in the Southeast became a state or joined the United States and explain why
description_plain: Your child can say when each Southeastern state or territory became part of the United States and explain why.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the U.S. territories and capitals considered part of the Southeastern region (Puerto Rico, United States Virgin Islands) and where they are located
description_plain: Your child can name U.S. territories in the Southeast like Puerto Rico and find them on a map.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the states and capitals in the Southeastern region of the United States (Alabama, Arkansas, Florida, Georgia, Kentucky, Louisiana, Mississippi, North Carolina, South Carolina, Tennessee, Virginia, and West Virginia) and where they are located
description_plain: Your child can name the Southeastern states and their capitals and find them on a map.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the states and territories that make up the Southeastern region including their admittance or incorporation to the United States and their identities.
description_plain: Your child understands which states and territories make up the Southeast, when they joined the U.S., and what makes each place unique.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major natural resources in the Southeast (e.g., agriculture - rice, cotton, citrus, sugar cane, tobacco, peanuts; oil; natural gas; coal; phosphate; fish - freshwater and marine), and explain their importance to the region
description_plain: Your child can name important natural resources in the Southeast like crops and minerals and explain why they matter to the region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain different climate zones and weather patterns in areas of the Southeast
description_plain: Your child understands the different weather and climate areas in the Southeast.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major geographical features of the Southeast (e.g., Appalachian Mountains, Gulf of Mexico, Mississippi River, Atlantic Ocean) on a map, and explain their importance to the region
description_plain: Your child can find big land features like the Appalachian Mountains and the Gulf of Mexico on a map of the Southeast and explain why they are important.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the geography and environment in the Southeastern region of the United States.
description_plain: Your child understands the land and environment of the Southeastern United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major industries in the Northeast (e.g., technology, manufacturing, education, healthcare, tourism, service industry), explain how they support jobs and the economy, and argue the ways geography and the environment impacted the growth of these industries
description_plain: Your child can name important businesses in the Northeast like technology and healthcare, explain how they create jobs, and describe how the land helped these businesses grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify notable landmarks (e.g., the country's capital in Washington D.C., Statue of Liberty, Liberty Bell, sites from the American Revolution, national and state parks) and explain their importance (e.g., supports tourism, creates community pride)
description_plain: Your child can point out important places like the Statue of Liberty and the Liberty Bell in the Northeast and explain why they are special.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major cities of the Northeast and their locations, analyze why the cities are located where they are, and argue the ways that geography and access to resources impacted the growth of these cities
description_plain: Your child can name major cities in the Northeast, find them on a map, and explain how geography and resources helped these cities grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the geography and environment of the Northeastern region contributed to its social, cultural, and economic development.
description_plain: Your child understands how the land and environment in the Northeast shaped its people, culture, and economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify population and demographic statistics (e.g., gender, ethnic backgrounds, religions) of the Northeast, and analyze what the data describes about the region
description_plain: Your child can find information about the people living in the Northeast, like their backgrounds and religions, and explain what this tells us about the region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify immigrant groups that have come to the Northeast over time (e.g., Brazilians, Cambodians, Cape Verdeans, Chinese, Colombians, Dominicans, French-Canadians, Guatemalans, Haitians, Hmong, Portuguese, Puerto Ricans, Indians, Irish, Mexicans, Salvadorans, Somalis, Vietnamese, and people from other regions of the world), explain their reasons for leaving their home country and coming to the United States, and analyze the ways they continue traditions today (e.g., through food, music, art, language, community gatherings and celebrations)
description_plain: Your child can name groups of immigrants who moved to the Northeast, explain why they left their countries, and describe how they keep their traditions alive today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how people from Africa were forced to move to the United States through the transatlantic slave trade and analyze the effects of that trade in the Northeast
description_plain: Your child understands how African people were forced to come to the United States through the slave trade and how this affected the Northeast.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the reasons different early colonial European immigrants (e.g., Dutch, English, including Pilgrims, French, Germans) came to the Northeast, and analyze the patterns of their settlements
description_plain: Your child can explain why early European settlers came to the Northeast and describe where they settled.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify different Indigenous groups in the Northeast (e.g., Mohawk, Oneida, Narragansett, Wampanoag, Massachuset, Wabanaki), their location on a map, and explain the ways they continue their cultural traditions today (e.g., through food, regalia, music, art, language, community gatherings and celebrations)
description_plain: Your child can name Native American groups in the Northeast, show where they lived on a map, and explain how they keep their culture alive today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the ways diverse peoples have come to live in the Northeastern region of the United States over time.
description_plain: Your child understands how different groups of people have come to live in the Northeast over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the symbolism associated with the states (e.g., flag, state motto, state flower, state bird), and explain the reasons those symbols are used
description_plain: Your child can identify state symbols like flags and flowers and explain why those symbols are important.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify when each state in the Northeast became a state, and explain why
description_plain: Your child can say when each Northeast state became part of the United States and why.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the states and capitals in the Northeastern region of the United States (Connecticut, Delaware, Maine, Maryland, Massachusetts, New Hampshire, New Jersey, New York, Pennsylvania, Rhode Island, Vermont, and the District of Columbia) and where they are located on a map
description_plain: Your child can name the states and capitals in the Northeast and find them on a map.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the states that make up the Northeastern region including their admittance to the United States and their identities.
description_plain: Your child understands which states are in the Northeast, when they joined the U.S., and what makes each state unique.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major natural resources in the Northeast (e.g., granite, iron ore, lumber, fish - freshwater and marine) and explain their importance to the region
description_plain: Your child can name important natural resources in the Northeast and explain why they matter to the region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain different climate zones and weather patterns in areas of the Northeast
description_plain: Your child understands the different weather and climate areas in the Northeast.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major geographical features of the Northeast (e.g., Atlantic coastline, Appalachian Mountains, woodlands, Hudson River, eastern portion of the Great Lakes) on a map and explain their importance to the region
description_plain: Your child can find major land features like mountains and rivers in the Northeast on a map and explain why they are important.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the geography and environment of the Northeastern region of the United States.
description_plain: Your child understands the land and environment of the Northeastern United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major imports and exports of the United States, and explain which countries are trading partners
description_plain: Your child can name important things the U.S. buys and sells to other countries and say who we trade with.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major industries of the United States (e.g., healthcare, education services, finance and insurance, manufacturing, technology), and analyze the ways they support jobs and the economy
description_plain: Your child can name big industries in the U.S. and explain how they help people have jobs and support the economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify population and demographic statistics (e.g., gender, ethnic backgrounds, religions) of the United States, and analyze what the data describes about the country
description_plain: Your child can find information about the people living in the U.S., like their backgrounds and religions, and explain what this tells us about the country.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the population and industries in the United States today.
description_plain: Your child understands the people and industries in the United States today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the symbolism associated with the United States (e.g., Flag of the United States, National anthem, bald eagle, Great Seal of the United States, words of the Pledge of Allegiance, National motto), and explain the reasons those symbols are used and their meanings
description_plain: Your child can name U.S. symbols like the flag and bald eagle and explain what they mean and why they are important.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the differences between rules and laws
description_plain: Your child understands the difference between rules and laws.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the type of government the United States has and analyze the reasons we have a government
description_plain: Your child can name the type of government the U.S. has and explain why we need a government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify who state representatives are (e.g., governors, senators) and explain that they are elected positions, and that they represent the people of their states
description_plain: Your child can name state leaders like governors and senators, explain that people vote for them, and say how they represent their states.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the role of the President, Vice President, and other Cabinet leaders (e.g., Secretary of State, Secretary of the Treasury, Secretary of the Interior), and identify the current people who hold those positions
description_plain: Your child can explain what the President, Vice President, and Cabinet leaders do and name who holds these jobs now.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the location of the capital of the United States and the White House
description_plain: Your child can find the U.S. capital and the White House on a map.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze components of the United States government.
description_plain: Your child understands important parts of the U.S. government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways states and territories differ and the rights afforded to those born in each area (e.g., political rights, voting rights)
description_plain: Your child can explain how states and territories are different and what rights people have in each place.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the regions that make up the United States on a map including their states and territories
description_plain: Your child can find the different regions of the U.S. on a map, including their states and territories.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain what states and territories are and their locations within the United States.
description_plain: Your child understands what states and territories are and where they are in the U.S.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify land bordering countries to the United States (e.g., Canada, Mexico), and explain relationships the U.S. has with them (e.g., trade, ways leaders work together, sharing resources)
description_plain: Your child can name countries that border the U.S. and explain how the U.S. works with them, like trading and sharing resources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major geographical features of North America (e.g., climate zones, bodies of water, mountains, deserts)
description_plain: Your child can name big land features in North America like mountains, deserts, and bodies of water.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain features of a map (e.g., cardinal directions, key/legends, map scales, latitude and longitude)
description_plain: Your child can explain parts of a map like directions, keys, scales, and lines of latitude and longitude.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the geography of the United States and its neighbors.
description_plain: Your child understands the geography of the U.S. and nearby countries.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how the work of these social scientists establishes knowledge of human development throughout time and peoples' relationships with each other and the environment
description_plain: Your child understands how social scientists learn about people’s history and how humans relate to each other and the environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze ways that oral traditions inform what we know about the past
description_plain: Your child understands how stories passed down by word of mouth help us learn about the past.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways anthropologists work with people today to learn about their cultures
description_plain: Your child understands how anthropologists work with people today to learn about their cultures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain approaches historians take to analyze and interpret the past using primary and secondary sources
description_plain: Your child understands how historians study old documents and other sources to learn about history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways that archeologists study the physical evidence left behind by humans to understand human culture
description_plain: Your child understands how archaeologists study objects left by people to learn about their cultures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how geographers use tools to understand the features of the globe and the inhabitants of the Earth
description_plain: Your child understands how geographers use tools to study the Earth and its people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the ways social scientists piece together information to have knowledge of history and the world today.
description_plain: Your child understands how social scientists gather clues and information to learn about history and the world today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how different economic systems can create more equitable outcomes for individuals and communities, particularly for those traditionally marginalized from the economic system.
description_plain: Your child can explain how different ways of running economies can help make life fairer for everyone, especially for people who have been treated unfairly before.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how inequities within the economic system have been addressed or sustained by the actions of those traditionally privileged and marginalized.
description_plain: Your child can look at how unfairness in the economy has been kept or changed by people with more or less privilege.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how those traditionally privileged and marginalized across intersecting identities can influence and interact with economic systems.
description_plain: Your child understands how people with different backgrounds and identities can affect and be affected by the economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the ways that different political systems utilize economic systems to organize and distribute goods and services to individuals and communities.
description_plain: Your child can name how different governments use economic systems to share goods and services with people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue whether the costs and benefits of an aspect of the means of production equitably serve all individuals and communities.
description_plain: Your child can explain if the costs and benefits of how things are made are fair to all people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and communities acting through intersectional identities and lived experiences can affect the means of production.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people's identities and experiences affect how things are made and produced.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain who has the power to make decisions related to the means of production and the effects those decisions have on individuals and communities
description_plain: Your child understands who makes decisions about producing goods and services and how those decisions affect people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the individuals and communities involved in the production of any good or service, the materials needed for producing them, where and how the materials are obtained, and the various interrelationships among all of these elements.
description_plain: Your child can name the people and communities involved in making goods or services, the materials used, where they come from, and how all these parts connect.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how a resource can be used differently to create a more equitable outcome for individuals and communities including how individuals and communities can influence systems of power to achieve that change.
description_plain: Your child can explain how using a resource differently can help make things fairer for people and how people can work to change systems of power.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how decisions affecting access to goods and services are influenced by systems of power and cultural norms including how these effects of decisions create more equitable or inequitable outcomes.
description_plain: Your child can explain how decisions about who gets goods and services are influenced by power and culture, and how these decisions can be fair or unfair.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how scarcity affects the cost and availability of desired goods and services, and who has the power to influence the factors related to cost and availability and why.
description_plain: Your child understands how limited resources affect the price and availability of goods and services, and who controls these factors and why.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the choices communities make about how to use resources based on the scarcity of that resource, including those that are familiar and unfamiliar.
description_plain: Your child can explain how communities decide to use resources when they are limited, including resources they know well and those they don't.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the systematic analysis of the spatial patterns provides an integral understanding of a place or region and supports equitable decisions about climate and land use.
description_plain: Your child can explain how studying maps and spatial patterns helps us understand places and make fair choices about climate and land use.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple types of maps, charts, and graphs and how they are used to interpret topographical information, draw inferences about the development of societies, and determine how places shape events and how places may be changed by events.
description_plain: Your child can use different maps, charts, and graphs to understand land shapes, how societies grow, and how places and events affect each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the characteristics and elements of maps, globes, geographic tools, and other technologies are used and selected to identify and describe local, regional and/or global locations.
description_plain: Your child understands how to use maps, globes, and tools to find and describe places nearby or far away.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify maps, globes, and other geographic tools and technologies that are used to describe where places are located both absolutely and relatively across time, space, and distance.
description_plain: Your child can identify maps, globes, and tools that show where places are located in different ways and times.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how decisions about resources and the environment made by individuals and/or communities impact current and future peoples differently and how those decisions might be made more equitable.
description_plain: Your child can explain how choices about resources and the environment affect people now and in the future, and how to make those choices fairer.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and societies at local, regional and/or global levels influence political, economic, and social decision-making.
description_plain: Your child understands how people and societies at different levels influence political, economic, and social decisions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how humans and their societies and institutions affect, modify and/or preserve the environment, as well as how the modifications of the physical environment affect physical, behavioral, and diverse cultural systems.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people and their communities change or protect the environment, and how these changes affect cultures and behaviors.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics of human systems, physical systems, and the environment, and ways they interact at local, regional and/or global levels.
description_plain: Your child can name features of human and physical systems and the environment, and how they interact locally and globally.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the relationship between populations and physical systems influence decision-making about the equitable access to resources and land at the local, regional, and/or global levels.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the relationship between people and the environment affects fair access to resources and land in different places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how human systems and the distribution of populations interact with and impact physical systems, and how conflict and access to resources influence physical systems.
description_plain: Your child understands how people and where they live affect the environment, and how conflicts and resource access change physical systems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how and why a population's characteristics, including their spatial distribution, growth, and movement, have divided, organized, and unified areas of Earth's surface and impacted both human and physical systems.
description_plain: Your child can explain how population features like size, growth, and movement shape areas on Earth and affect both people and the environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics of populations based on their size, place, region, and cultural demographics, as well as identifying patterns of migration.
description_plain: Your child can identify population traits like size, location, culture, and migration patterns.

subject: Social Studies
