description_plain: Your child will explain the complaints and important ideas written in the Declaration of Independence.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how liberty and natural rights are defined in the Declaration of Independence
description_plain: Your child understands how the Declaration of Independence defines freedom and natural rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the ideals of the Declaration of Independence.
description_plain: Your child will explain the main ideas found in the Declaration of Independence.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze protests and major events leading up to the American Revolution (e.g., Boston Massacre, Gaspee Affair, Stamp Act riots, North Carolina Regulators, various Tea Parties), and argue how these led to the start of Revolution
description_plain: Your child can describe key protests and events before the American Revolution and explain how they helped start the war.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the Proclamation of 1763 and other acts of the British government meant to recoup financial costs of the French and Indian War (e.g., Sugar Act 1764, Currency Act 1764, Stamp Act 1765, Townshend Act 1767) and assert control over the colonies (e.g., Quartering Act 1765, Coercive Acts 1774) and argue how these led to the start of the Revolution
description_plain: Your child will explain British laws after the French and Indian War and how these laws upset the colonies and led to the Revolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the roles and contributions Indigenous peoples had in the French and Indian War
description_plain: Your child understands the important roles Indigenous peoples played in the French and Indian War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the causes and course of the French and Indian War, and argue how it impacted the colonies
description_plain: Your child can explain what caused the French and Indian War, what happened during it, and how it affected the colonies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how Britain's response at the end of the French and Indian War led to the American Revolution.
description_plain: Your child will explain how Britain's actions after the French and Indian War helped cause the American Revolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how race was socially constructed during this time, and argue the ways it can be traced to how race is socially constructed today
description_plain: Your child can explain how ideas about race were created during this time and how those ideas still affect us today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the significant roles free Blacks had in the colonies (e.g., Anthony Johnson, John Quamino, Bristol Yamma), and explain their contributions
description_plain: Your child will name important free Black people in the colonies and explain what they did.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the conditions of slavery in colonial Rhode Island (e.g., first enslaved African in Rhode Island 1696 from ship Seaflower, role of Rhode Islanders in the slave trade, rural v. urban slavery, 1652 law limiting terms of servitude)
description_plain: Your child understands what slavery was like in colonial Rhode Island, including laws and daily life.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze laws and codes controlling enslaved Africans, free Blacks, indentured whites, and Indigenous peoples in the 1600-1700s and argue who benefited
description_plain: Your child can explain the laws that controlled enslaved Africans, free Blacks, indentured whites, and Indigenous peoples, and who these laws helped.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the role the system of slavery had in the economic development of the colonies, and argue who benefited
description_plain: Your child will explain how slavery helped the colonies' economy and who gained from it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the origins and course of African enslavement in the colonies and the development of chattel slavery, and argue who benefited from this new form of slavery
description_plain: Your child understands how African enslavement started and grew in the colonies and who benefited from this system.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the rationale local colonists used to enslave Indigenous peoples (e.g., enslavement of Pequot, Narragansett, and Wampanoag after the Pequot War 1637 and King Philip's War 1676, connection to African enslavement), and explain the conditions of enslavement
description_plain: Your child can explain why colonists enslaved Indigenous peoples and what their living conditions were like.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impacts the new system of enslavement had on the economy and the people involved.
description_plain: Your child will explain how the new system of slavery affected the economy and the people involved.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the conflicts between Indigenous peoples and European colonists and their outcomes (e.g., Aztec Conquest 1520s, Pueblo Revolt 1680, Pequot War 1630s, King Philip's War 1670s, California Missions 1760s-1830s)
description_plain: Your child can describe conflicts between Indigenous peoples and European colonists and what happened as a result.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the similarities and differences between political, cultural, and trade relationships between French, Spanish, and English colonists with Indigenous communities
description_plain: Your child will compare how French, Spanish, and English colonists worked with Indigenous communities in politics, culture, and trade.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the differences between Indigenous and European colonists' views on land ownership, land use, and property rights
description_plain: Your child understands how Indigenous peoples and European colonists had different ideas about land ownership and use.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the relationships between Indigenous peoples and European colonists and the evolving nature of those relationships.
description_plain: Your child will explain the changing relationships between Indigenous peoples and European colonists.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the similarities and differences in the composition and ideologies of each colony including population and reason for immigration, governmental structures, economic systems, sources of labor, lifeways, religion, and relationships with Indigenous communities
description_plain: Your child can compare the colonies by looking at who lived there, why people moved there, how governments worked, and how people lived.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the locations and geography of the colonies across North America (New England, Middle Colonies, Southern Colonies, New France, New Spain), and analyze the reasons colonists decided to settle in those locations
description_plain: Your child will locate the colonies on a map and explain why colonists chose to settle in those places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the similarities and differences of the colonies throughout North America.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the colonies in North America were alike and different.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the economic motivations of settler colonialism, and argue who benefited
description_plain: Your child will explain why settlers came to North America and who gained from their arrival.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the effects of the Columbian Exchange, and argue who benefited (e.g., introduction of invasive plant species to North America, introduction of animals such as pigs and horses, impact of diseases such as smallpox on Indigenous populations)
description_plain: Your child can describe the effects of the Columbian Exchange, like new plants, animals, and diseases, and who benefited.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the reasons colonial powers (e.g., Dutch, English, French, Portuguese, Russian, Spanish, Vikings) sailed to North America, their understanding of land ownership, and the initial responses of the Indigenous peoples they encountered, and argue the impacts of colonization
description_plain: Your child will explain why European countries came to North America, how they thought about land, how Indigenous peoples reacted, and what happened because of colonization.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the ways the motivations and methods of European colonization in North America affected the Indigenous peoples.
description_plain: Your child can explain how European colonization affected Indigenous peoples in different ways.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze ways that Indigenous peoples continue their government and traditional practices today
description_plain: Your child will describe how Indigenous peoples keep their governments and traditions alive today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the cooperation and conflict between Indigenous nations prior to European arrival
description_plain: Your child can explain how Indigenous nations worked together or had conflicts before Europeans arrived.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the cultures, government structures, and lived experiences (e.g., trade networks, kinship system, spiritual practices) of groups of Indigenous peoples across North America prior to the arrival of Europeans (e.g., Haudenosaunee, Huron, Cherokee, Navajo, Creek, Apache, Paiute, Pueblo, Hopi, Lakota (Sioux), Seminole, Taino, Inuit, Maya), including those local to Rhode Island (e.g., Nahaganset (Narragansett), Wampanoag (Pokanoket), Nehantick and Eastern Nehantick (Niantic), Pequot, Nipmuc, Massachuset, Mohegan, Manissean)
description_plain: Your child will learn about the cultures, governments, and daily lives of different Indigenous groups across North America before Europeans came.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the geographical spread of Indigenous communities and language families, and analyze the relationship between geography and location of settlements (e.g., access to resources, climate)
description_plain: Your child can explain where Indigenous communities lived, what languages they spoke, and how geography affected where they settled.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the lived experiences of Indigenous peoples prior to European colonization.
description_plain: Your child will learn about the lives of Indigenous peoples before Europeans arrived.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the locations people settled, and analyze the reasons people chose those areas over others (e.g., access to resources, fertile land)
description_plain: Your child can name where people settled and explain why they chose those places, like for good land or resources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the countries that make up North America today and their locations on a map
description_plain: Your child will identify the countries in North America today and find them on a map.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the major geographical features of North America (e.g., climate zones, bodies of water, mountains, deserts), and analyze their relationships with human settlement
description_plain: Your child can name major land features in North America and explain how they affected where people lived.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the countries, geographic features, and climates of North America.
description_plain: Your child can describe the countries, land features, and weather in North America.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how different economic systems can create more equitable outcomes for individuals and communities, particularly for those traditionally marginalized from the economic system.
description_plain: Your child can explain how different ways of running economies can help make life fairer for everyone, especially for people who have been treated unfairly before.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how inequities within the economic system have been addressed or sustained by the actions of those traditionally privileged and marginalized.
description_plain: Your child can look at how unfairness in the economy has been kept or changed by people with more or less privilege.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how those traditionally privileged and marginalized across intersecting identities can influence and interact with economic systems.
description_plain: Your child understands how people with different backgrounds and identities can affect and be affected by the economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the ways that different political systems utilize economic systems to organize and distribute goods and services to individuals and communities.
description_plain: Your child can name how different governments use economic systems to share goods and services with people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue whether the costs and benefits of an aspect of the means of production equitably serve all individuals and communities.
description_plain: Your child can explain if the costs and benefits of how things are made are fair to all people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and communities acting through intersectional identities and lived experiences can affect the means of production.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people's identities and experiences affect how things are made and produced.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain who has the power to make decisions related to the means of production and the effects those decisions have on individuals and communities
description_plain: Your child understands who makes decisions about producing goods and services and how those decisions affect people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the individuals and communities involved in the production of any good or service, the materials needed for producing them, where and how the materials are obtained, and the various interrelationships among all of these elements.
description_plain: Your child can name the people and communities involved in making goods or services, the materials used, where they come from, and how all these parts connect.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how a resource can be used differently to create a more equitable outcome for individuals and communities including how individuals and communities can influence systems of power to achieve that change.
description_plain: Your child can explain how using a resource differently can help make things fairer for people and how people can work to change systems of power.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how decisions affecting access to goods and services are influenced by systems of power and cultural norms including how these effects of decisions create more equitable or inequitable outcomes.
description_plain: Your child can explain how decisions about who gets goods and services are influenced by power and culture, and how these decisions can be fair or unfair.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how scarcity affects the cost and availability of desired goods and services, and who has the power to influence the factors related to cost and availability and why.
description_plain: Your child understands how limited resources affect the price and availability of goods and services, and who controls these factors and why.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the choices communities make about how to use resources based on the scarcity of that resource, including those that are familiar and unfamiliar.
description_plain: Your child can explain how communities decide to use resources when they are limited, including resources they know well and those they don't.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the systematic analysis of the spatial patterns provides an integral understanding of a place or region and supports equitable decisions about climate and land use.
description_plain: Your child can explain how studying maps and spatial patterns helps us understand places and make fair choices about climate and land use.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple types of maps, charts, and graphs and how they are used to interpret topographical information, draw inferences about the development of societies, and determine how places shape events and how places may be changed by events.
description_plain: Your child can use different maps, charts, and graphs to understand land shapes, how societies grow, and how places and events affect each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the characteristics and elements of maps, globes, geographic tools, and other technologies are used and selected to identify and describe local, regional and/or global locations.
description_plain: Your child understands how to use maps, globes, and tools to find and describe places nearby or far away.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify maps, globes, and other geographic tools and technologies that are used to describe where places are located both absolutely and relatively across time, space, and distance.
description_plain: Your child can identify maps, globes, and tools that show where places are located in different ways and times.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how decisions about resources and the environment made by individuals and/or communities impact current and future peoples differently and how those decisions might be made more equitable.
description_plain: Your child can explain how choices about resources and the environment affect people now and in the future, and how to make those choices fairer.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and societies at local, regional and/or global levels influence political, economic, and social decision-making.
description_plain: Your child understands how people and societies at different levels influence political, economic, and social decisions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how humans and their societies and institutions affect, modify and/or preserve the environment, as well as how the modifications of the physical environment affect physical, behavioral, and diverse cultural systems.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people and their communities change or protect the environment, and how these changes affect cultures and behaviors.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics of human systems, physical systems, and the environment, and ways they interact at local, regional and/or global levels.
description_plain: Your child can name features of human and physical systems and the environment, and how they interact locally and globally.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the relationship between populations and physical systems influence decision-making about the equitable access to resources and land at the local, regional, and/or global levels.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the relationship between people and the environment affects fair access to resources and land in different places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how human systems and the distribution of populations interact with and impact physical systems, and how conflict and access to resources influence physical systems.
description_plain: Your child understands how people and where they live affect the environment, and how conflicts and resource access change physical systems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how and why a population's characteristics, including their spatial distribution, growth, and movement, have divided, organized, and unified areas of Earth's surface and impacted both human and physical systems.
description_plain: Your child can explain how population features like size, growth, and movement shape areas on Earth and affect both people and the environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics of populations based on their size, place, region, and cultural demographics, as well as identifying patterns of migration.
description_plain: Your child can identify population traits like size, location, culture, and migration patterns.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how all individuals can act as local, national, and/or global agents of social change by using lessons learned from history.
description_plain: Your child understands how anyone can work for social change locally, nationally, or globally by learning from history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze historical change through the intersectional identities and lived experiences of people who have accomplished social change throughout history in relation to systems of power, identity, and resistance.
description_plain: Your child can study how people with different identities and experiences have made social change in history, especially related to power and resistance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how historical and social change have been and continue to be accomplished in relation to systems of power, identity, and resistance.
description_plain: Your child understands how social and historical changes happen through power, identity, and resistance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify peoples, events, technologies, and ideas involved in historical and social change in various geographical and temporal locations.
description_plain: Your child can name important people, events, ideas, and inventions involved in social and historical change in different places and times.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue, using multiple narratives rooted in identity, power, and resistance, how history itself is an interpretation of events.
description_plain: Your child can explain how history is told in different ways based on identity, power, and resistance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple types of sources, including art, music, oral histories, pamphlets, film, texts, etc., through a critical reflection of the creators' and students' intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can look at different sources like art, music, and stories and think about how the creator's and their own identities affect how they understand history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the purpose, audience, and perspective of multiple types of sources (art, music, oral histories, pamphlets, film, texts, etc.) relating to a historical event or series of events, individual, or group of people, including indications of bias toward or against the subject portrayed.
description_plain: Your child understands the purpose, audience, and viewpoint of different sources about history, including recognizing bias.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify key people, central ideas, and the mechanisms by which stories are told and retold regarding an event or series of events, centering the voices of historical actors and groups engaged in resistance and change.
description_plain: Your child can identify key people and ideas in stories about events, focusing on voices of those who resisted and made change.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how social change, intersectional identities, and lived experiences are crucial to the study and practice of history.
description_plain: Your child understands why social change, identities, and experiences are important to studying and practicing history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple sources to compare and contrast historical events through the lenses of identity, power, and resistance.
description_plain: Your child can compare historical events by looking at identity, power, and resistance using different sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain multiple causes and effects of historical events, centering and representing the voices and experiences of individuals and communities who were agents of change and resistance.
description_plain: Your child can explain many causes and effects of historical events, focusing on people and groups who made change and resisted.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify historical events that are culturally relevant to global, national, and local histories and connect to students' intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can name historical events that connect to cultures and histories around the world and relate to their own identities and experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue for a possible solution to make rights equitable and the roles of those involved in pursuing that solution.
description_plain: Your child can explain a fair solution to make rights equal for everyone and describe who helps make that happen.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and communities have been included or excluded from the political process based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences and the impact these actions have had on their rights, responsibilities, and the functioning of a democratic society.
description_plain: Your child understands how some people or groups have been included or left out of politics because of who they are and how this affects their rights and duties.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain different ways communities and individuals inform themselves, exercise their rights and responsibilities, and engage formally and/or informally in political processes.
description_plain: Your child can explain different ways people learn about and take part in politics, both officially and in everyday life.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what rights and responsibilities individuals and communities have in a society and who can take advantage of them.
description_plain: Your child can name the rights and duties people and communities have and who can use them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how rules and laws can be used to create an equitable society.
description_plain: Your child can explain how rules and laws can help make society fair for everyone.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how rules and laws positively and/or negatively impact different individuals and communities based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child understands how rules and laws can help or hurt different people based on who they are and their experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why rules and laws exist, and how they are implemented by and for individuals and communities based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can explain why we have rules and laws and how they are made and used for different people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what rules and laws are, and who has the power to make them, in different settings and cultures that are familiar and unfamiliar to students.
description_plain: Your child can identify what rules and laws are and who makes them in different places and cultures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how power can be distributed and used to create a more equitable society for communities and individuals based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can explain how power can be shared and used to make society fairer for all people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the purpose of government and the use of power, including balancing order and freedom, to advance and control different communities and individuals based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child understands why governments exist and how they use power to keep order and freedom for different people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how political power is and has been obtained and used to govern communities and individuals with attention to their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people get and use political power to lead communities, considering who they are.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what political power is and who has political power in a society.
description_plain: Your child can explain what political power is and who holds it in society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the philosophy of Confucianism, analyze its spread across Asia, and argue its impact
description_plain: Your child understands what Confucianism teaches, how it spread across Asia, and why it was important.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the spread of Christianity across Africa, Europe, and Asia including the role of the Vikings, Charlemagne, the Roman Catholic Church, the Greek Orthodox Church, and the Crusades, and argue its political and cultural impacts on societies
description_plain: Your child can explain how Christianity spread in Africa, Europe, and Asia, including the roles of groups like the Vikings and the Crusades, and how it affected politics and culture.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the decentralization and spread of Islam in places such as Cordoba in Spain, Ghana in West Africa, and Baghdad in Central Asia, and argue the impacts of its growing influence and division
description_plain: Your child understands how Islam spread to places like Spain, West Africa, and Central Asia, and how its growth and divisions affected those areas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impact of the expansion of religion across Afro-Eurasian regions from 300-1300 CE.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the spread of religions between 300 and 1300 CE changed different regions in Africa, Europe, and Asia.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the Black Death, and argue how its impacts were felt across Africa, Europe, and Asia
description_plain: Your child understands what the Black Death was and how it affected people in Africa, Europe, and Asia.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the cause and course of the Tang Dynasty's expansion into Korea and Japan, and argue its impact on those cultures
description_plain: Your child can explain why the Tang Dynasty expanded into Korea and Japan and how this affected their cultures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the influence of trade on the development of early sub-Saharan African states and societies including the Kingdom of Axum, Swahili coastal societies, and Timbuktu in West Africa
description_plain: Your child understands how trade helped early African societies like Axum, Swahili cities, and Timbuktu grow and develop.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the reasons for the growth of commercial cities such as Alexandria and Cairo in Egypt, Quilon in India, Melaka in Malaysia, and Quanzhou in China (e.g., new maritime technologies and empire expansion), and argue the impact of their growth
description_plain: Your child can explain why cities like Alexandria, Cairo, Quilon, Melaka, and Quanzhou grew and how new technology and empires helped them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze agricultural changes across Africa, Europe, and Asia from 300-600 CE, and argue the impacts on people and population numbers
description_plain: Your child understands how farming changed in Africa, Europe, and Asia from 300 to 600 CE and how this affected people and population growth.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impact of the expansion of territorial influence across Afro-Eurasian regions from 300-1300 CE.
description_plain: Your child can explain how expanding territories between 300 and 1300 CE affected regions in Africa, Europe, and Asia.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impacts of Bantus of Sub-Saharan Africa, Teotihuacan of Mesoamerica, and Mayans in the Yucatan on society and culture
description_plain: Your child can explain how the Bantus, Teotihuacan, and Mayans influenced their societies and cultures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the history and growth of the Bantus of Sub-Saharan Africa, Teotihuacan of Mesoamerica, and Mayans in the Yucatan, and argue the role of political unity on their development
description_plain: Your child understands the history and growth of the Bantus, Teotihuacan, and Mayans and how political unity helped them develop.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the beliefs and religious practices of Bantus of Sub-Saharan Africa, Teotihuacanos of Mesoamerica, and Mayans in the Yucatan and their development, and analyze how they changed over time (e.g., central teachings, influence of the environment on teachings, social stratification within the religion, religious texts, religious observations)
description_plain: Your child can explain the beliefs and religious practices of the Bantus, Teotihuacanos, and Mayans and how these changed over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the influences of other belief systems throughout the ancient world on society and culture.
description_plain: Your child can explain how different ancient belief systems influenced societies and cultures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the ways Hinduism, Judaism, Buddhism, Christianity, and Islam continue to influence society and culture today
description_plain: Your child understands how Hinduism, Judaism, Buddhism, Christianity, and Islam still affect society and culture today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impacts of Hinduism, Judaism, Buddhism, Christianity, and Islam on the development of societies and cultures
description_plain: Your child can explain how Hinduism, Judaism, Buddhism, Christianity, and Islam shaped societies and cultures in the past.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the history of Hinduism, Judaism, Buddhism, Christianity, and Islam, how they spread, the role of empires on their development,
description_plain: Your child understands the history of the five major religions, how they spread, and how empires helped their growth.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the beliefs and religious practices of Hinduism, Judaism, Buddhism, Christianity, and Islam, their development, and how they changed over time (e.g., central teachings, influence of the environment on teachings, social stratification within the religion, religious texts, religious observations)
description_plain: Your child can explain the beliefs and practices of Hinduism, Judaism, Buddhism, Christianity, and Islam and how they changed over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the definition and characteristics of universalizing religion
description_plain: Your child understands what a universalizing religion is and what makes it different.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impacts of the five major world religions on the development of societies and cultures through time.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the five major world religions affected societies and cultures throughout history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the routes of Red Sea and Indian Ocean trade, analyze the role of the environment on the success or failure of trade, and argue the economic and cultural influences and importance of navigational technology to the trade
description_plain: Your child can identify trade routes in the Red Sea and Indian Ocean, explain how the environment affected trade, and why navigation technology was important.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the development and use of the Silk Road and the role of caravan cities, and argue the impacts of the economic, cultural, and religious changes it brought
description_plain: Your child understands how the Silk Road and caravan cities developed and how they changed economies, cultures, and religions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the use of roads and highways for communication, trade, and control within empires (e.g., roads in the Persian Empire, Roman Empire, and Qin and Han China), and argue their impacts
description_plain: Your child can explain how roads and highways helped communication, trade, and control in empires like Persia, Rome, and China.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impact of the influence of trade routes in connecting the Afro-Eurasian regions.
description_plain: Your child can explain how trade routes connected Africa, Europe, and Asia and why this was important.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the formation and course of these globalizing empires including administration and government, labor systems, economic systems, social systems and hierarchies, and relationship to the environment, and argue the impacts of these developments and who benefited in these societies
description_plain: Your child understands how global empires were organized, including their governments, labor, economies, social groups, and environment, and who benefited.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the locations of the empires of the Han Dynasty in China, the Roman Empire, and the Mauryan Empire on a map, and analyze the ways their geographic locations and the environment affected their expansion and influence
description_plain: Your child can find the Han, Roman, and Mauryan empires on a map and explain how their locations and environments helped them grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the definition of and conditions of a "globalizing empire"
description_plain: Your child understands what makes an empire a 'globalizing empire' and the conditions needed for this.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the reasons for the rise of the globalizing empires of the Afro-Eurasian world and compare their characteristics.
description_plain: Your child can explain why global empires rose in Africa, Europe, and Asia and compare how they were similar and different.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the intellectual changes across Africa, Europe, and Asia (e.g., Master Kong Fuzi or Confucius, Daoism, Siddhartha Gautama or the Buddha, Zoroastrianism, Greek philosophers and thinkers), and argue the impacts of those changes
description_plain: Your child understands important thinkers like Confucius, Buddha, and Greek philosophers and how their ideas changed Africa, Europe, and Asia.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the political and economic changes across Africa, Europe, and Asia (e.g., Shang Yang reforms in the Qin dynasty and new methods of military campaigns, development of polis in Greece or civitas in Rome, development of a system of money), and argue the impacts of those changes
description_plain: Your child can explain political and economic changes like new laws, military strategies, city development, and money systems and their effects.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze cultural and environmental changes across Africa, Europe, and Asia (e.g., Vedic system of hierarchy in Northern India, Northern China's agriculture revolution, city growth on the Ganges plain), and argue the impacts of those changes
description_plain: Your child understands cultural and environmental changes like social classes, farming improvements, and city growth and how they affected people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impacts of the cultural, intellectual, political, economic, and environmental changes happening across Africa, Europe, and Asia during this time period.
description_plain: Your child can explain how culture, ideas, politics, economy, and environment changed Africa, Europe, and Asia during this time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the similarities and differences of the complex urban-based societies in sub-Saharan Africa and the Americas, the lived experiences of the people of Nubia, the Nok in West Africa, the Chavín of the Andes, and the Olmecs of Mesoamerica
description_plain: Your child can compare complex societies in sub-Saharan Africa and the Americas and understand what life was like for people in places like Nubia, Nok, Chavín, and Olmec.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the interaction of the early empires with peoples on the margins such as the Greeks and the Phoenicians in the Mediterranean, and argue the impacts of those interactions
description_plain: Your child understands how early empires interacted with groups like the Greeks and Phoenicians and why these interactions mattered.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify methods of government developed in the early empires, analyze how and why they developed, and argue who benefited
description_plain: Your child can explain how early empires created governments, why they did it, and who it helped.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the similarities and differences in the methods of ruling an empire and the lived experiences of individuals in the Neo-Assyrian (911-612 BCE) and Persian (560-331 BCE) empires in West Asia (Middle East), Vedics (1500-600 BCE) in South Asia, and early Zhou Empire (1045-771 BCE) in East Asia
description_plain: Your child can compare how different empires like the Neo-Assyrian, Persian, Vedic, and Zhou ruled and what life was like for their people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impacts of climate change, migrations, new technologies, and administrative innovations in the rise of new empires
description_plain: Your child can explain how climate change, migrations, new technology, and government ideas helped new empires rise.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the characteristics of empires and analyze the ways empires differed from the river valley civilizations or territorial states
description_plain: Your child understands what makes an empire different from earlier civilizations or smaller states.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impacts of the establishment of major empires, smaller urban-based societies, and the ways they interacted with surrounding peoples.
description_plain: Your child can explain how major empires and smaller societies started and how they interacted with nearby peoples.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the location of small-scale political entities (e.g., microsocieties such as Austronesians, Mycenaeans, Minoans), analyze their cultural beliefs and social hierarchies, and argue how their role in trade impacted economies and technological advances
description_plain: Your child can find where small ancient groups lived, understand their beliefs and social ranks, and explain how their trade helped economies and new technologies grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the locations of territorial states (e.g., Middle Kingdom Egypt, Mesopotamia Kingship, Vedic peoples, Shang State), and analyze their ethnic identity, government, and techniques of coexistence with other territorial states, response to environmental changes, trade networks, labor systems, and religion
description_plain: Your child can locate early kingdoms, learn about their people, governments, how they got along with neighbors, handled the environment, traded, worked, and practiced religion.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the role of nomads in the restructuring of the political landscape (e.g., establishment of regional trade networks, technological and social innovations such as horses and chariots)
description_plain: Your child understands how nomads changed politics by creating trade routes and introducing new tools and ways of living, like using horses and chariots.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the interaction between nomads and their environments as well as the development of new states throughout Afro-Eurasia.
description_plain: Your child studies how nomads lived with their environment and how new states formed across Africa, Europe, and Asia.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the reasons for the decline of the river valley civilizations
description_plain: Your child can explain why early river valley civilizations eventually declined.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the role of trade and contact between early river valley civilizations
description_plain: Your child understands how trade and contact between early river valley civilizations helped them grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify early river cities on a map (e.g., Mesopotamia, Old Kingdom Egypt, Indus Valley, Yellow and Yangzi River basin), and analyze their government structures, leaders, laws, economic systems, religion and belief systems, social hierarchies, technological and social innovations, and relationship to the environment
description_plain: Your child can find early river cities on a map and learn about their governments, leaders, laws, economies, religions, social classes, inventions, and how they used their environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the environmental developments that accompanied the rise of urban river basin societies (e.g., reliable water source, irrigation, warmer weather with expanded growing seasons, richer soil for agriculture)
description_plain: Your child understands how changes like steady water supply, irrigation, warmer weather, and better soil helped early river societies grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the development of early cities, states, and empires, and the role of trade between the entities.
description_plain: Your child studies how early cities, states, and empires developed and how trade connected them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the influence of geography on the development of the social, economic, and political structure of Indigenous civilizations in the Americas
description_plain: Your child learns how geography affected the social, economic, and political life of Indigenous civilizations in the Americas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the significance of the Fertile Crescent, identify its location, and analyze the role of natural resources in its development
description_plain: Your child can find the Fertile Crescent on a map, understand why it was important, and how natural resources helped it grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the interaction between and spread of complex societies (e.g., migration, trade, agriculture, exchange of culture and/or language, religious conversion, colonization)
description_plain: Your child studies how complex societies interacted through migration, trade, farming, sharing culture and language, religion, and colonization.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the impacts of the development of agriculture and herding on populations
description_plain: Your child understands how farming and herding changed populations and ways of life.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics of a complex society (e.g., economy that produces food surplus and ability to store food surplus, specialized jobs, systems for government, religion, art, technology) and analyze how those characteristics influenced social life
description_plain: Your child can identify features of complex societies like food surplus, specialized jobs, government, religion, art, and technology, and explain how these affected daily life.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the development of complex societies, their characteristics, and interactions with one another.
description_plain: Your child studies how complex societies developed, what made them unique, and how they interacted with each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the location of early hunter gatherer societies in the Americas, and analyze the roles of geography and environment on their settlement and development
description_plain: Your child can find where early hunter-gatherer groups lived in the Americas and understand how geography and environment influenced their lives.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the migration of people from Asia to the Americas
description_plain: Your child can explain how people moved from Asia to the Americas long ago.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze cultural characteristics of early societies around the world (e.g., language, art, technology, social structure)
description_plain: Your child learns about early societies around the world by studying their language, art, technology, and social groups.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain lifeways of early societies (e.g., Hunter-Gatherer, Nomadic, permanent civilization), and identify their locations around the world
description_plain: Your child understands how early people lived—whether as hunter-gatherers, nomads, or settled civilizations—and where they lived around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the lifeways and characteristics of early world societies and their expansion into the Americas.
description_plain: Your child studies early world societies, how they lived, and how they spread into the Americas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics of early Homo sapiens (e.g., art, language, religion), and explain how these characteristics lead to our understanding of how they lived and how culture developed
description_plain: Your child can identify traits of early humans like art, language, and religion, and explain how these show how they lived and developed culture.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain reasons that human ancestor lines transformed and replaced other hominid lines
description_plain: Your child understands why some human ancestors changed over time and replaced others.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the differences between Homo habilis, Homo erectus, Neanderthals, and Homo sapiens
description_plain: Your child can explain the differences between early human species like Homo habilis, Homo erectus, Neanderthals, and Homo sapiens.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the reasons human groups moved around the globe and identify the locations of those movements
description_plain: Your child studies why early humans moved around the world and where they went.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify significant archeological sites and their locations, the techniques used to obtain those findings, and analyze the types of evidence found of human ancestors and their evolution
description_plain: Your child can name important archaeological sites, how scientists found them, and what they tell us about human ancestors and their evolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain environmental changes that shaped the earth, and analyze how they allowed for the growth and development of human life
description_plain: Your child understands how changes in the environment shaped the Earth and helped humans grow and develop.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major geographical features around the world (e.g., climate zones, bodies of water, mountains, deserts)
description_plain: Your child can identify major world features like climate zones, oceans, mountains, and deserts.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the development of early humans and the characteristics of early human societies.
description_plain: Your child studies how early humans developed and what their societies were like.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify linear and comparative timelines, and explain how to read and use them
description_plain: Your child can read timelines that show events in order and compare different timelines.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify terms for historical periods (e.g., Paleolithic Era, Neolithic Era, Before Common Era, Common Era) and the time we are living in now
description_plain: Your child knows names for historical time periods like the Paleolithic and Neolithic Eras, and understands the current time period.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify ways to express the scale of time (e.g., decade, age, era, century, millennium, BCE, CE) and explain why times periods are named differently in different contexts
description_plain: Your child can explain different ways to measure time, like decades and centuries, and why time periods have different names.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the scale of time from today back to early humans.
description_plain: Your child understands how time is measured from today back to early humans.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the challenges in using primary and secondary sources (e.g., contextualization, corroboration, bias)
description_plain: Your child learns about challenges in using historical sources, like understanding context, checking facts, and recognizing bias.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the importance of using oral traditions when studying history
description_plain: Your child understands why oral stories are important for learning history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify types of secondary sources, and analyze ways that scholars use them to study the past
description_plain: Your child can identify different types of secondary sources and how historians use them to study the past.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify types of primary sources, and analyze ways that scholars use them to study the past
description_plain: Your child can identify different types of primary sources and how historians use them to learn about history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the sources scholars use to study the past.
description_plain: Your child studies the kinds of sources historians use to learn about the past.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze similarities and differences in the ways that geographers, archeologists, anthropologists, and historians work to identify primary sources and artifacts, analyze evidence, and construct interpretations of the past
description_plain: Your child can compare how geographers, archaeologists, anthropologists, and historians find and study artifacts and evidence to understand history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the approaches historians use to analyze and interpret the past using primary and secondary sources, and analyze the advantages and disadvantages of those approaches
description_plain: Your child understands how historians use different methods to study the past and the pros and cons of those methods.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways anthropologists work with people today to learn about their cultures and histories
description_plain: Your child learns how anthropologists work with people today to learn about their cultures and histories.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways archeologists study the physical evidence left behind by humans to understand human culture, and identify the tools that they use
description_plain: Your child can describe how archeologists use tools to study objects left by people to learn about their culture.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways geographers study the Earth, its land, features, and inhabitants to understand the relationship between humans and planet earth, and identify the tools that they use
description_plain: Your child can explain how geographers use tools to study the Earth’s land, features, and people to understand how humans and the planet are connected.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the jobs and necessary skills of people who study the past.
description_plain: Your child understands the different jobs and skills needed by people who study history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how different economic systems can create more equitable outcomes for individuals and communities, particularly for those traditionally marginalized from the economic system.
description_plain: Your child can explain how different ways of running economies can help make life fairer for everyone, especially for people who have been treated unfairly before.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how inequities within the economic system have been addressed or sustained by the actions of those traditionally privileged and marginalized.
description_plain: Your child can look at how unfairness in the economy has been kept or changed by people with more or less privilege.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how those traditionally privileged and marginalized across intersecting identities can influence and interact with economic systems.
description_plain: Your child understands how people with different backgrounds and identities can affect and be affected by the economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the ways that different political systems utilize economic systems to organize and distribute goods and services to individuals and communities.
description_plain: Your child can name how different governments use economic systems to share goods and services with people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue whether the costs and benefits of an aspect of the means of production equitably serve all individuals and communities.
description_plain: Your child can explain if the costs and benefits of how things are made are fair to all people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and communities acting through intersectional identities and lived experiences can affect the means of production.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people's identities and experiences affect how things are made and produced.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain who has the power to make decisions related to the means of production and the effects those decisions have on individuals and communities
description_plain: Your child understands who makes decisions about producing goods and services and how those decisions affect people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the individuals and communities involved in the production of any good or service, the materials needed for producing them, where and how the materials are obtained, and the various interrelationships among all of these elements.
description_plain: Your child can name the people and communities involved in making goods or services, the materials used, where they come from, and how all these parts connect.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how a resource can be used differently to create a more equitable outcome for individuals and communities including how individuals and communities can influence systems of power to achieve that change.
description_plain: Your child can explain how using a resource differently can help make things fairer for people and how people can work to change systems of power.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how decisions affecting access to goods and services are influenced by systems of power and cultural norms including how these effects of decisions create more equitable or inequitable outcomes.
description_plain: Your child can explain how decisions about who gets goods and services are influenced by power and culture, and how these decisions can be fair or unfair.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how scarcity affects the cost and availability of desired goods and services, and who has the power to influence the factors related to cost and availability and why.
description_plain: Your child understands how limited resources affect the price and availability of goods and services, and who controls these factors and why.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the choices communities make about how to use resources based on the scarcity of that resource, including those that are familiar and unfamiliar.
description_plain: Your child can explain how communities decide to use resources when they are limited, including resources they know well and those they don't.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the systematic analysis of the spatial patterns provides an integral understanding of a place or region and supports equitable decisions about climate and land use.
description_plain: Your child can explain how studying maps and spatial patterns helps us understand places and make fair choices about climate and land use.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple types of maps, charts, and graphs and how they are used to interpret topographical information, draw inferences about the development of societies, and determine how places shape events and how places may be changed by events.
description_plain: Your child can use different maps, charts, and graphs to understand land shapes, how societies grow, and how places and events affect each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the characteristics and elements of maps, globes, geographic tools, and other technologies are used and selected to identify and describe local, regional and/or global locations.
description_plain: Your child understands how to use maps, globes, and tools to find and describe places nearby or far away.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify maps, globes, and other geographic tools and technologies that are used to describe where places are located both absolutely and relatively across time, space, and distance.
description_plain: Your child can identify maps, globes, and tools that show where places are located in different ways and times.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how decisions about resources and the environment made by individuals and/or communities impact current and future peoples differently and how those decisions might be made more equitable.
description_plain: Your child can explain how choices about resources and the environment affect people now and in the future, and how to make those choices fairer.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and societies at local, regional and/or global levels influence political, economic, and social decision-making.
description_plain: Your child understands how people and societies at different levels influence political, economic, and social decisions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how humans and their societies and institutions affect, modify and/or preserve the environment, as well as how the modifications of the physical environment affect physical, behavioral, and diverse cultural systems.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people and their communities change or protect the environment, and how these changes affect cultures and behaviors.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics of human systems, physical systems, and the environment, and ways they interact at local, regional and/or global levels.
description_plain: Your child can name features of human and physical systems and the environment, and how they interact locally and globally.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the relationship between populations and physical systems influence decision-making about the equitable access to resources and land at the local, regional, and/or global levels.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the relationship between people and the environment affects fair access to resources and land in different places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how human systems and the distribution of populations interact with and impact physical systems, and how conflict and access to resources influence physical systems.
description_plain: Your child understands how people and where they live affect the environment, and how conflicts and resource access change physical systems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how and why a population's characteristics, including their spatial distribution, growth, and movement, have divided, organized, and unified areas of Earth's surface and impacted both human and physical systems.
description_plain: Your child can explain how population features like size, growth, and movement shape areas on Earth and affect both people and the environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics of populations based on their size, place, region, and cultural demographics, as well as identifying patterns of migration.
description_plain: Your child can identify population traits like size, location, culture, and migration patterns.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how all individuals can act as local, national, and/or global agents of social change by using lessons learned from history.
description_plain: Your child understands how anyone can work for social change locally, nationally, or globally by learning from history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze historical change through the intersectional identities and lived experiences of people who have accomplished social change throughout history in relation to systems of power, identity, and resistance.
description_plain: Your child can study how people with different identities and experiences have made social change in history, especially related to power and resistance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how historical and social change have been and continue to be accomplished in relation to systems of power, identity, and resistance.
description_plain: Your child understands how social and historical changes happen through power, identity, and resistance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify peoples, events, technologies, and ideas involved in historical and social change in various geographical and temporal locations.
description_plain: Your child can name important people, events, ideas, and inventions involved in social and historical change in different places and times.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue, using multiple narratives rooted in identity, power, and resistance, how history itself is an interpretation of events.
description_plain: Your child can explain how history is told in different ways based on identity, power, and resistance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple types of sources, including art, music, oral histories, pamphlets, film, texts, etc., through a critical reflection of the creators' and students' intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can look at different sources like art, music, and stories and think about how the creator's and their own identities affect how they understand history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the purpose, audience, and perspective of multiple types of sources (art, music, oral histories, pamphlets, film, texts, etc.) relating to a historical event or series of events, individual, or group of people, including indications of bias toward or against the subject portrayed.
description_plain: Your child understands the purpose, audience, and viewpoint of different sources about history, including recognizing bias.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify key people, central ideas, and the mechanisms by which stories are told and retold regarding an event or series of events, centering the voices of historical actors and groups engaged in resistance and change.
description_plain: Your child can identify key people and ideas in stories about events, focusing on voices of those who resisted and made change.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how social change, intersectional identities, and lived experiences are crucial to the study and practice of history.
description_plain: Your child understands why social change, identities, and experiences are important to studying and practicing history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple sources to compare and contrast historical events through the lenses of identity, power, and resistance.
description_plain: Your child can compare historical events by looking at identity, power, and resistance using different sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain multiple causes and effects of historical events, centering and representing the voices and experiences of individuals and communities who were agents of change and resistance.
description_plain: Your child can explain many causes and effects of historical events, focusing on people and groups who made change and resisted.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify historical events that are culturally relevant to global, national, and local histories and connect to students' intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can name historical events that connect to cultures and histories around the world and relate to their own identities and experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue for a possible solution to make rights equitable and the roles of those involved in pursuing that solution.
description_plain: Your child can explain a fair solution to make rights equal for everyone and describe who helps make that happen.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and communities have been included or excluded from the political process based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences and the impact these actions have had on their rights, responsibilities, and the functioning of a democratic society.
description_plain: Your child understands how some people or groups have been included or left out of politics because of who they are and how this affects their rights and duties.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain different ways communities and individuals inform themselves, exercise their rights and responsibilities, and engage formally and/or informally in political processes.
description_plain: Your child can explain different ways people learn about and take part in politics, both officially and in everyday life.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what rights and responsibilities individuals and communities have in a society and who can take advantage of them.
description_plain: Your child can name the rights and duties people and communities have and who can use them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how rules and laws can be used to create an equitable society.
description_plain: Your child can explain how rules and laws can help make society fair for everyone.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how rules and laws positively and/or negatively impact different individuals and communities based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child understands how rules and laws can help or hurt different people based on who they are and their experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why rules and laws exist, and how they are implemented by and for individuals and communities based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can explain why we have rules and laws and how they are made and used for different people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what rules and laws are, and who has the power to make them, in different settings and cultures that are familiar and unfamiliar to students.
description_plain: Your child can identify what rules and laws are and who makes them in different places and cultures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how power can be distributed and used to create a more equitable society for communities and individuals based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can explain how power can be shared and used to make society fairer for all people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the purpose of government and the use of power, including balancing order and freedom, to advance and control different communities and individuals based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child understands why governments exist and how they use power to keep order and freedom for different people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how political power is and has been obtained and used to govern communities and individuals with attention to their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people get and use political power to lead communities, considering who they are.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what political power is and who has political power in a society.
description_plain: Your child can explain what political power is and who holds it in society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impacts of globalization on the environment (e.g., the Green Revolution, increased consumption of natural resources, rise in pollution, and climate change)
description_plain: Your child can explain how globalization affects the environment, like changes in farming, using more natural resources, pollution, and climate change.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the problems created by globalization for individuals (e.g., child labor, low-wages and poor working conditions, and exploitation of workers) exemplified by the Maquiladoras, and argue the impacts on society
description_plain: Your child can explain problems globalization causes for workers, such as child labor, low pay, bad working conditions, and how these affect society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the creation of global networks and international cooperations (e.g., North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA), transnational issues, G-8 and G-20 summits, Amnesty International, Greenpeace, nongovernmental organizations (NGOs), criminal and terrorist organizations), and analyze how they serve the international population
description_plain: Your child can explain how global groups and agreements are created and how they help people around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the increased migration to urban spaces, spread of television shows, films, and music, and the popularization of international sports such as football and FIFA, and argue their impacts on society
description_plain: Your child can explain how more people moving to cities, sharing TV shows, movies, music, and sports affects society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the definition of globalization as the convergence of people, resources, and ideas around a common version of modernity
description_plain: Your child understands globalization means people, resources, and ideas coming together to create a shared modern way of life.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impacts of globalization on people and the environment.
description_plain: Your child can explain how globalization affects both people and the environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the incomplete independence of South Africa due to the presence of a large population of white settler colonialism, the institution of apartheid, and the role of Nelson Mandela and the African National Congress, and argue the impact of the movement
description_plain: Your child can explain how South Africa’s independence was limited by white settlers and apartheid, and how leaders like Nelson Mandela helped change that.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the negotiated independence of India and Pakistan from Britain and African countries from the British, French, Portuguese, Spanish, Belgians, Italians, and Germany, and argue their impact on the Indian and Pakistani peoples
description_plain: Your child can explain how India, Pakistan, and some African countries gained independence from European powers and how this affected their people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the Algerian war for independence from France including the role of the European settler population colons, and argue its impact on Algerians
description_plain: Your child can explain the war Algeria fought to become independent from France and how it affected Algerian people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the Chinese civil war between Nationalists led by Chiang Kai-shek and Communists led by Mao Zedong, and argue its impact on the Chinese people
description_plain: Your child can explain the Chinese civil war between two groups and how it affected the people of China.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the similarities and differences among the four types of decolonization - civil wars, wars of independence, negotiated independence, and incomplete decolonization
description_plain: Your child can compare four ways countries became independent: civil wars, wars of independence, negotiated independence, and incomplete independence.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the global societal and cultural impacts of the four types of decolonization.
description_plain: Your child can explain how different types of independence movements affected societies and cultures around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the Malta Summit (1989) and the fall of the Soviet Union (1991), and argue the impact of those events
description_plain: Your child can explain what happened at the Malta Summit and the fall of the Soviet Union and why these events mattered.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the role of new worldwide organizations and ideals (e.g., the United Nations, the International Monetary Fund, the Universal Declaration of Human Rights), and argue their impact on societies
description_plain: Your child can explain how new global organizations like the United Nations affect countries and people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify other Cold War "hot spots" include Algeria, Afghanistan, Cambodia, Angola, Nicaragua, Guatemala, the Congo, Iran, Hungary, and Cuba, and argue the impact of their participation on civilian populations
description_plain: Your child can identify places where Cold War conflicts happened and explain how these fights affected civilians.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the cause, course, and consequence of the Vietnam War (1955-1975) (e.g., Ho Chi Minh's, "Declaration of Independence of the Democratic Republic of Vietnam," 1954 Geneva peace accords, Gulf of Tonkin Resolution, Operation Rolling Thunder, invasion of Cambodia and Laos, Tet Offensive, My Lai massacre, evacuation of Saigon), and argue its impacts on the Vietnamese people
description_plain: Your child can explain the causes, events, and effects of the Vietnam War and how it impacted Vietnamese people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the cause, course, and consequence of the Korean War (1950-1953) (e.g., the role of Soviet Union and China, 38th parallel, consideration of atomic technology, and the human cost of the conflict), and argue its impacts on the Korean people
description_plain: Your child can explain the causes, events, and effects of the Korean War and how it affected Korean people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the alignment of independent and colonized governments all over the world with either the Soviet Union or United States using the North Atlantic Treaty Organization and the Warsaw Pact
description_plain: Your child can explain how countries chose sides during the Cold War by joining groups like NATO or the Warsaw Pact.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the definition of the Cold War and the term "superpower," and analyze the ideologies of the Cold War, the differences between capitalism and communism, and the arms race
