description_plain: Your child understands what the Cold War was, what a superpower is, and the differences between capitalism and communism.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the global impact of the Cold War and its growth out of the events of World War II.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the Cold War started after World War II and how it affected the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the consequences, and argue the impacts of World War II (e.g., the loss of Jewish life and community, military and civilian casualties, the Nuremberg Trials, the Yalta Conference, division of Germany and Austria into Soviet and allied sectors, and the "Iron Curtain")
description_plain: Your child can explain the effects of World War II, including losses, trials, and how countries were divided afterward.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the nature and course of the Holocaust (e.g., the history of antisemitism around the world, Nazi ideology and politics, Nuremberg Laws, Kristallnacht, ghettos, concentration camps, killing centers, Jewish and non-Jewish resistance, Warsaw Ghetto Uprising, other Nazi victims including LGBTQIA+ individuals, persons with disabilities, Roma, and political activists, and the final solution), and argue its impact on the people of Europe
description_plain: Your child can explain what happened during the Holocaust and how it affected Jewish people and other groups in Europe.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the course of World War II (e.g., the Stalin-Hitler Pact of 1939, differences between Eastern and Western Fronts warfare, use of technology and blitzkrieg warfare, role of North Africa and Western Asia (Middle East), the Pacific theater tactics and consequences, and dropping of the atomic bomb)
description_plain: Your child can explain the major events and battles of World War II and how new weapons and strategies were used.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes of World War II (e.g., the failure of the Weimar Republic, the rise of German Nazism and Adolph Hitler, installation of Italian Fascism and Benito Mussolini, a Militarist Japan's Sino-Japanese War with China, and the invasion of Poland by Germany)
description_plain: Your child can explain the causes of World War II, including the rise of dictators and invasions by countries like Germany and Japan.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the definitions of totalitarianism, communism, socialism, fascism, and holocaust, and analyze where and why these ideas gained popularity
description_plain: Your child understands what totalitarianism, communism, socialism, fascism, and the Holocaust mean and why these ideas became popular.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the global impacts of the cause, course, and consequences of World War II.
description_plain: Your child can explain how World War II started, what happened during the war, and its effects on the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how the Russian Revolution, including the rise of Joseph Stalin and the communist party, was a consequence of the War, and argue who benefited from these political changes
description_plain: Your child can explain how the Russian Revolution happened after World War I and who gained power from it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the consequences, and argue the impacts of World War I (e.g., military and civilian casualties, the peace negotiations at Versailles, France, Woodrow Wilson's "Fourteen Points" plan, the role of the League of Nations, the drawing of new nation-state borders and its impact on Western Asia (Middle East), the Balfour Declaration, and German reparations)
description_plain: Your child can explain the effects of World War I, including losses, peace talks, new countries, and how the war changed the Middle East.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the Armenian genocide from 1915-1916 and the role of the Near East Relief organization, and argue the impact on Armenian society
description_plain: Your child can explain what happened during the Armenian genocide and how it affected Armenian people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the course of the War including tactics on the Western versus Eastern fronts, role of technological advancements (e.g., machine guns, poison gas, aircrafts, and high explosives), and the blurring of the roles of soldier and civilian
description_plain: Your child can explain how World War I was fought, including new weapons and how soldiers and civilians were affected.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes of World War I including alliances between European powers, Russia, the Ottoman Empire and others, the political instability of the Balkans, and the reasons each country and state justified entry into the War
description_plain: Your child can explain the causes of World War I, including alliances and political problems in Europe.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the global impacts of the cause, course, and consequences of World War I.
description_plain: Your child can explain how World War I started, what happened during the war, and its effects worldwide.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the cause, course, and consequences of Mexican Revolution (1910-1920) as a response to the feudal system of la encomienda and the unequal social structure, and argue its impacts on Mexican society
description_plain: Your child can explain the Mexican Revolution, why it happened, what occurred, and how it changed Mexican society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the cause, course, and consequences of the Boxer Uprising (1899-1901) as a response against foreign influence in China, and argue its impacts on the Chinese people
description_plain: Your child can explain the Boxer Uprising in China, why people fought against foreign influence, and its effects on China.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the cause, course, and consequences of anti-colonial uprisings in Africa (1874-1931) (e.g., Anglo-Boer War in South Africa, Maji Maji revolt, Nyasaland uprising, and Herero Uprising), and argue their impacts on the peoples affected
description_plain: Your child can explain several African uprisings against colonial powers and how these fights changed the people involved.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the ways that global resistance to colonial powers led to societal and cultural change.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people around the world resisted colonial rule and how this led to changes in society and culture.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the cause, course, and consequence of American colonization of Caribbean and Pacific Islands, and argue who benefited from that action
description_plain: Your child can explain how the U.S. colonized Caribbean and Pacific Islands, what happened during that time, and who benefited.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the cause, course, and consequence of partitioning Africa by Belgian, British, French, German, Italian, Portuguese, and Spanish in 1884, and argue who benefited from that action
description_plain: Your child can explain why European countries divided Africa in 1884, describe what happened during that time, and discuss who gained the most from it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the cause, course, and consequence of the imperial model of colonization in India by the British (e.g., shift from East India Company rule to British crown rule or "raj"), and argue who benefited from that movement
description_plain: Your child can describe how British rule in India changed from company control to direct crown rule, explain what happened during this time, and discuss who benefited.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the definition and provide examples of imperialism, colonialism, and partitioning
description_plain: Your child understands what imperialism, colonialism, and partitioning mean and can give examples of each.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impacts of colonization by European powers on countries around the world in the late 19th and early 20th centuries.
description_plain: Your child can explain how European colonization affected different countries around the world in the late 1800s and early 1900s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the Opium Wars (1839-1860) and the Taiping Rebellion (1850-1864), and argue the impact on China's economy, politics, religions, and its population and who benefited
description_plain: Your child can explain what happened during the Opium Wars and Taiping Rebellion in China, how these events affected China's economy, government, religion, and people, and who gained from them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the influence and consequence of the East India Company on commerce, population centers, and culture in India and the shift to new colonial power centers (e.g., Calcutta and Bombay, and argue who benefited
description_plain: Your child can describe how the East India Company changed trade, cities, and culture in India and explain who benefited from these changes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze Egyptian reforms under Muhammad Ali after the French withdrawal in 1801 (e.g., reforms to the military, education, and agriculture), and argue the impact of those reforms
description_plain: Your child can explain the changes Muhammad Ali made in Egypt after 1801, like improvements in the army, schools, and farming, and discuss how these changes affected Egypt.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze advancements and consequences of the industrial revolution in the late 18th and early 19th century, and argue who benefited
description_plain: Your child can describe the important inventions and changes during the Industrial Revolution and explain who gained the most from them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the shift of African trade from the foreign slave trade to raw goods (e.g., palm oil and cloves) and the role of continued enslavement within Africa, and argue the impact of that trade
description_plain: Your child can explain how African trade shifted from selling slaves to trading raw materials and how slavery continued inside Africa, including the effects of these changes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impact of societal changes on global economies in the late 18th and early 19th centuries.
description_plain: Your child can explain how big changes in society affected world economies in the late 1700s and early 1800s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the 1780s Tupac Amaru Revolution of the Andean Indians and Mexican Independence in 1821, and argue the impact on Latin American nation building
description_plain: Your child can explain the Tupac Amaru Revolution and Mexican Independence, and describe how these events helped shape Latin American countries.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the similarities and differences in the causes, courses, and consequences of the American Revolution (1775-1783), the French Revolution (1789-1799), and the Haitian Revolution (1791-1803), and argue the impact on their respective societies
description_plain: Your child can compare the causes, events, and results of the American, French, and Haitian Revolutions and explain how each affected their societies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the global impact of worldwide revolutions during the late 18th to early 19th centuries.
description_plain: Your child can explain how revolutions around the world in the late 1700s and early 1800s had effects on many countries.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the technological advances of the time (e.g., the printing press, manufactured paper, microscope, telescope, thermometer, and barometer), and argue the impacts on society and culture and who benefited from those advances
description_plain: Your child can describe important inventions like the printing press and microscope, explain how they changed society and culture, and discuss who benefited.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the historical roots of the Scientific Revolution in Greco-Roman rationalism, Jewish, Christian, and Muslim science, and Renaissance humanism, analyze their influences on astronomy, mathematics, engineering, and human anatomy, and argue the impacts on society and culture
description_plain: Your child understands how ideas from ancient Greece and Rome, religious science, and Renaissance thinking influenced science and society during the Scientific Revolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify advances in art and architecture during the Renaissance, and argue the impacts on society and culture
description_plain: Your child can identify important art and architecture from the Renaissance and explain how they affected society and culture.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the ideas of the Enlightenment including concepts such the social contract, rule of law, citizenship, representation, liberty, equality, individual rights, and natural rights
description_plain: Your child understands key ideas from the Enlightenment like laws, rights, freedom, and citizenship.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the global impact of cultural and intellectual movements in the early modern period.
description_plain: Your child can explain how cultural and intellectual movements in the early modern period changed the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the reformation of neo-Confucian teachings and practices of Wang Yangming in China, and analyze its influence
description_plain: Your child can explain how Wang Yangming changed Confucian teachings in China and why it mattered.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the foundation of Sikhism by Guru Nanak in South Asia, and analyze its influence
description_plain: Your child can explain how Guru Nanak started Sikhism in South Asia and how it influenced people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the Protestant Reformation, counter-Reformation from the Catholic Church, and the Spanish Inquisition, and argue their impact on society
description_plain: Your child can explain the Protestant Reformation, the Catholic Church's response, and the Spanish Inquisition, and discuss how they affected society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the influences of religious transformations in the early modern period on society and culture.
description_plain: Your child can explain how changes in religion during the early modern period influenced society and culture.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain resistance efforts by enslaved peoples including everyday actions, rebellions, maintaining culture, and building new traditions
description_plain: Your child can explain how enslaved people resisted by everyday actions, rebellions, keeping their culture, and creating new traditions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the shift of enslavement from religious and culturally based to race based
description_plain: Your child can explain how slavery changed from being based on religion and culture to being based on race.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the development and expansion of the Atlantic slave trade and chattel slavery on European plantations in the Caribbean, North America, and South America and argue the impact on Indigenous and African peoples and who benefited from that impact
description_plain: Your child can describe how the Atlantic slave trade grew and affected Indigenous and African peoples, and explain who benefited.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the characteristics and conditions of pre-European African slave trade to North Africa, Egypt, the Red Sea, and Swahili coast
description_plain: Your child can explain what slavery was like in Africa before Europeans arrived, including where and how it happened.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the characteristics and conditions of enslavement in different times and places around the globe (e.g., ancient Greece, ancient Rome, Han China)
description_plain: Your child can describe what slavery was like in different ancient places like Greece, Rome, and China.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impact of the solidification of the Atlantic system of slavery on Indigenous and African peoples.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the Atlantic system of slavery affected Indigenous and African peoples.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the expansion of gunpowder empires (e.g., Mughal Empire in India, Safavid Persia, Ottoman Empire, and Ming China), and argue their influences on society and culture
description_plain: Your child can describe how empires using gunpowder weapons grew and explain how they influenced society and culture.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze broad patterns of trade across the world in 1400 including the importance of corn, silver, sugar, and other goods with closer looks at trade and exchanges in Majorca and Calicut, and argue their influences on society and culture
description_plain: Your child can explain important trade patterns around 1400, including goods like corn and silver, and how trade affected societies and cultures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the influence of worldwide trade patterns in the expansion of territories and on societies and cultures.
description_plain: Your child can explain how worldwide trade helped countries grow and changed societies and cultures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify explorers (e.g., Admiral Zheng He, Marco Polo, Ferdinand Magellan, Vasco de Gama, Sir Frances Drake, Christopher Columbus, Hernando Cortes, James Cook), analyze the rationale behind their voyages, and argue the impact they had on existing populations
description_plain: Your child can name famous explorers, explain why they traveled, and describe how their journeys affected the people they met.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the Columbian Exchange, and argue who benefited
description_plain: Your child can explain what the Columbian Exchange was and discuss who gained from it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the cause, course, figures, and responses to British colonization of Australia and New Zealand, and argue the impact on the Indigenous peoples of those lands
description_plain: Your child can explain how British colonization happened in Australia and New Zealand and describe its effects on Indigenous peoples.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the cause, course, figures, and responses to Spanish conquests of the Incan Empire, Aztec Empire, and the Taíno people, and argue the impact on the people they encountered
description_plain: Your child can explain how the Spanish conquered the Incan and Aztec Empires and the Taíno people, and describe the impact on those groups.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the cause, course, figures, and responses to expansion by the Portuguese, Spanish, English, and French along the coasts of Africa, into the Indian Ocean, and to the China coast, and argue the impact on the people they encountered
description_plain: Your child can explain how European countries expanded along Africa’s coasts, the Indian Ocean, and China, and describe how this affected local people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impact of European exploration and conquest across the globe on the people they encountered.
description_plain: Your child can explain how European exploration and conquest affected the people they met around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the five major world religions Hinduism, Judaism, Buddhism, Christianity, and Islam, and analyze their influences on society and culture in 1300 CE
description_plain: Your child can name the five major world religions and explain how they influenced society and culture around the year 1300.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify populations and political power centers of the world in 1300 CE, and explain the importance of the trade routes that connected those locations
description_plain: Your child can identify important populations and political centers in 1300 and explain why trade routes connecting them were important.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major geographical features around the world (e.g., climate zones, bodies of water, mountains, deserts), and analyze the influence of geography on where people settled worldwide
description_plain: Your child can identify major world geography like climates, oceans, mountains, and deserts, and explain how geography influenced where people lived.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the status of population and power centers around the world in 1300 CE.
description_plain: Your child can describe where people lived and where power was held around the world in the year 1300.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how different economic systems can create more equitable outcomes for individuals and communities, particularly for those traditionally marginalized from the economic system.
description_plain: Your child can explain how different ways of running economies can help make life fairer for everyone, especially for people who have been treated unfairly before.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how inequities within the economic system have been addressed or sustained by the actions of those traditionally privileged and marginalized.
description_plain: Your child can look at how unfairness in the economy has been kept or changed by people with more or less privilege.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how those traditionally privileged and marginalized across intersecting identities can influence and interact with economic systems.
description_plain: Your child understands how people with different backgrounds and identities can affect and be affected by the economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the ways that different political systems utilize economic systems to organize and distribute goods and services to individuals and communities.
description_plain: Your child can name how different governments use economic systems to share goods and services with people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue whether the costs and benefits of an aspect of the means of production equitably serve all individuals and communities.
description_plain: Your child can explain if the costs and benefits of how things are made are fair to all people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and communities acting through intersectional identities and lived experiences can affect the means of production.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people's identities and experiences affect how things are made and produced.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain who has the power to make decisions related to the means of production and the effects those decisions have on individuals and communities
description_plain: Your child understands who makes decisions about producing goods and services and how those decisions affect people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the individuals and communities involved in the production of any good or service, the materials needed for producing them, where and how the materials are obtained, and the various interrelationships among all of these elements.
description_plain: Your child can name the people and communities involved in making goods or services, the materials used, where they come from, and how all these parts connect.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how a resource can be used differently to create a more equitable outcome for individuals and communities including how individuals and communities can influence systems of power to achieve that change.
description_plain: Your child can explain how using a resource differently can help make things fairer for people and how people can work to change systems of power.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how decisions affecting access to goods and services are influenced by systems of power and cultural norms including how these effects of decisions create more equitable or inequitable outcomes.
description_plain: Your child can explain how decisions about who gets goods and services are influenced by power and culture, and how these decisions can be fair or unfair.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how scarcity affects the cost and availability of desired goods and services, and who has the power to influence the factors related to cost and availability and why.
description_plain: Your child understands how limited resources affect the price and availability of goods and services, and who controls these factors and why.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the choices communities make about how to use resources based on the scarcity of that resource, including those that are familiar and unfamiliar.
description_plain: Your child can explain how communities decide to use resources when they are limited, including resources they know well and those they don't.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the systematic analysis of the spatial patterns provides an integral understanding of a place or region and supports equitable decisions about climate and land use.
description_plain: Your child can explain how studying maps and spatial patterns helps us understand places and make fair choices about climate and land use.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple types of maps, charts, and graphs and how they are used to interpret topographical information, draw inferences about the development of societies, and determine how places shape events and how places may be changed by events.
description_plain: Your child can use different maps, charts, and graphs to understand land shapes, how societies grow, and how places and events affect each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the characteristics and elements of maps, globes, geographic tools, and other technologies are used and selected to identify and describe local, regional and/or global locations.
description_plain: Your child understands how to use maps, globes, and tools to find and describe places nearby or far away.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify maps, globes, and other geographic tools and technologies that are used to describe where places are located both absolutely and relatively across time, space, and distance.
description_plain: Your child can identify maps, globes, and tools that show where places are located in different ways and times.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how decisions about resources and the environment made by individuals and/or communities impact current and future peoples differently and how those decisions might be made more equitable.
description_plain: Your child can explain how choices about resources and the environment affect people now and in the future, and how to make those choices fairer.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and societies at local, regional and/or global levels influence political, economic, and social decision-making.
description_plain: Your child understands how people and societies at different levels influence political, economic, and social decisions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how humans and their societies and institutions affect, modify and/or preserve the environment, as well as how the modifications of the physical environment affect physical, behavioral, and diverse cultural systems.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people and their communities change or protect the environment, and how these changes affect cultures and behaviors.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics of human systems, physical systems, and the environment, and ways they interact at local, regional and/or global levels.
description_plain: Your child can name features of human and physical systems and the environment, and how they interact locally and globally.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the relationship between populations and physical systems influence decision-making about the equitable access to resources and land at the local, regional, and/or global levels.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the relationship between people and the environment affects fair access to resources and land in different places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how human systems and the distribution of populations interact with and impact physical systems, and how conflict and access to resources influence physical systems.
description_plain: Your child understands how people and where they live affect the environment, and how conflicts and resource access change physical systems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how and why a population's characteristics, including their spatial distribution, growth, and movement, have divided, organized, and unified areas of Earth's surface and impacted both human and physical systems.
description_plain: Your child can explain how population features like size, growth, and movement shape areas on Earth and affect both people and the environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics of populations based on their size, place, region, and cultural demographics, as well as identifying patterns of migration.
description_plain: Your child can identify population traits like size, location, culture, and migration patterns.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how all individuals can act as local, national, and/or global agents of social change by using lessons learned from history.
description_plain: Your child understands how anyone can work for social change locally, nationally, or globally by learning from history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze historical change through the intersectional identities and lived experiences of people who have accomplished social change throughout history in relation to systems of power, identity, and resistance.
description_plain: Your child can study how people with different identities and experiences have made social change in history, especially related to power and resistance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how historical and social change have been and continue to be accomplished in relation to systems of power, identity, and resistance.
description_plain: Your child understands how social and historical changes happen through power, identity, and resistance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify peoples, events, technologies, and ideas involved in historical and social change in various geographical and temporal locations.
description_plain: Your child can name important people, events, ideas, and inventions involved in social and historical change in different places and times.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue, using multiple narratives rooted in identity, power, and resistance, how history itself is an interpretation of events.
description_plain: Your child can explain how history is told in different ways based on identity, power, and resistance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple types of sources, including art, music, oral histories, pamphlets, film, texts, etc., through a critical reflection of the creators' and students' intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can look at different sources like art, music, and stories and think about how the creator's and their own identities affect how they understand history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the purpose, audience, and perspective of multiple types of sources (art, music, oral histories, pamphlets, film, texts, etc.) relating to a historical event or series of events, individual, or group of people, including indications of bias toward or against the subject portrayed.
description_plain: Your child understands the purpose, audience, and viewpoint of different sources about history, including recognizing bias.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify key people, central ideas, and the mechanisms by which stories are told and retold regarding an event or series of events, centering the voices of historical actors and groups engaged in resistance and change.
description_plain: Your child can identify key people and ideas in stories about events, focusing on voices of those who resisted and made change.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how social change, intersectional identities, and lived experiences are crucial to the study and practice of history.
description_plain: Your child understands why social change, identities, and experiences are important to studying and practicing history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple sources to compare and contrast historical events through the lenses of identity, power, and resistance.
description_plain: Your child can compare historical events by looking at identity, power, and resistance using different sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain multiple causes and effects of historical events, centering and representing the voices and experiences of individuals and communities who were agents of change and resistance.
description_plain: Your child can explain many causes and effects of historical events, focusing on people and groups who made change and resisted.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify historical events that are culturally relevant to global, national, and local histories and connect to students' intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can name historical events that connect to cultures and histories around the world and relate to their own identities and experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue for a possible solution to make rights equitable and the roles of those involved in pursuing that solution.
description_plain: Your child can explain a fair solution to make rights equal for everyone and describe who helps make that happen.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and communities have been included or excluded from the political process based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences and the impact these actions have had on their rights, responsibilities, and the functioning of a democratic society.
description_plain: Your child understands how some people or groups have been included or left out of politics because of who they are and how this affects their rights and duties.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain different ways communities and individuals inform themselves, exercise their rights and responsibilities, and engage formally and/or informally in political processes.
description_plain: Your child can explain different ways people learn about and take part in politics, both officially and in everyday life.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what rights and responsibilities individuals and communities have in a society and who can take advantage of them.
description_plain: Your child can name the rights and duties people and communities have and who can use them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how rules and laws can be used to create an equitable society.
description_plain: Your child can explain how rules and laws can help make society fair for everyone.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how rules and laws positively and/or negatively impact different individuals and communities based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child understands how rules and laws can help or hurt different people based on who they are and their experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why rules and laws exist, and how they are implemented by and for individuals and communities based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can explain why we have rules and laws and how they are made and used for different people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what rules and laws are, and who has the power to make them, in different settings and cultures that are familiar and unfamiliar to students.
description_plain: Your child can identify what rules and laws are and who makes them in different places and cultures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how power can be distributed and used to create a more equitable society for communities and individuals based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can explain how power can be shared and used to make society fairer for all people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the purpose of government and the use of power, including balancing order and freedom, to advance and control different communities and individuals based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child understands why governments exist and how they use power to keep order and freedom for different people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how political power is and has been obtained and used to govern communities and individuals with attention to their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people get and use political power to lead communities, considering who they are.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what political power is and who has political power in a society.
description_plain: Your child can explain what political power is and who holds it in society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify methods for evaluating the credibility of online and print sources
description_plain: Your child can find ways to tell if information from websites or books is trustworthy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the benefits and challenges of digital news and social media
description_plain: Your child can explain the good and bad things about news and social media online.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the function of news articles, news programs, websites, editorials, cartoons, advertisements, social media posts, and argue the impacts of those venues
description_plain: Your child can look at different types of news and media, like articles and social posts, and explain how they affect people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the role of the press, and analyze how that role has changed over time, and the responsibilities it has to the public
description_plain: Your child understands what the press does, how its role has changed over time, and its duties to the public.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the idea of freedom of the press, its purpose, and its origins
description_plain: Your child understands what freedom of the press means, why it exists, and where it started.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the benefits and challenges of media in a democracy.
description_plain: Your child can explain the good and bad effects of media in a democracy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze current global issues (e.g., climate change, disease, food insecurity), explain ways people as global citizens can be involved (e.g., volunteering, advocacy, educating others), and argue the potential impacts
description_plain: Your child can talk about current world problems like climate change and how people can help by volunteering or teaching others.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze human rights violations and genocides (e.g., Armenian genocide, Jewish Holocaust, Cambodian genocide, Rwandan genocide, human trafficking, child labor, modern slavery) and the ways nations and organizations intervene
description_plain: Your child can explain serious human rights abuses and how countries and groups try to stop them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the United Nations and other organizations (e.g., Human Rights Watch, Anti-Slavery International, Save the Children, Amnesty International) secure human rights across the globe, analyze their approaches, and argue their impacts globally
description_plain: Your child understands how groups like the United Nations work to protect human rights and can explain their effects worldwide.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain what human rights are, analyze the elements of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, and argue their impact globally
description_plain: Your child understands what human rights are, what the Universal Declaration of Human Rights says, and how it affects people around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impacts individuals and groups have made in securing human rights globally.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people and groups have helped protect human rights globally.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways students can contribute to efforts for equality
description_plain: Your child can describe ways students can help support equality.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify civil rights issues that continue to exist today, analyze current debates surrounding these issues, and argue their position on one or more of the issues
description_plain: Your child can identify current civil rights issues, understand different opinions about them, and explain their own views.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the cooperation between movements (e.g., school segregation legal challenges, Delano Grape Strike 1965, Section 504 Sit-in 1977), and argue the impacts of working together
description_plain: Your child can explain how different movements worked together and why that teamwork was important.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the strategies and outcomes of the African American, Latinx, Asian American and Pacific Islander, Indigenous, Women, LGBTQIA+, Immigrants, and Disability rights movements (e.g., root inequities, legal challenges, social movements, role of community leaders, efforts of ordinary people, any legal changes as a result of the movements, Rhode Island connections), and argue the impacts of the outcomes to different groups of people
description_plain: Your child can describe the goals and results of various civil rights movements and explain how these changes affected different groups.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the concept of equal rights
description_plain: Your child understands what equal rights mean.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impacts individuals and groups have made towards securing civil rights in the United States.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people and groups have helped secure civil rights in the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain career opportunities in public service at local, state, and federal levels
description_plain: Your child can name different public service jobs at local, state, and national levels.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze issues surrounding elections (e.g., redistricting, voter identification laws, campaign financing, campaign ads, voter turnout) and how they reflect voters' positions
description_plain: Your child can explain election issues like voting rules and campaign ads and how they show what voters think.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the methods of participation in elections (e.g., running for office, campaigning, lobbying, demonstrating, volunteering at polling places, voting, filing legal challenges)
description_plain: Your child understands different ways people can take part in elections, like voting, campaigning, or volunteering.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the role of primaries and the Electoral College
description_plain: Your child can explain what primary elections and the Electoral College do.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the election process in Rhode Island, how that process differs in other states, and argue the ways that those different processes affect individuals (e.g., requirements for individuals, procedures, rules, regulations)
description_plain: Your child can compare how elections work in Rhode Island and other states and explain how these differences affect people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how to register to vote, find a polling place, and access voter information in Rhode Island
description_plain: Your child knows how to register to vote, find where to vote, and get voter information in Rhode Island.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the political process, how elections work, issues surrounding elections, and the ways people can get involved.
description_plain: Your child can explain how elections work, what problems they face, and how people can get involved.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways that every individual can actively participate in their local communities
description_plain: Your child can describe ways anyone can help in their local community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the roles and expectations of all individuals in a democracy (e.g., paying taxes, obeying laws, military service, public service, voting, serving on a jury)
description_plain: Your child understands what people are expected to do in a democracy, like paying taxes and voting.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the importance of the responsibilities and obligations of a citizen and how people can participate in their communities.
description_plain: Your child can explain why citizens have responsibilities and how they can take part in their communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue ways that government actions help and hinder individual groups' rights
description_plain: Your child can explain how government actions can both help and hurt the rights of different groups.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the freedoms for all individuals existing in civic, political, and private life despite legal citizenship (e.g., labor rights, children's rights, cultural freedoms, religious freedoms, rights to subsistence, education, health care), and argue the impacts across different populations
description_plain: Your child can explain freedoms people have in daily life, like religious or labor rights, and how these affect different groups.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the difference between rights protected by the Constitution of the United States and other laws for all individuals living in the United States despite legal status (e.g., equity, tolerance, due process, freedom of speech, religion, and privacy) and those rights that apply only to legal citizens (e.g., voting, running for and holding elected office), and argue the impacts across different populations
description_plain: Your child can explain the difference between rights everyone has in the U.S. and rights only legal citizens have, like voting.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze ways that individuals live in the United States as participatory citizens but not as legal citizens
description_plain: Your child understands how some people live as active citizens even if they are not legal citizens.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the history of when groups or individuals legally became citizens
description_plain: Your child knows the history of when different groups or people became legal citizens.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the process for individuals to become legal citizens, and analyze how that is applied differently to groups
description_plain: Your child understands how people become legal citizens and how this process has been different for some groups.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the differences between the term "citizen" as it pertains to being an active and responsible participant in society as opposed to being a legal citizen as an individual with full rights under a particular system of government
description_plain: Your child understands the difference between being a citizen who participates in society and being a legal citizen with full rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the importance of the rights citizens hold in a democracy and what it means to protect those rights.
description_plain: Your child can explain why citizens' rights are important in a democracy and why we need to protect them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the impact of the doctrine of incorporation on states
description_plain: Your child can explain how the idea of incorporating rights has affected states.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the effects of major court interpretations addressing freedom of religion, assembly, press, petition, and speech under the first Amendment; the due process clause and the equal protection clause of the fourteenth Amendment; and cases where individual rights conflicted with community or national beliefs
description_plain: Your child can explain important court decisions about freedoms like religion and speech and how these affect people's rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the process of judicial review established in Marbury v. Madison – how cases come before the Supreme Court, how they are argued, how the Court issues decisions and dissents
description_plain: Your child understands how the Supreme Court reviews cases, hears arguments, and makes decisions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze court decisions and interpretations that affected freedoms and citizen rights.
description_plain: Your child can explain court decisions that have changed freedoms and citizen rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the implications of the "necessary and proper" clause to the Constitution
description_plain: Your child can explain how the 'necessary and proper' clause lets the government make laws that help carry out its duties.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze laws or executive orders that challenged civil rights by limiting individual rights (e.g., 1942 Executive order 9066, 1953 Executive order 10450), and argue their impacts
description_plain: Your child can look at laws or orders that limited people's rights and explain how they affected civil rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the laws or executive orders expanding civil rights and equal protection for race, religion, gender, sexuality, and disability that demonstrate the evolving protections to civil rights (e.g., 1964 Civil Rights Act, 1965 Voting Rights Act, 1972 Title IX, 1972 Equal Employment Act, 1990 American with Disabilities Act, 2014 Executive Order 13672, 2022 Executive Order 14076), and argue their impacts
description_plain: Your child can study laws and orders that expanded rights for different groups and explain how these changes helped protect people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze significant changes to the Constitution (e.g., 14th Amendment, 19th Amendment, 26th Amendment), and argue the impacts of those changes
description_plain: Your child can describe important changes to the Constitution and explain how those changes affected the country.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the purpose, procedure, and limits of executive orders
description_plain: Your child can explain what executive orders are, how they are made, and what limits they have.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify how congressional laws are passed
description_plain: Your child can explain the steps Congress takes to pass laws.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the historical significance of Federal laws enacted by Congress and the Executive branch to protect, expand, or limit individual rights.
description_plain: Your child can explain why laws made by Congress and the President are important for protecting or limiting people's rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the development and implementation of public policy at each level of government
description_plain: Your child can explain how public policies are created and put into action at local, state, and national levels.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the process and goals for addressing issues with overlapping jurisdiction (e.g., health care, transportation, education, housing) including between local governments, Rhode Island state government, and tribal governments
description_plain: Your child can explain how different governments work together to solve shared problems like health care and education.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the relationships between the Narragansett tribal and local and state governments and their powers, and argue the impacts of those relationships
description_plain: Your child can explain how the Narragansett tribe works with local and state governments and how these relationships affect people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the relationships between different levels of government and their powers, and argue the impacts and who benefits
description_plain: Your child can explain how different levels of government share power and who benefits from these relationships.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the similarities and differences among reserved, expressed, implied, and concurrent powers
description_plain: Your child can explain the different types of government powers and how they are used.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze what happens when jurisdictions overlap with opposing views and goals.
description_plain: Your child can explain what happens when different governments have conflicting goals or rules.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the systems, laws, and policies of tribal governments in and local to Rhode Island (e.g., constitutions, infrastructure, leadership, constituent's rights and responsibilities, land use, economies), and argue the impacts on their constituents
description_plain: Your child can explain how tribal governments in Rhode Island work and how their laws and policies affect their people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the tribal governments local to Rhode Island including their cultural practices, values, and experiences with colonization (e.g., Narragansett in RI, Mashantucket Pequot and Mohegan in CT, and Wampanoag Tribe of Gay Head and Mashpee Wampanoag in MA)
description_plain: Your child can describe the culture, values, and history of local tribal governments, including their experiences with colonization.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the diversity of tribal governments located in North America
description_plain: Your child can explain how tribal governments across North America are different from each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the elements of sovereignty and how federal and state recognition of tribes is handled, and argue the impacts to Indigenous groups that are and are not state or federally recognized
description_plain: Your child can explain what sovereignty means for tribes and how being recognized by the state or federal government affects Indigenous groups.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impact of the systems, practices, and values of tribal governments.
description_plain: Your child can explain how tribal governments' systems and values impact their communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways people can create change through Rhode Island government (e.g., vote, petition, protest, write a letter to a representative) and how the state Constitution can be amended
description_plain: Your child can explain how people can make changes in Rhode Island government, like voting or petitioning, and how the state Constitution can be changed.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze important state issues (e.g., infrastructure, education, regulation of business, civil and criminal laws, land use) and governmental response, and argue how responses have impacted the issues
description_plain: Your child can explain important state issues and how the government has responded, including how those responses affected the problems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how state government is funded
description_plain: Your child can explain where Rhode Island gets money to run its government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the structure, organization, function, and responsibilities of Rhode Island's judiciary
description_plain: Your child can explain how Rhode Island's court system is organized and what it does.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the structure, organization, function, and responsibilities of Rhode Island's executive branch
description_plain: Your child can explain how Rhode Island's executive branch is organized and what its responsibilities are.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the structure, organization, function, and responsibilities of Rhode Island's General Assembly
description_plain: Your child can explain how Rhode Island's General Assembly is organized and what it does.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the history of the Rhode Island Constitution (e.g., drafting, signing, amendments), and analyze the influence of the earlier Charter on the creation of the state constitution
description_plain: Your child can explain the history of Rhode Island's Constitution and how earlier documents influenced it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the current and historic impact of the Rhode Island state government.
description_plain: Your child can explain how Rhode Island's government has affected the state both now and in the past.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways people can create change in their local government (e.g., vote, petition, voice opinions at a hearing)
description_plain: Your child can explain how people can make changes in their local government by voting, petitioning, or speaking up.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze important local issues (e.g., public schools, public services, housing, land use, business regulation) and governmental response, and argue how local responses have impacted these issues
description_plain: Your child can explain important local issues and how the local government has responded, including the effects of those responses.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the treatment of different groups of people in the local city or town (e.g., the unhoused, senior citizens, unemployed) and the related resources offered, and argue how treatment and resources have impacted those groups
description_plain: Your child can explain how different groups in their town are treated and what resources are available to them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the function, funding, leadership, and administration of local city or town government
description_plain: Your child can explain how their local city or town government works, including how it is funded and led.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the current and historic impacts of the city or town government(s) local to students.
description_plain: Your child can explain how their local city or town government has affected their community now and in the past.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the Constitution's dual purpose – to enumerate power and to limit the abuse of power
description_plain: Your child can explain that the Constitution both gives the government power and limits how that power can be used.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the three branches of government provide checks on one another, and analyze how effective those checks are
description_plain: Your child can explain how the three branches of government check each other and how well those checks work.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the ways the branches of government provide checks on one another to limit the abuse of power.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the branches of government keep each other from having too much power.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze current cases before the Supreme Court, and argue how their decisions could affect students
description_plain: Your child can look at current Supreme Court cases and explain how their decisions might affect students.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how the Supreme Court has interpreted the Bill of Rights and Constitutional Amendments over time (e.g., due process of law, free speech, equal protection, protection against unreasonable search and seizure), and argue how their decisions impact society
description_plain: Your child can explain how the Supreme Court has interpreted rights over time and how those decisions affect society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the scope of the Supreme Court and important rulings, past and present
description_plain: Your child can explain what the Supreme Court does and some important decisions it has made.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the structure of the judiciary (e.g., eligibility, length of service, selection, and confirmation process)
description_plain: Your child can explain how judges are chosen for the courts and how long they serve.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the importance of the role of the Supreme Court and its influence on issues of today.
description_plain: Your child can explain why the Supreme Court is important and how it influences current issues.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the role and responsibilities of the Vice President
description_plain: Your child can explain what the Vice President does and what their responsibilities are.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the role of the electoral college in presidential elections, and argue its impact in recent elections
description_plain: Your child can explain how the electoral college works in choosing the president and share opinions about its effects in recent elections.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the process of presidential elections, and analyze campaigns and how they have changed over time
description_plain: Your child understands how presidential elections happen and can describe how campaigns have changed over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the scope and limits of the presidency (both foreign and domestic) has changed over time
description_plain: Your child can explain how the president’s powers inside and outside the country have grown or changed over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the structure of the presidency (e.g., how elected, requirements for office, specific powers, removal process)
description_plain: Your child understands how the president is chosen, what the job requires, the powers the president has, and how a president can be removed.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the structure of the executive branch (e.g., cabinet, departments)
description_plain: Your child can describe the parts of the executive branch, like the president’s cabinet and government departments.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impact of Article II of the Constitution of the United States and how the power of the presidency has changed over time.
description_plain: Your child can explain how Article II of the Constitution gives power to the president and how that power has changed over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze ways that individual citizens can participate in the legislative process
description_plain: Your child understands different ways regular people can take part in making laws.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the relationships between legislators and professional lobbyists, and analyze the positive and negative effects that lobbying has on the legislative process (e.g., corporations, unions, nonprofit organizations, private citizens)
description_plain: Your child can explain how lawmakers and lobbyists work together and can talk about good and bad effects of lobbying.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the role of political parties and how they influence Congressional legislation
description_plain: Your child understands the role of political parties and how they affect laws made by Congress.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the importance and impact of recent issues brought before Congress (e.g., gun ownership, abortion, LGBTQIA+ rights, immigration)
description_plain: Your child can talk about important recent issues in Congress like gun laws, abortion, LGBTQIA+ rights, and immigration.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify current legislative leaders for Rhode Island and analyze their positions/platforms
description_plain: Your child can name current Rhode Island lawmakers and explain their views and goals.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the process for electing members to Congress
description_plain: Your child understands how people are elected to Congress.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the structural components of Congress and its two houses (e.g., mechanics, specific powers, eligibility, and length of terms of members, how laws are passed)
description_plain: Your child can describe how Congress is set up, including its two parts, how members are chosen, how long they serve, and how laws get passed.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the relationship between enumerated and implied powers
description_plain: Your child understands the difference between powers clearly listed in the Constitution and powers that are suggested or implied.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impact of Article I of the Constitution of the United States and the work of Congress with a focus on the critical issues that Congress is debating today.
description_plain: Your child can explain how Article I of the Constitution shapes Congress and talk about important issues Congress is working on today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the current challenges to and uses of the Bill of Rights (e.g., gun ownership, right to protest, right to online privacy, prayer in schools)
description_plain: Your child can discuss current challenges and uses of the Bill of Rights, like gun ownership, protests, online privacy, and prayer in schools.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the amendments in the Bill of Rights, and analyze why they were added to the Constitution
description_plain: Your child understands the amendments in the Bill of Rights and why they were added to the Constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the Federalist Papers and the Anti-Federalist responses and what they tell us about the new Constitution (e.g., federalism, factions, checks and balances, independent judiciary, republicanism, limited government) (Suggested Federalist Papers – 9, 10, 39, 51, 70, 78, 84)
description_plain: Your child can explain what the Federalist Papers and Anti-Federalist responses tell us about the Constitution’s ideas like checks and balances and limited government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the views of the leaders of the Constitutional Convention on critical issues (e.g., slavery, rights of individuals, distribution of political power, representation, rights of states) and how these issues were resolved in the proposed Constitution
description_plain: Your child understands the views of leaders at the Constitutional Convention on big issues like slavery and state power, and how they solved those problems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the conflicts that lead to compromises during the 1787 Constitutional Convention (e.g., The Virginia Plan, The New Jersey Plan, the Connecticut Compromise, Shays' Rebellion, Northwest Ordinance 1787, The Great Compromise, Rhode Island's initial resistance to ratifying the Constitution of the United States) and argue the impact of those compromises
description_plain: Your child can explain the conflicts and compromises made during the 1787 Constitutional Convention and why those compromises mattered.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the events of the Constitutional Convention and the issues raised during the debates.
description_plain: Your child understands what happened at the Constitutional Convention and the main issues people debated.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify ways that students see the ideals of the Declaration of Independence represented in their lives
description_plain: Your child can find examples of the Declaration of Independence’s ideas in their own life.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the ways that the United States has lived up to and fallen short of the ideals in the Declaration of Independence, and argue ways that individuals were left out of the ideas of liberty
description_plain: Your child can talk about how the U.S. has met or not met the ideals in the Declaration of Independence and who was left out of those ideas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze events that were immediate influenced by the Declaration of Independence (e.g., French Revolution, Haitian Revolution, Grievance 27, continuation of slavery)
description_plain: Your child understands events that happened right after the Declaration of Independence, like the French and Haitian Revolutions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the backgrounds (race, gender, occupation, religion, age, location, and view of slavery) of the Declaration of Independence signatories, and argue the effect that their perspectives had on their political views
description_plain: Your child can describe the backgrounds of the Declaration’s signers and explain how their views affected their politics.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the grievances and ideals laid out in the Declaration of Independence
description_plain: Your child understands the complaints and ideas written in the Declaration of Independence.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the definition of liberty according to the Declaration of Independence
description_plain: Your child can explain what liberty means according to the Declaration of Independence.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue whether the United States has lived up to the ideals written in the Declaration of Independence by considering the authors, signers, and their ideals.
description_plain: Your child can discuss whether the U.S. has lived up to the ideals in the Declaration by looking at the people who wrote and signed it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the necessity of separation of powers in the American political system
description_plain: Your child can explain why it’s important to have separate powers in the American government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify important documents and perspectives during this period including but not limited to the Haudenosaunee Confederacy, the Constitution of the United States, the Magna Carta, the Mayflower Compact, and argue the ways they influenced the construction of a rule of law in United States government
description_plain: Your child can name important documents like the Magna Carta and Mayflower Compact and explain how they helped shape U.S. laws.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze ways other forms of representative governments influenced the American system (e.g., Ancient Athens, Roman Republic, 18th century Britain, Indigenous governments)
description_plain: Your child understands how governments from ancient Athens, Rome, Britain, and Indigenous groups influenced America’s government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the purpose of government and the concepts of rule of law as defined by other governments (e.g., Ancient Athens, Roman Republic, 18th century Britain, Indigenous communities)
description_plain: Your child can explain why governments exist and what rule of law means, using examples from history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain different types of governments (e.g., democracy, oligarchy, monarchy)
description_plain: Your child can explain different types of governments like democracy, monarchy, and oligarchy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue the impact of influences from ancient Athens, the Roman Republic, 18th century Britain, and Indigenous communities in constructing laws.
description_plain: Your child can explain how ideas from ancient Athens, Rome, Britain, and Indigenous communities helped create American laws.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways students can participate in their communities today, analyze ways that young people have made contemporary contributions, and argue their impacts
description_plain: Your child understands how students can take part in their communities today and can talk about how young people have made a difference.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze American founding documents' treatment of a citizen's responsibilities, who had access to citizenship, and who was denied access to civic participation
description_plain: Your child can explain what founding American documents say about citizens’ duties, who could be a citizen, and who was excluded.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the similarities and differences among Athenians', Romans', members of the Haudenosaunee Confederacy, and British citizens' views on civic participation, civic duty/common good, citizenship and a citizen's responsibilities, and government organization, and argue the impacts on their societies
description_plain: Your child can compare how Athenians, Romans, Indigenous peoples, and British citizens thought about being involved in government and how that affected their societies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue what it meant to be civically engaged in the past and identify ways to participate today.
description_plain: Your child can explain what it meant to be involved in government in the past and how people can participate today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze discussions of natural rights today
description_plain: Your child can talk about ideas of natural rights and how they are discussed today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the efficacy of varying perspectives from figures during this period (e.g., John Locke, Jean Jacques Rousseau, James Otis, Phillis Wheatley, Mary Wollstonecraft)
description_plain: Your child can explain different views from important thinkers of the past and how effective those ideas were.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze enlightenment thinker views on equality, enslavement, women's rights, education, habeas corpus, and protection from government
description_plain: Your child can explain what Enlightenment thinkers believed about equality, slavery, women's rights, education, legal protections, and government power.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the similarities and differences among Athenians', Romans', and members of the Haudenosaunee Confederacy's views of the rights of individuals
description_plain: Your child can compare what Athenians, Romans, and the Haudenosaunee Confederacy thought about individual rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the relationship between parts of the Declaration of Independence, Bill of Rights, the Constitution of the United States, and natural rights
description_plain: Your child can explain how the Declaration of Independence, Bill of Rights, and Constitution relate to natural rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the difference between natural rights v. legal rights
description_plain: Your child understands the difference between natural rights people have by birth and legal rights given by laws.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the idea of natural rights and its roots to ancient Athens, the Roman Republic, Enlightenment thinkers, and Indigenous peoples.
description_plain: Your child can explain where the idea of natural rights comes from, including ancient Athens, Rome, Enlightenment thinkers, and Indigenous peoples.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how different economic systems can create more equitable outcomes for individuals and communities, particularly for those traditionally marginalized from the economic system.
description_plain: Your child can explain how different ways of running economies can help make life fairer for everyone, especially for people who have been treated unfairly before.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how inequities within the economic system have been addressed or sustained by the actions of those traditionally privileged and marginalized.
description_plain: Your child can look at how unfairness in the economy has been kept or changed by people with more or less privilege.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how those traditionally privileged and marginalized across intersecting identities can influence and interact with economic systems.
description_plain: Your child understands how people with different backgrounds and identities can affect and be affected by the economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the ways that different political systems utilize economic systems to organize and distribute goods and services to individuals and communities.
description_plain: Your child can name how different governments use economic systems to share goods and services with people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue whether the costs and benefits of an aspect of the means of production equitably serve all individuals and communities.
description_plain: Your child can explain if the costs and benefits of how things are made are fair to all people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and communities acting through intersectional identities and lived experiences can affect the means of production.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people's identities and experiences affect how things are made and produced.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain who has the power to make decisions related to the means of production and the effects those decisions have on individuals and communities
description_plain: Your child understands who makes decisions about producing goods and services and how those decisions affect people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the individuals and communities involved in the production of any good or service, the materials needed for producing them, where and how the materials are obtained, and the various interrelationships among all of these elements.
description_plain: Your child can name the people and communities involved in making goods or services, the materials used, where they come from, and how all these parts connect.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how a resource can be used differently to create a more equitable outcome for individuals and communities including how individuals and communities can influence systems of power to achieve that change.
description_plain: Your child can explain how using a resource differently can help make things fairer for people and how people can work to change systems of power.

subject: Social Studies
