description: Analyze how decisions affecting access to goods and services are influenced by systems of power and cultural norms including how these effects of decisions create more equitable or inequitable outcomes.
description_plain: Your child can explain how decisions about who gets goods and services are influenced by power and culture, and how these decisions can be fair or unfair.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how scarcity affects the cost and availability of desired goods and services, and who has the power to influence the factors related to cost and availability and why.
description_plain: Your child understands how limited resources affect the price and availability of goods and services, and who controls these factors and why.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the choices communities make about how to use resources based on the scarcity of that resource, including those that are familiar and unfamiliar.
description_plain: Your child can explain how communities decide to use resources when they are limited, including resources they know well and those they don't.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the systematic analysis of the spatial patterns provides an integral understanding of a place or region and supports equitable decisions about climate and land use.
description_plain: Your child can explain how studying maps and spatial patterns helps us understand places and make fair choices about climate and land use.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple types of maps, charts, and graphs and how they are used to interpret topographical information, draw inferences about the development of societies, and determine how places shape events and how places may be changed by events.
description_plain: Your child can use different maps, charts, and graphs to understand land shapes, how societies grow, and how places and events affect each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the characteristics and elements of maps, globes, geographic tools, and other technologies are used and selected to identify and describe local, regional and/or global locations.
description_plain: Your child understands how to use maps, globes, and tools to find and describe places nearby or far away.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify maps, globes, and other geographic tools and technologies that are used to describe where places are located both absolutely and relatively across time, space, and distance.
description_plain: Your child can identify maps, globes, and tools that show where places are located in different ways and times.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how decisions about resources and the environment made by individuals and/or communities impact current and future peoples differently and how those decisions might be made more equitable.
description_plain: Your child can explain how choices about resources and the environment affect people now and in the future, and how to make those choices fairer.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and societies at local, regional and/or global levels influence political, economic, and social decision-making.
description_plain: Your child understands how people and societies at different levels influence political, economic, and social decisions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how humans and their societies and institutions affect, modify and/or preserve the environment, as well as how the modifications of the physical environment affect physical, behavioral, and diverse cultural systems.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people and their communities change or protect the environment, and how these changes affect cultures and behaviors.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics of human systems, physical systems, and the environment, and ways they interact at local, regional and/or global levels.
description_plain: Your child can name features of human and physical systems and the environment, and how they interact locally and globally.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how the relationship between populations and physical systems influence decision-making about the equitable access to resources and land at the local, regional, and/or global levels.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the relationship between people and the environment affects fair access to resources and land in different places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how human systems and the distribution of populations interact with and impact physical systems, and how conflict and access to resources influence physical systems.
description_plain: Your child understands how people and where they live affect the environment, and how conflicts and resource access change physical systems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how and why a population's characteristics, including their spatial distribution, growth, and movement, have divided, organized, and unified areas of Earth's surface and impacted both human and physical systems.
description_plain: Your child can explain how population features like size, growth, and movement shape areas on Earth and affect both people and the environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics of populations based on their size, place, region, and cultural demographics, as well as identifying patterns of migration.
description_plain: Your child can identify population traits like size, location, culture, and migration patterns.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how all individuals can act as local, national, and/or global agents of social change by using lessons learned from history.
description_plain: Your child understands how anyone can work for social change locally, nationally, or globally by learning from history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze historical change through the intersectional identities and lived experiences of people who have accomplished social change throughout history in relation to systems of power, identity, and resistance.
description_plain: Your child can study how people with different identities and experiences have made social change in history, especially related to power and resistance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how historical and social change have been and continue to be accomplished in relation to systems of power, identity, and resistance.
description_plain: Your child understands how social and historical changes happen through power, identity, and resistance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify peoples, events, technologies, and ideas involved in historical and social change in various geographical and temporal locations.
description_plain: Your child can name important people, events, ideas, and inventions involved in social and historical change in different places and times.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue, using multiple narratives rooted in identity, power, and resistance, how history itself is an interpretation of events.
description_plain: Your child can explain how history is told in different ways based on identity, power, and resistance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple types of sources, including art, music, oral histories, pamphlets, film, texts, etc., through a critical reflection of the creators' and students' intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can look at different sources like art, music, and stories and think about how the creator's and their own identities affect how they understand history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the purpose, audience, and perspective of multiple types of sources (art, music, oral histories, pamphlets, film, texts, etc.) relating to a historical event or series of events, individual, or group of people, including indications of bias toward or against the subject portrayed.
description_plain: Your child understands the purpose, audience, and viewpoint of different sources about history, including recognizing bias.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify key people, central ideas, and the mechanisms by which stories are told and retold regarding an event or series of events, centering the voices of historical actors and groups engaged in resistance and change.
description_plain: Your child can identify key people and ideas in stories about events, focusing on voices of those who resisted and made change.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how social change, intersectional identities, and lived experiences are crucial to the study and practice of history.
description_plain: Your child understands why social change, identities, and experiences are important to studying and practicing history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple sources to compare and contrast historical events through the lenses of identity, power, and resistance.
description_plain: Your child can compare historical events by looking at identity, power, and resistance using different sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain multiple causes and effects of historical events, centering and representing the voices and experiences of individuals and communities who were agents of change and resistance.
description_plain: Your child can explain many causes and effects of historical events, focusing on people and groups who made change and resisted.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify historical events that are culturally relevant to global, national, and local histories and connect to students' intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can name historical events that connect to cultures and histories around the world and relate to their own identities and experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue for a possible solution to make rights equitable and the roles of those involved in pursuing that solution.
description_plain: Your child can explain a fair solution to make rights equal for everyone and describe who helps make that happen.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how individuals and communities have been included or excluded from the political process based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences and the impact these actions have had on their rights, responsibilities, and the functioning of a democratic society.
description_plain: Your child understands how some people or groups have been included or left out of politics because of who they are and how this affects their rights and duties.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain different ways communities and individuals inform themselves, exercise their rights and responsibilities, and engage formally and/or informally in political processes.
description_plain: Your child can explain different ways people learn about and take part in politics, both officially and in everyday life.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what rights and responsibilities individuals and communities have in a society and who can take advantage of them.
description_plain: Your child can name the rights and duties people and communities have and who can use them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how rules and laws can be used to create an equitable society.
description_plain: Your child can explain how rules and laws can help make society fair for everyone.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how rules and laws positively and/or negatively impact different individuals and communities based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child understands how rules and laws can help or hurt different people based on who they are and their experiences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why rules and laws exist, and how they are implemented by and for individuals and communities based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can explain why we have rules and laws and how they are made and used for different people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify what rules and laws are, and who has the power to make them, in different settings and cultures that are familiar and unfamiliar to students.
description_plain: Your child can identify what rules and laws are and who makes them in different places and cultures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Argue how power can be distributed and used to create a more equitable society for communities and individuals based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child can explain how power can be shared and used to make society fairer for all people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the purpose of government and the use of power, including balancing order and freedom, to advance and control different communities and individuals based on their intersectional identities and lived experiences.
description_plain: Your child understands why governments exist and how they use power to keep order and freedom for different people.

subject: Social Studies
